profile.domain: Grid-Edge IoT Firmware DevOps

project_goal.value: Research prototype for containerized OTA firmware update pipeline targeting substation relay controllers with staged rollout and automatic rollback safety guarantees
project_goal.rationale: Core R&D objective to evaluate DevOps tooling for OT/IT convergence

target_users.value: Grid operations R&D engineers evaluating deployment automation for substation OT environments
target_users.rationale: Primary user group identified during prototype scoping

project_class.rationale: Exploratory system validating feasibility of container-based OTA in OT environments

capabilities.rationale: No UI required; focus on pipeline automation and relay controller integration

complexity_level.rationale: Staged rollout orchestration and OT protocol integration introduce significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Firmware updates to substation relay controllers can cause grid instability if rollback fails

data_sensitivity.rationale: Relay controller firmware and substation topology data are operationally sensitive under NERC CIP

external_exposure.rationale: Prototype accessible only on the isolated OT research lab network segment

access_model.value: Custom JWT with role-based access: firmware-engineer, deployment-operator, read-only-observer
access_model.rationale: Minimal viable access control for prototype multi-role R&D team

feature_scope.value: Firmware package registry, staged rollout orchestration across relay controller clusters, health-check gating between deployment waves, rollback snapshot management, incident logging
feature_scope.rationale: Full prototype feature set for evaluation

mvp_scope.value: Single-cluster staged rollout with two-wave deployment, automated rollback on health-check failure, firmware package upload and signing verification
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum scope to validate core OTA pipeline safety

security_baseline.value: Firmware packages signed with Ed25519 keys; TLS 1.3 between orchestrator and relay agents; secrets in AWS Secrets Manager; no public internet exposure
security_baseline.rationale: OT security baseline per IEC 62443 for prototype environment

frontend_stack.value: None - backend and API only prototype
frontend_stack.rationale: R&D team interacts via REST API and CLI scripts; no UI in scope

backend_stack.rationale: Team proficiency in Python for OT tooling; FastAPI for rapid API development

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with Redis 7 for deployment job state
data_platform.rationale: Relational model fits firmware package and deployment job relationships; Redis for real-time job queue

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate within isolated VPC, no public subnets
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with network isolation for OT research lab environment

privacy_retention_policy.value: Deployment logs retained 2 years for incident post-mortem; firmware packages retained indefinitely with version archival
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Operational records retention for grid equipment maintenance compliance

future_scope.value: Multi-substation orchestration, integration with SCADA change management workflows, hardware-in-the-loop test bench automation
future_scope.rationale: Post-prototype production roadmap items

constraints.value: Must operate on air-gapped-adjacent lab network with no internet egress; firmware update window restricted to 01:00-04:00 local time
constraints.rationale: OT operational constraints for substation testing environment

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for deployment pipeline metrics; structured JSON logs to CloudWatch; PagerDuty alert for rollback events
observability_baseline.rationale: R&D team monitoring standard

execution_preference.value: 2-week research sprints with weekly demo to grid ops stakeholders
execution_preference.rationale: Agile cadence suited to exploratory prototype work

compliance_context.value: NERC CIP-007 patch management requirements; IEC 62443 OT security zones and conduits for research environment
compliance_context.rationale: High risk level and confidential OT data require CIP compliance framing even for prototype

project.goal: Validate containerized OTA firmware update pipeline for substation relay controllers with rollback safety
project.target_users: Grid R&D engineers

backend.framework: FastAPI with Celery for async job orchestration

security.auth: Custom JWT with Ed25519 firmware signing

data.cache: Redis 7 for Celery job broker and deployment state

open_questions.relay_protocol: IEC 61850 GOOSE vs proprietary serial - confirm with hardware team

reasoner_reviews.product: Prototype scope is tightly bounded around the staged-rollout safety mechanism, which is the right R&D focus. Firmware package registry and rollback snapshots are essential supporting features. Limiting to two deployment waves for MVP is pragmatic given the restricted update window constraint.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI with Celery on ECS Fargate is well-suited for job orchestration. The deployment job state machine should be persisted in PostgreSQL rather than only Redis to survive container restarts during a live rollout. Health-check gating logic needs formal pass/fail thresholds defined upfront to avoid ambiguous rollback decisions mid-window.
reasoner_reviews.security: IEC 62443 zone model requires that the ECS orchestrator and relay controller agents communicate over a dedicated VPC subnet with security group rules permitting only port 8883. Ed25519 signature verification must happen on the relay agent side, not just server-side, to prevent firmware injection.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 01:00-04:00 update window must be enforced by the scheduler via a Redis distributed lock, not just by convention. Air-gap constraints mean all container images must be pre-pulled into ECR before the update window opens.

backend.service_design: Two FastAPI services: firmware-registry (package upload, Ed25519 verification, version catalog) and deployment-orchestrator (job creation, wave scheduling, health-check polling, rollback trigger). Celery workers process deployment tasks asynchronously with PostgreSQL-backed result store.
backend.api_patterns: REST API at /api/v1: POST /firmware-packages (upload+verify), GET /firmware-packages/:id, POST /deployment-jobs (create staged rollout), GET /deployment-jobs/:id/waves (wave status), POST /deployment-jobs/:id/abort (manual rollback trigger)
backend.business_modules: DeploymentStateMachine with states: scheduled, wave_1_in_progress, wave_1_health_check, wave_2_in_progress, wave_2_health_check, completed, rolling_back, rolled_back, failed. Transitions guarded by health-check results.
backend.failure_handling: On health-check timeout or FAIL response from any controller in a wave, state machine transitions to rolling_back, issues firmware revert commands to all affected controllers, and writes incident record to rollback_snapshots table.

security.auth_design: JWT issued by auth service with claims: sub (engineer ID), role (firmware-engineer/deployment-operator/observer), exp (4-hour validity). All API routes require valid JWT. Deployment-write routes restricted to firmware-engineer and deployment-operator roles.
security.secrets_management: Ed25519 private signing keys stored in AWS Secrets Manager, never on disk. Relay agent public keys provisioned at controller manufacture time and stored in relay_controllers.public_key column.
security.security_risks: Relay controller authentication relies on pre-provisioned public keys; a compromised controller could accept firmware from a rogue orchestrator if network segmentation fails. Security group rules restrict orchestrator-to-controller communication to the dedicated lab VLAN only.

data.entities: firmware_packages (id, version_semver, sha256_hash, ed25519_signature, uploaded_by, uploaded_at, status ENUM draft/verified/deprecated), relay_controllers (id, substation_id, model, firmware_version_current, public_key, last_health_check_at, status ENUM active/updating/fault), deployment_jobs (id, firmware_package_id, wave_count, update_window_start, update_window_end, status ENUM, created_by, created_at), deployment_waves (id, job_id, wave_number, controller_ids JSONB, status ENUM pending/in_progress/passed/failed, health_check_result JSONB, started_at, completed_at), rollback_snapshots (id, controller_id, firmware_version_pre_update, snapshot_taken_at, restored_at, restoration_trigger), substation_nodes (id, name, grid_zone, location_coords, active_controllers_count)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ for deployment record durability. Redis 7 for Celery broker and ephemeral wave-progress counters with 24h TTL.
data.data_risks: Deployment job records contain controller IDs and firmware versions mapping to physical substation equipment. Row-level access controls restrict deployment-operator role to jobs they created.

devops.deployment_topology: Two ECS Fargate services in private subnets: firmware-registry and deployment-orchestrator. Celery workers as separate ECS task definition scaling 1-4 tasks based on SQS queue depth. RDS PostgreSQL and ElastiCache Redis in isolated data subnet. Internal NLB only.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint (ruff), type-check (mypy), unit tests (pytest 85% coverage gate), docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling update. lab-staging requires manual approval gate.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics: deployment_jobs_total, wave_health_check_pass_rate, rollback_events_total, firmware_upload_latency_seconds. Grafana dashboard: deployment pipeline state overview. CloudWatch for container logs.
devops.devops_risks: Celery worker scale-down during active deployment wave could orphan in-progress jobs. ECS scale-in protection enabled on Celery task definitions during active deployment windows.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a research prototype for OTA firmware updates to substation relay controllers. Key decisions: FastAPI with Celery for async deployment orchestration, PostgreSQL-backed state machine for job persistence across container restarts, Ed25519 signature verification on both orchestrator and relay agent sides, Redis distributed lock to enforce the 01:00-04:00 update window constraint. No frontend needed; R&D team interacts via REST API.
target_output.title: Grid-Edge Relay Controller OTA Firmware Update Pipeline
target_output.executive_summary: A research prototype validating containerized over-the-air firmware update pipelines for substation relay controllers. Built with Python 3.12/FastAPI on AWS ECS Fargate within an isolated OT research lab VPC. Implements staged two-wave rollout with PostgreSQL-persisted state machines, automated health-check gating, and atomic rollback to last-known-good firmware snapshots. Targets NERC CIP-007 patch management practices in an OT/IT convergence R&D context.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-service architecture on ECS Fargate in private VPC subnets: firmware-registry handles package upload with Ed25519 signature verification, and deployment-orchestrator manages the staged rollout state machine via Celery workers. The orchestrator polls relay controller agents over TLS 1.3 for health-check responses after each deployment wave, transitioning job state from wave_in_progress to wave_health_check. PostgreSQL 16 on RDS persists all job and wave records for post-mortem durability. Redis 7 backs the Celery broker and holds distributed locks enforcing the 01:00-04:00 update window restriction. No public internet exposure; all traffic routed through internal NLB within the OT research VPC.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, Celery 5.x, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ, Redis 7 on ElastiCache. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ECR, internal NLB, AWS Secrets Manager for signing keys. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with ruff, mypy strict, pytest 85% gate. Observability: Prometheus client, Grafana dashboards, CloudWatch Logs structured JSON.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Firmware Package Registry: upload binary packages, verify Ed25519 signatures, assign semver, mark status draft/verified/deprecated. Deployment Job Orchestration: create staged rollout jobs targeting relay controller clusters, schedule within update window, execute wave-by-wave with configurable controller counts per wave. Health-Check Gating: after each wave poll all participating controllers for firmware version confirmation and operational heartbeat; gate next wave on 100% PASS. Rollback Engine: on health-check failure issue revert commands referencing rollback_snapshots, write incident record. Incident Log: structured post-mortem records for every rollback event.
target_output.system_components: firmware-registry service (FastAPI, validates Ed25519 signatures, catalogs firmware_packages), deployment-orchestrator service (FastAPI state machine API, Celery workers for async wave execution), PostgreSQL 16 RDS (deployment_jobs, deployment_waves, rollback_snapshots, relay_controllers, firmware_packages, substation_nodes), Redis 7 ElastiCache (Celery broker, update-window distributed lock, wave-progress counters), relay controller agents (embedded Python process receiving firmware and responding to health-check polls), AWS Secrets Manager (Ed25519 signing keys), internal NLB.
target_output.workflows: Staged Firmware Rollout: R&D engineer POSTs to /api/v1/deployment-jobs with firmware_package_id and target controller cluster. Orchestrator validates update window via Redis lock, creates deployment_waves records, queues Celery tasks. Wave 1 pushes firmware to 20% of controllers, waits for agent acknowledgment, polls health-check endpoint. All controllers must return firmware_version=target and status=operational within 10-minute timeout. On 100% PASS wave 2 begins with remaining 80%. Automated Rollback: If any controller returns FAIL or times out, Celery task transitions job.status to rolling_back, issues firmware-revert commands referencing rollback_snapshots.firmware_version_pre_update for each affected controller, polls for revert confirmation, creates incident record, fires PagerDuty P1 alert.
target_output.data_model: firmware_packages (id UUID PK, version_semver VARCHAR, sha256_hash CHAR(64), ed25519_signature BYTEA, uploaded_by, uploaded_at, status ENUM draft/verified/deprecated). relay_controllers (id UUID PK, substation_id FK, model, firmware_version_current, public_key BYTEA, last_health_check_at, status ENUM active/updating/fault). deployment_jobs (id UUID PK, firmware_package_id FK, wave_count INT, update_window_start, update_window_end, status ENUM scheduled/wave_1_in_progress/wave_1_health_check/wave_2_in_progress/completed/rolling_back/rolled_back/failed, created_by, created_at). deployment_waves (id UUID PK, job_id FK, wave_number INT, controller_ids JSONB, status ENUM pending/in_progress/passed/failed, health_check_result JSONB, started_at, completed_at). rollback_snapshots (id UUID PK, controller_id FK, firmware_version_pre_update, snapshot_taken_at, restored_at, restoration_trigger). substation_nodes (id UUID PK, name, grid_zone, location_coords, active_controllers_count INT).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/firmware-packages - multipart upload with Ed25519 verification, returns package_id. GET /api/v1/firmware-packages/:id - package metadata and status. POST /api/v1/deployment-jobs - create rollout specifying firmware_package_id and controller_cluster_id. GET /api/v1/deployment-jobs/:id - full job status with wave progress. GET /api/v1/deployment-jobs/:id/waves - per-wave health-check results. POST /api/v1/deployment-jobs/:id/abort - trigger immediate rollback. GET /api/v1/relay-controllers - catalog with current firmware versions and health status. All endpoints require JWT Bearer; deployment-write routes require firmware-engineer or deployment-operator role claim.
target_output.security_and_compliance: NERC CIP-007 patch management: all firmware packages tracked with SHA-256 hash, Ed25519 signature, and deployment audit trail in deployment_jobs. IEC 62443 zone model: orchestrator and relay agents on separate VPC subnets with security group permitting only TCP 8883. Ed25519 private signing keys in AWS Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation. JWT tokens expire after 4 hours. Deployment jobs require explicit operator approval before wave 2 executes. All API access logged with operator ID and action to CloudWatch for CIP-007 audit trail.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster with two task definitions: firmware-registry (1 task, 1 vCPU/2 GB) and deployment-orchestrator (1 API task + 1-4 Celery workers, 0.5 vCPU/1 GB each). RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ db.t4g.medium. ElastiCache Redis cluster.t4g.small. GitHub Actions pipeline: ruff lint, mypy strict, pytest 85% gate, docker build, ECR push, ECS force-new-deployment. lab-staging requires manual approval. Container images pre-pulled into ECR before each update window. ECS scale-in protection enabled on Celery workers during active deployment_job states. No auto-scaling of orchestrator during active rollout to prevent state machine disruption.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics via prometheus-fastapi-instrumentator: deployment_jobs_created_total, deployment_jobs_completed_total (labeled by status), wave_health_check_pass_rate (gauge per job), rollback_events_total (labeled by trigger reason), firmware_upload_duration_seconds histogram. Grafana dashboard: active deployment state, wave progress bar, rollback rate over last 30 days. CloudWatch Logs: structured JSON with job_id, wave_number, controller_id correlation. PagerDuty P1 alert on any rollback event within update window.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated -450/month for prototype lab: RDS t4g.medium ~, ElastiCache t4g.small ~, ECS Fargate 3 tasks average ~, ECR storage ~, CloudWatch Logs ~, networking ~. Prototype not designed for multi-substation scale; production version would require Celery worker horizontal scaling and RDS read replica for wave-status polling.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Sprint 1 (2 weeks): firmware-registry service with Ed25519 verification, PostgreSQL schema, Docker test harness simulating relay agents, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 Sprint 2 (2 weeks): deployment-orchestrator state machine, wave execution via Celery, health-check polling, rollback engine, Redis update-window lock. Phase 3 Sprint 3 (2 weeks): integration testing against physical relay controller test bench, Prometheus metrics, Grafana dashboard, incident log API, stakeholder demo with full rollback scenario.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with ruff and mypy strict enforced in CI. SQLAlchemy 2.0 async for all DB operations. All Celery tasks must be idempotent - wave tasks check current wave.status before executing to handle retries. Ed25519 signature verification logic has 100% unit test coverage given safety-critical nature. No direct SQL - all queries through SQLAlchemy ORM with explicit transaction boundaries.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: relay controller agent crash during wave leaves deployment_job in ambiguous state. Mitigation: 10-minute health-check timeout triggers automatic rollback regardless of agent state. Risk: PostgreSQL RDS failover during active wave could cause Celery worker to lose connection mid-state-transition. Mitigation: idempotent wave tasks re-check state on reconnect. Tradeoff: fixed 20%/80% wave split is simple but may be too coarse for production; future versions should support configurable canary percentages. Risk: Ed25519 key compromise allows malicious firmware signing. Mitigation: Secrets Manager access restricted to ECS task role only with quarterly rotation.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose REST polling for health-checks over webhook callbacks from relay agents - simpler given prototype agent constraints. Ed25519 selected over RSA-2048 for firmware signing due to smaller signature size fitting relay controller memory. PostgreSQL chosen over InfluxDB since deployment event volume is low and relational join queries on wave-to-controller mappings are required.

metadata.notes: OT/IT convergence prototype for substation relay OTA updates with NERC CIP-007 framing and automated rollback.

profile.domain: Multi-Utility Grid Data Interchange API Gateway

project_goal.value: Kubernetes-based unified API gateway enabling regional transmission operators to programmatically exchange real-time grid state data and capacity reservation requests, replacing point-to-point EDI integrations
project_goal.rationale: Core business need to modernize inter-utility data exchange infrastructure

target_users.value: Regional transmission operators and utility participants requiring programmatic grid state and capacity reservation APIs
target_users.rationale: B2B partner API users identified during EDI replacement scoping

project_class.rationale: Platform-level API gateway infrastructure, not an application

capabilities.rationale: API gateway with partner auth, grid system integrations, and DevOps automation

complexity_level.rationale: Sub-200ms capacity reservation latency at partner scale with real-time grid state synchronization

risk_level.rationale: Real-time grid operational data; unauthorized capacity reservations could cause grid imbalance

data_sensitivity.rationale: Grid state snapshots and capacity reservation data classified as Critical Energy Infrastructure Information under NERC CIP-011

external_exposure.rationale: API is B2B only; access restricted to registered utility participants with mTLS client certificates

access_model.value: mTLS client certificates for partner authentication; Azure AD B2C service principals for authorization scopes; API keys as secondary credential for read-only participants
access_model.rationale: Partner-facing B2B API requires hardware-bound authentication per NERC CIP-011

feature_scope.value: Capacity reservation API with real-time curtailment validation, grid state snapshot streaming, nightly interchange schedule reconciliation, settlement file generation, participant management portal, audit log API
feature_scope.rationale: Full inter-utility interchange platform feature set

mvp_scope.value: Capacity reservation POST endpoint with sub-200ms curtailment validation, grid state snapshot GET, participant onboarding with mTLS certificate provisioning
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for first utility participant pilot

security_baseline.value: mTLS for all partner API connections; Azure AD B2C service principals; TLS 1.3 internal; secrets in Azure Key Vault; WAF on NGINX Ingress; RBAC on AKS namespaces
security_baseline.rationale: NERC CIP-011 requires strong authentication and CEII data protection

frontend_stack.value: None - infrastructure API gateway with no UI layer
frontend_stack.rationale: B2B API consumed programmatically by utility SCADA and EMS systems

backend_stack.rationale: Go performance characteristics suit sub-200ms latency requirement; Gin for low-overhead HTTP routing

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server with Redis 7 for grid state caching
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for capacity reservations and participants; Redis for sub-millisecond grid state read path

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) with Azure DevOps Pipelines
hosting_target.rationale: AKS for Kubernetes-native infrastructure with Azure AD integration for partner auth

privacy_retention_policy.value: Grid state snapshots retained 7 years per FERC Order 2222 recordkeeping; API audit logs retained 3 years; participant API keys rotated annually
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: FERC and NERC regulatory retention requirements

future_scope.value: Real-time WebSocket stream for grid state push notifications, automated FERC compliance reporting, expansion to 50+ utility participants
future_scope.rationale: Post-pilot growth roadmap

constraints.value: Capacity reservation response must be under 200ms p99; must support 30 concurrent utility participants at launch; Azure region lock to eastus2 for CEII data residency
constraints.rationale: Operational SLA and regulatory data residency constraints

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with distributed tracing across Gin services; Kubernetes metrics via kube-state-metrics to Datadog; PagerDuty alerts for SLA breaches
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard for production infrastructure

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure-as-code with Terraform for AKS and Azure resources; GitOps with ArgoCD for application deployments
execution_preference.rationale: Platform team workflow standard for Kubernetes infrastructure

compliance_context.value: NERC CIP-011 CEII handling; FERC Order 2222 recordkeeping; SOC 2 Type II for utility participant trust; mTLS mandatory for all external connections
compliance_context.rationale: High risk level with partner-facing confidential grid data requires multi-framework compliance

project.goal: Unified API gateway for multi-utility grid state and capacity reservation interchange
project.target_users: Regional transmission operators

backend.framework: Gin with custom middleware for mTLS validation

security.auth: mTLS client certificates + Azure AD B2C service principals
security.ceii: Azure Key Vault for CEII data encryption keys

data.cache: Redis 7 for grid state snapshot caching

reasoner_reviews.product: The capacity reservation SLA of sub-200ms is achievable with Redis caching of grid state snapshots, avoiding a hot PostgreSQL read path. The nightly reconciliation batch job is well-separated from the real-time API path. Participant onboarding complexity is underestimated - mTLS certificate provisioning requires an automated PKI workflow, not manual certificate issuance.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go/Gin is the right choice for sub-200ms p99. The grid state snapshot should be pre-validated and cached in Redis keyed by region and timestamp, with a 30-second TTL matching typical SCADA polling intervals. Capacity reservation validation logic must read from the Redis cache, not PostgreSQL, to meet the latency SLA. PostgreSQL is only written post-validation.
reasoner_reviews.security: NERC CIP-011 requires that CEII data be encrypted at rest with utility-controlled keys. Azure Key Vault with customer-managed keys (CMK) satisfies this. mTLS mutual certificate validation must occur at the NGINX Ingress level, not just application-level - prevents unauthenticated traffic from reaching Gin services at all.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: eastus2 Azure region satisfies CEII data residency. AKS node pool autoscaling from 3-10 nodes on CPU 60% target handles participant burst. Redis Cache for Azure with 6 GB tier supports 30 concurrent participants reading grid state snapshots without eviction pressure.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The first-round plan did not include a participant certificate rotation workflow, leaving the mTLS trust chain management undefined. Without an automated certificate renewal process, expired certificates will silently break participant integrations. This must be resolved before pilot.

backend.service_design: Three Go services: capacity-reservation-api (handles POST /reservations with curtailment validation against Redis grid state), grid-state-ingestor (ingests real-time grid state from SCADA feeds, writes to PostgreSQL and Redis), settlement-reconciler (nightly batch reading interchange_schedules to produce settlement files per utility participant).
backend.api_patterns: REST with OpenAPI 3.1. POST /v1/reservations (submit capacity request, validate curtailment, return booking confirmation or rejection within 200ms), GET /v1/grid-state/:region (current snapshot from Redis cache), GET /v1/reservations/:id (booking status), GET /v1/interchange-schedules?date=YYYY-MM-DD (accepted schedules for settlement).
backend.scaling_notes: capacity-reservation-api scales horizontally to 10 pods; HPA on request latency p95 > 150ms. grid-state-ingestor runs as 2 replicas with leader election for SCADA feed deduplication. settlement-reconciler runs as single CronJob at 23:30 UTC with PodDisruptionBudget.
backend.backend_risks: Redis cache miss on grid state snapshot during SCADA feed gap would force fallback to PostgreSQL, potentially breaching 200ms SLA. Mitigation: stale-while-revalidate pattern with 5-minute grace TTL and circuit breaker returning last-good snapshot.

security.auth_design: NGINX Ingress with lua-resty-mtls validates partner client certificates against Azure Key Vault-hosted CA chain on every request. Azure AD B2C service principal tokens encode participant_id and authorization scopes (reservation:write, grid_state:read). Gin middleware validates both mTLS fingerprint and JWT scope on each route.
security.authorization_model: Participant scopes: reservation:write (transmission operators only), grid_state:read (all registered participants), settlement:read (own participant data only). Scope claims in Azure AD B2C tokens enforced by Gin middleware, not NGINX.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every API request logged to audit_logs table with participant_id, endpoint, request_body_hash, response_status, latency_ms, timestamp. Audit logs shipped to Azure Immutable Blob Storage for NERC CIP-011 tamper-evident retention. Datadog custom metric: reservation_unauthorized_attempts_total.
security.security_risks: mTLS certificate expiry for a participant will cause 401 failures. Mitigation: automated certificate expiry monitoring with 30-day and 7-day Datadog alerts; documented self-service renewal via participant portal.

data.entities: utility_participants (id UUID PK, participant_code VARCHAR, name, mtls_cert_fingerprint, azure_ad_principal_id, status ENUM active/suspended/onboarding, onboarded_at), capacity_reservations (id UUID PK, participant_id FK, region_code, capacity_mw DECIMAL, effective_from TIMESTAMPTZ, effective_to TIMESTAMPTZ, curtailment_headroom_mw DECIMAL, status ENUM confirmed/rejected/cancelled, validated_at), grid_state_snapshots (id UUID PK, region_code, snapshot_at TIMESTAMPTZ, available_capacity_mw DECIMAL, load_mw DECIMAL, generation_mw DECIMAL, congestion_zones JSONB, source_feed), interchange_schedules (id UUID PK, participant_id FK, schedule_date DATE, hour_block INT, scheduled_mw DECIMAL, accepted_at TIMESTAMPTZ), api_keys (id UUID PK, participant_id FK, key_hash CHAR(64), expires_at, last_used_at), audit_logs (id UUID PK, participant_id FK, endpoint, request_body_hash, response_status, latency_ms, timestamp)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server for transactional records. Redis 7 caches current grid_state_snapshots per region_code with 30-second TTL. Azure Immutable Blob Storage for audit log archival. Azure Key Vault CMK for PostgreSQL column-level encryption of capacity_mw and grid state values.
data.analytics_events: Daily settlement reconciliation reads all confirmed capacity_reservations and interchange_schedules to generate per-participant settlement files in CSV format distributed via Azure Blob SAS URLs.
data.data_risks: Grid state snapshot CEII classification requires column-level encryption for capacity and generation values. Participant query isolation must prevent cross-participant data leakage in interchange_schedules reads.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster in eastus2 with 3 system node pools and 2 user node pools (standard tier D4s_v5). NGINX Ingress Controller with mTLS termination. ArgoCD for GitOps deployment from main branch. Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (Business Critical, 4 vCores). Azure Cache for Redis 6 GB. Azure Key Vault for CMK and partner CA certificates.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipelines: go vet, golangci-lint, go test (90% coverage gate), docker build with distroless base, ACR push, ArgoCD sync trigger. Staging deployment requires security team approval. Production deployment locked to maintenance window with change ticket.
devops.rollback_strategy: ArgoCD rollback to previous Helm chart revision on failed health probe. PostgreSQL point-in-time recovery to 5-minute granularity. Redis snapshot for grid state recovery if cache corruption detected.
devops.devops_risks: AKS node pool upgrade disruption during active trading hours could delay capacity reservation responses. Mitigation: maintenance windows scheduled 02:00-04:00 UTC with PodDisruptionBudget ensuring minimum 2 capacity-reservation-api pods remain available.

ISS-001.title: mTLS certificate lifecycle management undefined
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described mTLS for partner authentication but did not specify how certificates are issued, rotated, or renewed for utility participants. Expired certificates will silently break participant integrations without a managed PKI workflow.

history.note: Identified by critic reviewer - no certificate lifecycle section in security plan

ISS-002.title: Grid state snapshot cache miss fallback path missing
ISS-002.detail: The 200ms SLA depends on Redis cache hits for grid state. No fallback strategy was defined for cache miss during SCADA feed gaps, risking SLA breaches under fault conditions.

history.note: Identified by architect_reasoner - cache miss path not addressed

focus_issues.title: mTLS certificate lifecycle management undefined
focus_issues.detail: No certificate issuance or renewal workflow defined; expired certs will break participant integrations

focus_issues.title: Grid state snapshot cache miss fallback path missing
