target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 revision targeting ISS-002 (step lock timeout) and ISS-003 (CIM import idempotency). Adding explicit 10-minute Redis TTL with SET NX EX 600 for step_lock atomic acquisition and PagerDuty alert on auto-release. Adding ON CONFLICT (cim_mrid) DO UPDATE to CIM import pipeline with import_batch_id for rollback. ISS-001 (Istio mTLS) was resolved in round 2 and carried forward. All three issues now resolved - plan should converge in this round.

fix_report.action_taken: StepLockManager now uses Redis SET NX EX 600 (10-minute TTL) for atomic lock acquisition. Added lock_timeout Prometheus counter. PagerDuty alert fires when step_lock_timeout_total increments, notifying dispatcher that a field crew step lock expired and the step is unlocked. switching_steps.status transitions back to pending on auto-release.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Step locks automatically release after 10 minutes with dispatcher notification, preventing permanently locked isolation points from blocking switching order completion

fix_report.action_taken: CIM XML import pipeline now uses PostgreSQL ON CONFLICT (cim_mrid) DO UPDATE SET ... for topology_nodes and isolation_points, making re-imports safe and idempotent. Each import assigned a UUID import_batch_id. Added DELETE /topology/cim-import/:batch_id rollback endpoint that removes all topology_nodes and isolation_points with matching import_batch_id using a database transaction. Added import idempotency_key validation to reject duplicate concurrent imports.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Corrected CIM XML files can be safely re-imported without duplicating topology nodes; bad imports can be atomically rolled back by batch_id

target_output.title: Distribution Grid Switching Order Management Platform v3
target_output.executive_summary: Vue 3 + FastAPI web application for control room dispatchers and field crews to manage switching orders on medium-voltage distribution networks. Deployed on GKE Autopilot with Istio mTLS service mesh and Keycloak OIDC authentication. Features real-time SVG single-line diagram with WebSocket topology updates, switching order approval workflow with step-level field crew execution tracking, and TopologyRuleEngine pre-validation. Round 3 resolves all three tracked issues: Istio mTLS (ISS-001 round 2), step lock 10-minute timeout (ISS-002), and CIM import idempotency (ISS-003).
target_output.architecture_overview: Three FastAPI services on GKE Autopilot with Istio sidecar mTLS STRICT: topology-api (CIM import, device state, WebSocket broadcast to Vue clients via Redis pub/sub), switching-order-api (order CRUD, SwitchingOrderStateMachine, StepLockManager with 10-minute Redis TTL, TopologyRuleEngine), and integration-api (GIS/SCADA ingest). Vue 3 SPA connects to topology-api WebSocket for live single-line diagram updates, patching Pinia topology store on device_state_changed events. Keycloak OIDC with 15-minute JWT and silent refresh governs all route access. PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL HA persists switching orders, topology, and audit trail. Redis Memorystore backs step locks (SET NX EX 600) and topology pub/sub channel.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Vue 3, TypeScript, Pinia, Vue Router 4, Vue Query, keycloak-js, D3.js v7 (SVG single-line diagram rendering), Vitest. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, alembic, pandapower (CIM XML parsing), redis-py, websockets. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot, Istio 1.21 (mTLS STRICT), Keycloak 24, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16, Memorystore Redis 6 GB, Cloud Load Balancing with IAP. CI/CD: GitLab CI, ArgoCD, Helm, Artifact Registry. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana, Google Cloud Logging, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Single-Line Diagram Viewer: SVG topology rendering with isolation_points color-coded by device state (closed/open/unknown) and network_segments highlighted during active switching orders. Switching Order Lifecycle: dispatcher creates order with step sequence, TopologyRuleEngine validates sequence against connectivity graph and returns topology_rule_violations list, dispatcher approves, field crews execute step-by-step with StepLockManager preventing concurrent operations on the same isolation_point. Step Execution: field crew acquires lock via PATCH /steps/:id/execute, completes action, uploads optional photo evidence, marks complete. CIM Network Model Import: POST multipart CIM XML triggers pandapower parse, idempotent upsert on cim_mrid, import_batch_id assigned for rollback capability. Audit Trail: all topology-modifying actions written to audit_logs with keycloak_sub, display_name, changed_fields JSONB.
target_output.system_components: topology-api pod (FastAPI, pandapower CIM parser, WebSocket manager broadcasting to Redis topology_updates channel, 2-6 pods HPA on WebSocket connection count), switching-order-api pod (FastAPI, SwitchingOrderStateMachine, StepLockManager with Redis SET NX EX 600, TopologyRuleEngine with materialized view query, 2-4 pods), integration-api pod (FastAPI, SCADA/GIS ingest handlers, 1-2 pods), Istio sidecar proxies (mTLS STRICT between all pods), PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL HA (switching_orders, switching_steps, isolation_points, network_segments, topology_nodes, outage_tickets, crew_assignments, audit_logs), Redis Memorystore 6 GB (step_lock entries with 600s TTL, topology_updates pub/sub), Keycloak 24 (LDAP-federated, GKE Deployment), Cloud Load Balancing with IAP (VPN enforcement).
target_output.workflows: Switching Order Creation and Approval: dispatcher creates switching order with title, region_code, planned window, and ordered list of steps (each specifying isolation_point_id and action open/close/tag). switching-order-api calls TopologyRuleEngine which queries topology_rule_violations materialized view to verify the step sequence does not create an unintended island or violate N-1 connectivity. If violations exist, order returns to draft with violation list. Dispatcher resolves and resubmits. On clean validation, order status transitions to pending_approval; second dispatcher approves (PATCH .../approve), status transitions to approved. Step Execution with Lock Management: field crew opens Step Execution Form for their assigned step. Frontend POSTs PATCH /switching-orders/:id/steps/:step_id/execute. StepLockManager calls Redis SET NX EX 600 keyed by isolation_point_id. If lock acquired, step.status transitions to locked; crew performs physical switching action, confirms in UI, optionally uploads photo. PATCH .../complete transitions step.status to completed, Redis lock deleted. If crew does not complete within 10 minutes, Redis TTL expires, step.status reverts to pending, Prometheus step_lock_timeout_total increments, PagerDuty alert fires to dispatcher. CIM XML Import: operator uploads CIM XML via POST /topology/cim-import. pandapower parses CIM elements. PostgreSQL transaction executes INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (cim_mrid) DO UPDATE for topology_nodes and isolation_points. import_batch_id UUID assigned to all inserted/updated records. On parse failure, transaction rolls back atomically. DELETE /topology/cim-import/:batch_id removes all records with matching import_batch_id.
target_output.data_model: switching_orders (id UUID PK, title, region_code, outage_ticket_id FK, created_by keycloak_sub, approved_by keycloak_sub, status ENUM draft/pending_approval/approved/in_execution/completed/cancelled, planned_start TIMESTAMPTZ, planned_end TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at). switching_steps (id UUID PK, order_id FK, step_number INT, isolation_point_id FK, action ENUM open/close/tag/remove_tag, assigned_crew_id FK, status ENUM pending/locked/completed/failed, completed_by keycloak_sub, completed_at, photo_evidence_url). isolation_points (id UUID PK, cim_mrid VARCHAR UNIQUE, name, network_segment_id FK, device_type ENUM breaker/recloser/switch/fuse, current_state ENUM open/closed/unknown, last_state_changed_at, import_batch_id UUID). network_segments (id UUID PK, cim_mrid UNIQUE, name, voltage_kv DECIMAL, upstream_node_id FK, downstream_nodes JSONB, segment_type ENUM feeder/lateral/tie, import_batch_id UUID). topology_nodes (id UUID PK, cim_mrid UNIQUE, substation_id, bus_voltage_kv DECIMAL, connected_segments JSONB, import_batch_id UUID). outage_tickets (id UUID PK, order_id FK, affected_customer_count INT, cause, status ENUM open/in_progress/restored/closed, opened_at, closed_at). crew_assignments (id UUID PK, switching_order_id FK, crew_leader keycloak_sub, crew_members JSONB, assigned_step_ids JSONB). audit_logs (id UUID PK, keycloak_sub VARCHAR, display_name VARCHAR, action ENUM, resource_type, resource_id, changed_fields JSONB, ip_address, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: POST /api/v1/switching-orders - create with steps array, triggers TopologyRuleEngine validation. PATCH /api/v1/switching-orders/:id/approve - dispatcher role required, transitions to approved. PATCH /api/v1/switching-orders/:id/steps/:step_id/execute - field-crew role, acquires Redis step lock. PATCH /api/v1/switching-orders/:id/steps/:step_id/complete - releases lock, sets completed_by. GET /api/v1/topology/network-model - returns current topology graph. POST /api/v1/topology/cim-import - multipart CIM XML, returns {import_batch_id, import_summary: {nodes_upserted, isolation_points_upserted, violations}}. DELETE /api/v1/topology/cim-import/:batch_id - rollback import batch. WebSocket ws:/topology-api/ws/topology-updates - broadcasts {event: device_state_changed, isolation_point_id, new_state, changed_at}. All routes require Keycloak JWT; role-specific routes enforce Keycloak realm role claims via FastAPI dependency.
target_output.security_and_compliance: NERC CIP-002 BES asset identification: all switching order actions and topology modifications recorded in audit_logs with keycloak_sub, display_name, and changed_fields JSONB. Shipped to Google Cloud Storage immutable bucket for 10-year retention. Istio mTLS STRICT on all three service pods prevents lateral movement within GKE cluster. Keycloak OIDC 15-minute JWT with silent refresh; httpOnly cookie refresh token with Secure and SameSite=Strict. GKE Network Policy denies ingress from CIDR ranges outside utility VPN allocation. IAP enforces authentication before Cloud Load Balancer allows connection. PostgreSQL row-level security: field_crew role can only read/write switching_steps assigned to their crew_assignment_id. IEC 61968/61970 CIM interoperability satisfied by pandapower CIM XML parser consuming standard CIM profiles.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot cluster us-central1 with Istio 1.21 installed via GKE managed Istio add-on. PeerAuthentication resource enforces mTLS STRICT across the grid-ops namespace. Three Deployments with HPA: topology-api (2-6 pods on WebSocket connection count > 100 per pod), switching-order-api (2-4 pods on CPU 70%), integration-api (1-2 pods). Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 HA (2 vCPUs, 8 GB) with PITR 1-minute granularity. Memorystore Redis 6 GB. GitLab CI pipeline: ruff, mypy strict, pytest 90% gate, Vue ESLint + tsc, Vitest 85% gate, docker buildx multi-arch, Artifact Registry push, Helm chart version bump, ArgoCD auto-sync to staging. Production deployment requires control room supervisor GitLab approval and scheduled maintenance window. ArgoCD automated rollback on failed readiness probe.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics: switching_orders_created_total, switching_orders_completed_total, step_locks_acquired_total, step_lock_timeout_total (PagerDuty alert on increment), topology_rule_violations_blocked_total, cim_imports_total (labeled by success/failure), websocket_connections_active (gauge). Grafana dashboards: Switching Order Pipeline (orders by status, avg time-to-complete per phase), Step Lock Health (active locks, timeout rate), Topology Change Rate. Google Cloud Logging: structured JSON with switching_order_id and step_id correlation fields. PagerDuty P2 on step_lock_timeout (crew unresponsive), P1 on switching-order-api pod crash during in_execution order.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated ,800/month for control room pilot (10 dispatchers, 50 field crew): GKE Autopilot ~, Cloud SQL HA ~, Memorystore Redis ~, Cloud Load Balancing ~, Istio/Keycloak overhead ~, Artifact Registry/GitLab ~, Google Cloud Logging ~, PagerDuty ~. Scale to 200 concurrent users: GKE autoscaling handles without configuration change; Cloud SQL scale to 4 vCPUs at ~,200.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Pilot (8 weeks): Keycloak OIDC + LDAP federation, switching order CRUD with TopologyRuleEngine validation, step execution with StepLockManager 10-minute TTL, basic SVG single-line diagram, CIM XML import with idempotency, audit log, GKE deployment with Istio mTLS, GitLab CI/CD. Phase 2 (4 weeks): real-time WebSocket topology updates via Redis pub/sub, photo evidence upload to Cloud Storage, outage ticket management, crew assignment module, Grafana dashboards. Phase 3 (4 weeks): CIM import batch rollback endpoint, SCADA device state integration via integration-api, advanced topology rule validation (N-1 analysis), Prometheus SLO alerting, NERC CIP-002 audit report export.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with ruff and mypy strict enforced in CI. SQLAlchemy 2.0 async throughout; no raw SQL except topology_rule_violations materialized view definition. All switching step state transitions go through SwitchingOrderStateMachine - no direct status field updates in route handlers. Vue 3 Composition API with explicit TypeScript types for all Pinia store state. Step lock logic has 100% pytest coverage with mocked Redis including TTL expiry simulation. CIM parser tests use real IEC 61968 CIM XML sample fixtures, not synthetic test data.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GKE Autopilot pod eviction during active switching order with locked steps. Mitigation: switching-order-api PodDisruptionBudget ensures minimum 1 pod available during cluster maintenance; Redis TTL auto-release handles lock cleanup on pod restart. Risk: TopologyRuleEngine materialized view refresh blocks CIM import transaction during high-frequency imports. Mitigation: REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY used to avoid table lock. Risk: WebSocket connection loss drops real-time topology updates without client notification. Mitigation: client-side reconnection with exponential backoff, server sends full topology snapshot on reconnect. Tradeoff: pandapower for CIM parsing adds 80 MB to container image - accepted for rich CIM profile support over writing custom parser. Risk: Cloud SQL PITR restore to 1-minute granularity could lose up to 60 seconds of switching order state. Mitigation: acceptable for pilot; production version should add synchronous replication with 0-RPO for safety-critical switching data.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose D3.js SVG rendering over Leaflet/GeoJSON for single-line diagram - SVG schematic is the correct representation for distribution network topology, not geographic map. Confirmed pandapower for CIM XML parsing over custom lxml-based parser - pandapower handles complex CIM profile variations in utility GIS exports. Memorystore Redis chosen over Pub/Sub for topology broadcast due to sub-millisecond pub/sub latency requirement for real-time diagram updates. step_lock_timeout set to 10 minutes based on field crew task analysis showing 95th-percentile manual switching time is 7 minutes.

metadata.notes: Round 3 strong revision resolving all three issues: Istio mTLS, step lock timeout, and CIM import idempotency for distribution grid switching order management on GKE.

profile.domain: Transmission Planning Engineering — Load Flow Analysis Desktop Tool

project_goal.value: Replace a legacy DOS-based load flow calculation tool with a modern desktop application for steady-state power flow, voltage profile analysis, and N-1 contingency screening across high-voltage transmission networks
project_goal.rationale: Legacy tool is unmaintained, lacks GUI, and cannot import modern network model formats used by ISOs

target_users.value: Transmission planning engineers at large investor-owned utilities running steady-state and contingency analyses
target_users.rationale: Primary user group identified in stakeholder interviews with planning departments

project_class.rationale: Compute-intensive Newton-Raphson solver runs locally on engineering workstations; no cloud compute required

capabilities.rationale: Electron UI, embedded Python solver, local SQLite persistence, and analytical reporting

complexity_level.rationale: Sparse matrix Newton-Raphson solver, PSS/E RAW file parsing, and single-line diagram rendering require specialized engineering

risk_level.rationale: Internal-only application handling non-public network models; no external attack surface

data_sensitivity.rationale: Network topology models are sensitive but not subject to NERC CIP Critical Infrastructure Protection requirements at this classification

external_exposure.rationale: Application runs entirely on workstation with no outbound network calls except optional Sentry crash telemetry

access_model.value: Windows LDAP single sign-on; role separation between read-only analysts and licensed planners who can execute solver runs
access_model.rationale: Integrates with existing Active Directory infrastructure used by all utility engineering applications

feature_scope.value: PSS/E RAW v29-v33 file import, Newton-Raphson load flow solver, N-1 and N-2 contingency screening, voltage violation detection, overloaded branch flagging, annotated single-line diagram via React Flow, PDF and Excel report export
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set replacing legacy capabilities plus modern report generation

mvp_scope.value: PSS/E RAW import with version auto-detection, base case Newton-Raphson solver via msgpack IPC, N-1 contingency screening, voltage and thermal violation summary report
mvp_scope.rationale: Core planning workflow needed for Q2 transmission expansion study deadline

security_baseline.value: Windows LDAP authentication, SQLite database encrypted with SQLCipher AES-256, no outbound internet access except anonymized Sentry telemetry, application MSI code-signed with utility EV certificate
security_baseline.rationale: Workstation security policy requires signed executables and domain authentication

frontend_stack.value: Electron 30 with React 18 and TypeScript, React Flow 11 for single-line diagram canvas, Recharts for voltage profile plots
frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise in React; Electron provides native file system access for PSS/E model files

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with NumPy 1.26 and SciPy 1.12 for sparse matrix Newton-Raphson, exposed via FastAPI-over-stdio msgpack bridge to Electron main process
backend_stack.rationale: SciPy sparse linear algebra is the standard for power systems solver implementations; msgpack bridge resolves 60-second IPC constraint

data_platform.value: SQLite 3.45 with SQLCipher AES-256 encryption for case storage; solver results stored as msgpack BLOB columns
data_platform.rationale: No network database needed for local-only workstation application; msgpack BLOBs avoid re-serialization overhead

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Windows 11 engineering workstations with 32 GB RAM, distributed via MSI installer through SCCM
hosting_target.rationale: Engineers work on dedicated high-RAM workstations for large network models

privacy_retention_policy.value: Network model files and calculation results retained locally per workstation; no centralized data collection; Sentry crash reports anonymized before transmission
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Data governance policy keeps proprietary network models on-premises

future_scope.value: Multi-case scenario comparison, IEC CIM XML import, PTDF/LODF sensitivity matrix calculation, optional shared case library on corporate file server via UNC path
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 and 3 roadmap after MVP adoption

constraints.value: Must run on Windows 11 workstations with 32 GB RAM; solver must handle networks up to 15,000 buses within 60-second wall-clock limit; no mandatory cloud connectivity
constraints.rationale: Engineering workstation spec and planning study time constraints

observability_baseline.value: Sentry Desktop SDK for crash reporting with anonymized payloads; local perf_log.jsonl per solver run capturing bus count, solve time, and convergence status
observability_baseline.rationale: Crash telemetry sent to Sentry SaaS; no centralized logging infrastructure needed for workstation-only app

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery with monthly internal milestone reviews; planning engineers participate in UAT for each solver feature before release
execution_preference.rationale: Domain expert feedback loop is critical for numerical solver validation against known reference cases

project.goal: Modernize transmission planning load flow tooling replacing legacy DOS tool
project.target_users: Transmission planning engineers at investor-owned utilities
project.timeline: Q2 MVP for N-1 contingency studies

frontend.diagram: React Flow 11 for interactive single-line diagram

backend.runtime: Python 3.12 embedded via msgpack stdin/stdout bridge
backend.solver: NumPy/SciPy sparse Newton-Raphson with LU factorization
backend.parser: Custom PSS/E RAW v29-v33 parser with version auto-detection

security.signing: MSI code-signed with utility EV certificate

data.blob_format: msgpack-encoded NumPy arrays for solver results

devops.packaging: electron-builder MSI with embedded Python runtime

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP scope correctly focuses on PSS/E import, base case solve, and N-1 screening for the Q2 transmission expansion study deadline. Deferring PTDF sensitivity matrices and multi-case comparison to Phase 2 is appropriate. The version auto-detection fix for PSS/E RAW files removes a major friction point engineers encounter when importing models from different ISO sources.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Switching to FastAPI-over-stdio with msgpack binary framing resolves the critical IPC bottleneck. Transferring 15,000-element NumPy arrays as msgpack bytes over stdin/stdout reduces overhead from 8-12 seconds per N-1 batch to under 0.5 seconds, keeping the full contingency screen within the 60-second wall-clock limit. Storing solver result BLOBs in SQLite avoids re-serialization on report generation. The compensation theorem shortcut for N-1 screening using cached base-case LU factors is the correct algorithmic choice.
reasoner_reviews.security: SQLCipher AES-256 for the local case database correctly protects proprietary network topology models at the internal sensitivity classification. Windows LDAP integration enforces domain authentication. Code signing with utility EV certificate is non-negotiable for SCCM deployment. Sentry payload anonymization (stripping file paths, sending only exception type and stack frame hashes) is necessary before enabling crash telemetry.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 60-second constraint is now achievable with msgpack IPC: base Y-bus formation benchmarks at 8 seconds, and N-1 screening of 2,000 branches using compensation theorem completes in under 45 seconds on a 12-core workstation with the base-case LU factors cached. The 32 GB RAM workstation spec comfortably supports the sparse Y-bus for 15,000-bus networks.

backend.service_design: Python solver module structured as a FastAPI-over-stdio server launched by Electron main process; receives msgpack-encoded command frames on stdin and returns msgpack-encoded result frames on stdout, eliminating Electron IPC JSON overhead for large NumPy array transfers
backend.business_modules: NetworkParser (PSS/E RAW v29-v33 lexer with @-comment version token detection and field layout schema registry), YBusBuilder (CSR sparse admittance matrix formation with shunt admittances and transformer tap ratios), NewtonRaphsonSolver (Jacobian formation, scipy.sparse.linalg.splu LU factorization, mismatch iteration with convergence norm tracking), ContingencyScreener (compensation theorem N-1 batch processor using cached base-case LU factors, worker thread pool sized to CPU count minus one), ViolationDetector (per-unit voltage threshold and branch MVA thermal rating checks)
backend.failure_handling: Solver divergence caught when mismatch norm exceeds 1e-3 after 50 iterations; fallback to fast-decoupled Newton-Raphson with user notification dialog; non-convergent contingencies logged with NC flag in violation report rather than crashing the batch
backend.scaling_notes: Compensation theorem shortcut reduces N-1 screening from O(n*solver_time) to O(n*forward_back_substitution), enabling 2,000-branch screening in under 45 seconds on a 12-core 32 GB workstation
backend.backend_risks: PSS/E RAW format v29/v33 field layout differences in bus and branch records; auto-detection mitigated by @-comment version token; CIM XML import deferred to Phase 3 to contain scope

frontend.app_structure: Electron 30 shell with React renderer; Electron main process manages Python solver subprocess lifecycle, Windows LDAP session, SQLite case database, and msgpack IPC routing; renderer hosts React SPA
frontend.pages_and_flows: Case Manager (open/save/import RAW files, recent cases list), Network Viewer (React Flow single-line diagram with voltage-coded bus nodes and loading-percentage-colored branch edges), Solver Console (run controls, real-time per-iteration mismatch chart, convergence status), Contingency Results (virtualized violation table sortable by severity, voltage deviation, and thermal loading), Report Generator (PDF and XLSX export preview with filter controls)
frontend.ui_modules: BusNode (React Flow custom node with voltage magnitude and angle tooltip), BranchEdge (color-coded by MVA loading percentage with thermal rating label), ViolationBadge (inline flag on overloaded or under/over-voltage elements), SolverProgressStream (real-time iteration counter and mismatch norm chart fed via msgpack frames), ContingencyViolationTable (react-virtual for 2,000-row result sets)
frontend.performance_notes: React Flow canvas virtualized; only elements within viewport rendered for 15,000-node diagrams. Contingency results table uses react-virtual for smooth scrolling over 2,000+ rows. Voltage profile charts use canvas-based Recharts to avoid SVG layout thrashing.

security.auth_design: Electron main process calls Windows LDAP via electron-windows-active-directory on every app launch; application blocks solver execution and exits on domain session expiry; no standalone credential store
security.secrets_management: SQLCipher database encryption key derived from user LDAP credentials hash XOR machine GUID; key computed at runtime and never persisted to disk or registry
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All case opens, solver runs, and report exports logged to audit_events SQLite table with user domain account, action type, case file path, and ISO 8601 timestamp; table protected by append-only trigger to prevent deletion

data.entities: bus_nodes (id, case_id, name, base_kv, bus_type ENUM PQ/PV/slack, vm_pu, va_deg, zone_id, area_id), transmission_lines (id, case_id, from_bus_id, to_bus_id, r_pu, x_pu, b_pu, rating_mva, status), generators (id, case_id, bus_id, pg_mw, qg_mvar, pmax, pmin, vset_pu, status), load_forecasts (id, case_id, bus_id, pd_mw, qd_mvar), contingency_scenarios (id, case_id, outage_type ENUM branch/generator, element_id, name), power_flow_results (id, case_id, contingency_id, bus_voltages_msgpack BLOB, branch_flows_msgpack BLOB, converged BOOL, iterations INT, solve_time_ms, created_at), audit_events (id, user_domain, action, case_path, timestamp)
data.storage_design: SQLite database per case file packaged as a self-contained .lfc bundle containing network model, msgpack BLOB solver results, and audit event log; enables file-share distribution on corporate file server without a database server
data.schema_notes: bus_voltages and branch_flows stored as msgpack-encoded NumPy float64 arrays in BLOB columns; avoids JSON round-trip overhead when generating reports from stored results
data.migration_notes: Schema versioned via SQLite user_version PRAGMA; migration scripts bundled in MSI installer and executed on first launch after upgrade; .lfc format backwards-compatible to allow older solver versions to re-open cases

devops.deployment_topology: Single MSI installer bundles Electron 30 app, embedded Python 3.12 runtime with pre-compiled NumPy/SciPy Windows x64 wheels, SQLCipher DLL, and Sentry SDK; distributed via SCCM to 40 engineering workstations
devops.environments: dev (developer workstation with verbose solver logging and Sentry debug mode), staging (one UAT engineer workstation with production LDAP and realistic 5,000-bus test case), production (SCCM push to 40 engineering workstations on scheduled maintenance window)
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions Windows runner pipeline: ESLint TypeScript lint, pytest solver unit tests against IEEE 14-bus and 118-bus reference solutions, electron-builder MSI generation, signtool.exe EV certificate signing using certificate in GitHub Actions secret, upload to internal Nexus artifact store for SCCM pickup
devops.rollback_strategy: Previous MSI version retained in SCCM package history; IT re-pushes prior version on request; .lfc case file schema backwards-compatibility ensures cases opened in newer version remain readable by rollback version
devops.observability_stack: Sentry Desktop SDK with anonymized payloads (file paths stripped, only exception type and module-hashed stack frames); local perf_log.jsonl per solver run with bus_count, n1_count, base_solve_ms, n1_batch_ms, peak_ram_mb, converged flag

ISS-001.title: IPC JSON serialization causes N-1 batch result transfer to exceed 60-second constraint
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan specified JSON-over-Electron-IPC for solver result transfer. Transferring 15,000-element NumPy voltage arrays as JSON strings adds 8-12 seconds overhead per N-1 contingency batch, making full N-1 screening infeasible within the 60-second wall-clock limit.

history.note: Identified by constraints reviewer during round 1 audit

ISS-002.title: PSS/E RAW version auto-detection not specified
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan did not specify how the parser handles PSS/E RAW files from v29 through v33, which have differing field counts. Engineers use models from multiple ISO sources with different format versions.

history.note: Raised by backend specialist review

focus_issues.title: IPC JSON serialization causes N-1 batch result transfer to exceed 60-second constraint
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan specified JSON IPC; 15,000-element voltage arrays as JSON strings add 8-12 seconds overhead per contingency batch.

latest_recommendations: Replace JSON IPC with msgpack binary framing via stdin/stdout subprocess bridge for solver result transfer
Specify PSS/E RAW version auto-detection via @-comment header token parsing

convergence_state.reason: High severity IPC serialization issue blocks 60-second N-1 screening constraint

previous_audits.summary: Sound architectural foundation with correct solver and UI technology choices, but the IPC data serialization strategy is critically underspecified and will cause the 60-second N-1 screening constraint to be violated on large networks.
strengths: Correct choice of SciPy sparse Newton-Raphson for the solver core
React Flow single-line diagram is appropriate for network visualization
SQLCipher encryption correctly applied to local case database
concerns: JSON serialization of 15,000-element NumPy arrays over Electron IPC will add 8-12 seconds per N-1 batch
PSS/E RAW version handling unaddressed despite engineers using v29-v33 files from multiple ISO sources
blocking_issues: IPC serialization must switch to msgpack binary framing before N-1 batch screening can meet the 60-second wall-clock limit
recommendations: Adopt FastAPI-over-stdio with msgpack framing for Python solver subprocess communication
Add @-comment version token detection to RAW parser with field layout schema registry
Store solver result arrays as msgpack BLOBs in SQLite to avoid re-serialization on report generation

issue_updates.title: IPC JSON serialization causes N-1 batch result transfer to exceed 60-second constraint
issue_updates.detail: Plan specified JSON IPC but did not address serialization bottleneck for large array transfers

issue_updates.title: PSS/E RAW version auto-detection not specified

previous_plan.title: Transmission Load Flow Desktop Application Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Desktop replacement for legacy DOS load flow tool built on Electron 30 and React 18. Python 3.12 NumPy/SciPy solver embedded via Electron IPC JSON. SQLite with SQLCipher for case storage. Targets 15,000-bus transmission networks with N-1 contingency screening.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Electron shell hosts React 18 renderer. Python 3.12 solver spawned as child process, communicates results over Electron IPC as JSON strings. SQLite stores cases. MSI installer bundles all dependencies for SCCM deployment.
previous_plan.data_model: bus_nodes, transmission_lines, generators, load_forecasts, contingency_scenarios, power_flow_results stored per case in SQLite with SQLCipher encryption.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Windows LDAP authentication. SQLCipher AES-256 for local database. Code-signed MSI. Sentry crash reporting.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must resolve the critical IPC serialization bottleneck by switching from JSON-over-Electron-IPC to a FastAPI-over-stdio msgpack bridge. This is the blocking fix for the 60-second N-1 screening constraint on 15,000-bus networks. Simultaneously addressing PSS/E RAW version auto-detection to handle ISO model files from v29 through v33. The overall Electron/Python/SQLite architecture is sound and carries forward with these targeted fixes applied to the IPC layer and parser module.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced Electron IPC JSON with FastAPI-over-stdio Python server using msgpack binary framing. Electron main process communicates with the Python solver subprocess via stdin/stdout msgpack frames, reducing 15,000-element voltage array transfer overhead from 8-12 seconds to under 0.5 seconds. Solver results stored as msgpack BLOB in SQLite to avoid re-serialization on report generation.
fix_report.expected_outcome: N-1 contingency screening of 2,000 branches on 15,000-bus network completes within 60-second wall-clock limit; ISS-001 resolved

fix_report.action_taken: Added PSS/E RAW version auto-detection to parser: reads first @-prefixed comment line to extract version token (e.g. 'PSS/E-30' or 'PSS/E-33'), selects matching field layout schema from version registry dict, falls back to v33 layout for unknown tokens with user warning dialog.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Parser correctly handles RAW files from all major ISO sources across v29-v33 format versions without manual engineer intervention

target_output.title: Transmission Load Flow Desktop Application Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A modern desktop replacement for a legacy DOS load flow tool built with Electron 30 and React 18 for Windows 11 engineering workstations. A Python 3.12 solver subprocess using NumPy/SciPy sparse Newton-Raphson communicates with the Electron main process via a FastAPI-over-stdio msgpack bridge, resolving the IPC serialization bottleneck and enabling N-1 contingency screening of 15,000-bus transmission networks within a 60-second wall-clock limit. PSS/E RAW v29-v33 import with version auto-detection, React Flow interactive single-line diagram, and PDF/XLSX contingency violation reports deliver a complete transmission planning workflow replacing the legacy tool.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-process desktop architecture: Electron 30 main process manages Windows LDAP authentication, SQLite case database, and the Python solver subprocess lifecycle. The Python subprocess runs a FastAPI-over-stdio server that receives msgpack-encoded command frames on stdin and returns msgpack-encoded result arrays on stdout, eliminating JSON serialization overhead for 15,000-element bus voltage and branch flow arrays. The Electron renderer process hosts the React 18 SPA including React Flow single-line diagram canvas, real-time solver console, virtualized contingency violation table, and report generator. SQLite 3.45 with SQLCipher stores case data and solver result arrays as msgpack BLOB columns. MSI installer bundles Electron, embedded Python 3.12 runtime, NumPy/SciPy wheels, and SQLCipher DLL for zero-dependency workstation deployment via SCCM.
target_output.technology_stack: Desktop shell: Electron 30. Frontend: React 18, TypeScript strict mode, React Flow 11 for single-line diagram, Recharts for voltage profile plots, react-virtual for virtualized contingency tables. IPC protocol: msgpack-python binary framing via subprocess stdin/stdout. Solver: Python 3.12, NumPy 1.26, SciPy 1.12 (sparse.linalg.splu for LU factorization). Database: SQLite 3.45 with SQLCipher AES-256. Auth: electron-windows-active-directory for Windows LDAP. Packaging: electron-builder MSI with embedded Python runtime. Crash reporting: Sentry Desktop SDK with anonymized payloads. CI: GitHub Actions Windows runner with signtool.exe EV certificate signing.
target_output.functional_feature_map: PSS/E RAW Import: parse v29-v33 network model files with @-comment version token auto-detection into bus_nodes, transmission_lines, generators, load_forecasts entities stored in SQLite. Base Case Load Flow: Newton-Raphson solver with configurable convergence tolerance and iteration limit; real-time per-iteration mismatch display in solver console. N-1 Contingency Screening: outage each branch using compensation theorem on cached base-case LU factors; flag voltage per-unit and branch MVA thermal violations. Single-Line Diagram: interactive React Flow canvas with voltage-coded bus nodes and loading-percentage branch edges. Report Export: PDF via puppeteer-core and XLSX via exceljs from violation records.
target_output.system_components: Electron Main Process: app lifecycle manager, Windows LDAP session handler via electron-windows-active-directory, SQLite case database manager with SQLCipher, Python solver subprocess launcher and msgpack IPC router, local localhost:19234 HTTP server for puppeteer PDF rendering. Python Solver Subprocess: FastAPI-over-stdio server with modules NetworkParser (PSS/E RAW v29-v33 with version auto-detection from @-comment token), YBusBuilder (CSR sparse admittance matrix with shunt admittances and tap ratios), NewtonRaphsonSolver (scipy.sparse.linalg.splu LU factorization, mismatch iteration), ContingencyScreener (N-1 compensation theorem batch worker pool), ViolationDetector (voltage per-unit and MVA thermal threshold checks). React Renderer: React Flow canvas with custom BusNode and BranchEdge types, SolverProgressStream component, react-virtual ContingencyViolationTable, Recharts voltage profile panel, Report Generator with filter controls. SQLite/SQLCipher Case Bundle (.lfc): network model tables, power_flow_results with msgpack BLOB columns, audit_events log.
target_output.workflows: PSS/E Import and Base Case Solve: Engineer opens Case Manager and selects a .raw file. Parser reads first @-comment line to extract version token, selects field layout schema from version registry, builds bus_nodes and transmission_lines records, stores in SQLite case bundle. Engineer configures solver tolerance and clicks Run Load Flow. Electron main sends msgpack command frame to Python subprocess stdin: {cmd:'solve_base', params:{tol:1e-6, max_iter:50}}. Solver forms sparse Y-bus via YBusBuilder, performs LU factorization via splu, iterates Newton-Raphson streaming per-iteration mismatch frames back to renderer. On convergence returns {type:'solve_result', data:{converged:true, bus_voltages:<msgpack bytes>, branch_flows:<msgpack bytes>}}. Electron stores msgpack BLOBs in power_flow_results. React Flow canvas re-renders with voltage-coded nodes. N-1 Contingency Screening: Engineer selects contingency set and clicks Screen N-1. Electron main dispatches {cmd:'screen_n1', params:{contingency_ids:[...], chunk_size:50}} to Python subprocess. ContingencyScreener applies compensation theorem per outage using cached base-case LU factors, collecting voltage and thermal violations per contingency. Results stream back as {type:'contingency_result', data:{contingency_id, converged, voltage_violations:[...], thermal_violations:[...]}} frames. Non-convergent contingencies flagged NC. ContingencyViolationTable populates with full results for engineer review and filter.
target_output.data_model: bus_nodes (id INTEGER PK, case_id FK, name TEXT, base_kv REAL, bus_type TEXT CHECK IN ('PQ','PV','slack'), vm_pu REAL, va_deg REAL, zone_id INTEGER, area_id INTEGER). transmission_lines (id INTEGER PK, case_id FK, from_bus_id FK, to_bus_id FK, r_pu REAL, x_pu REAL, b_pu REAL, rating_mva REAL, status INTEGER). generators (id INTEGER PK, case_id FK, bus_id FK, pg_mw REAL, qg_mvar REAL, pmax REAL, pmin REAL, vset_pu REAL, status INTEGER). load_forecasts (id INTEGER PK, case_id FK, bus_id FK, pd_mw REAL, qd_mvar REAL). contingency_scenarios (id INTEGER PK, case_id FK, outage_type TEXT CHECK IN ('branch','generator'), element_id INTEGER, name TEXT). power_flow_results (id INTEGER PK, case_id FK, contingency_id FK NULLABLE, bus_voltages_msgpack BLOB, branch_flows_msgpack BLOB, converged INTEGER, iterations INTEGER, solve_time_ms INTEGER, created_at TEXT). audit_events (id INTEGER PK, user_domain TEXT, action TEXT, case_path TEXT, timestamp TEXT). Schema versioned via SQLite user_version PRAGMA; migrations bundled in MSI installer.
target_output.api_design: Msgpack IPC protocol over subprocess stdin/stdout — not HTTP REST. Command frames sent by Electron main to Python subprocess stdin: {cmd:'solve_base', params:{tol:1e-6, max_iter:50}}; {cmd:'screen_n1', params:{contingency_ids:[42,43,...], chunk_size:50}}; {cmd:'get_violations', params:{v_min:0.95, v_max:1.05, mva_threshold:100.0}}. Response frames returned on Python stdout: {type:'iter_update', data:{iteration:3, mismatch_norm:0.0023}}; {type:'solve_result', data:{converged:true, iterations:8, solve_time_ms:7200, bus_voltages:<msgpack-bytes>, branch_flows:<msgpack-bytes>}}; {type:'contingency_result', data:{contingency_id:42, converged:true, voltage_violations:[{bus_id:1234, vm_pu:0.932}], thermal_violations:[{line_id:89, loading_pct:108.4}]}}. Local HTTP (localhost:19234): GET /report/violations?v_min=0.95&mva_threshold=100 returns HTML violation summary for puppeteer PDF rendering.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Windows LDAP authentication enforced at app launch via electron-windows-active-directory; solver execution blocked and application exits on domain session expiry. SQLCipher AES-256 database encryption key derived at runtime from LDAP credentials hash XOR machine GUID; key never persisted to disk or Windows registry. MSI code-signed with utility EV certificate; SCCM endpoint policy blocks unsigned executables. Sentry crash telemetry anonymized before transmission: file paths stripped, only exception class and module-hashed stack frame identifiers sent. All case file operations and solver runs logged to append-only audit_events SQLite table with user domain account and ISO 8601 timestamp.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions Windows runner CI pipeline: ESLint TypeScript lint, pytest solver unit tests validating Newton-Raphson convergence against IEEE 14-bus reference (max bus voltage error < 1e-6 per-unit) and IEEE 118-bus case, electron-builder MSI generation, signtool.exe EV certificate signing using certificate stored in GitHub Actions encrypted secret, upload to internal Nexus artifact repository for SCCM pickup. Three environments: dev (developer workstation with verbose solver frame logging and Sentry debug mode), staging (single planning engineer UAT workstation with production LDAP and a 5,000-bus test network), production (SCCM push to 40 engineering workstations on scheduled maintenance window). Rollback: previous MSI retained in SCCM package history; IT re-pushes prior version; .lfc case file schema backwards-compatibility via user_version PRAGMA ensures cases remain openable after rollback. Installer embeds Python 3.12 runtime with NumPy/SciPy wheels pre-compiled for Windows x64 to avoid pip execution on locked-down workstations.
target_output.observability: Sentry Desktop SDK captures unhandled exceptions and Python subprocess crashes; releases tagged by MSI build version for regression tracking across workstation fleet. Local perf_log.jsonl written after each solver run: {timestamp, case_id, bus_count, branch_count, n1_contingency_count, base_solve_ms, n1_batch_ms, peak_ram_mb, converged, solver_version}. Case Manager settings panel displays solver performance history from local perf_log for the current workstation. Threshold alert: if base_solve_ms exceeds 30,000 on a case, a dialog warns the engineer that the network size is approaching workstation memory limits and suggests enabling fast-decoupled fallback.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero recurring cloud infrastructure cost; all computation runs on existing engineering workstations. Sentry free tier covers crash telemetry from 40 workstations. GitHub Actions Windows runner CI estimated at 200 minutes per month. SCCM distribution scales to additional workstations at no incremental software cost. Scaling path for networks exceeding 15,000 buses: Python solver can be replaced with a Julia SparseArrays Newton-Raphson implementation (3-5x speed improvement) without changing the msgpack IPC protocol, deferring workstation hardware upgrades.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): PSS/E RAW v29-v33 import with @-comment version auto-detection, base case Newton-Raphson solver via msgpack stdin/stdout bridge, N-1 branch contingency screening using compensation theorem, voltage and thermal violation summary table, basic React Flow single-line diagram with voltage coloring, PDF violation report via puppeteer-core, Windows LDAP auth, SQLCipher case database, MSI installer with embedded Python. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Annotated React Flow diagram snapshots in PDF reports, XLSX export via exceljs, multi-case scenario comparison panel, Recharts voltage profile chart per selected contingency. Phase 3 (8 weeks): IEC CIM XML network model import, PTDF and LODF sensitivity matrix calculation, optional shared case library on corporate UNC file server with read/write locking.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode for all Electron main and React renderer code. ESLint with import/no-cycle to prevent circular Electron main/renderer module dependencies. Python solver covered by pytest: Newton-Raphson convergence validated against IEEE 14-bus and 118-bus reference solutions with per-unit voltage error tolerance 1e-6. Msgpack IPC protocol versioned with a 'proto_version' field in every command and response frame; increment on breaking schema changes. Case file .lfc schema versioned via SQLite user_version PRAGMA; migration scripts mandatory in every MSI installer that changes the schema. PRs require one senior engineer review plus one domain-expert planning engineer sign-off for any changes to solver modules.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SciPy sparse LU memory footprint on very dense 15,000-bus Y-bus matrices approaching 32 GB workstation RAM limit. Mitigation: profile Y-bus sparsity on largest known ISO network models in staging; switch to iterative GMRES solver with ILU preconditioner if peak RAM exceeds 24 GB. Risk: Electron major version upgrades breaking electron-windows-active-directory LDAP native bindings. Mitigation: pin Electron version in package.json; test LDAP integration in staging before each Electron major upgrade. Risk: Python subprocess crash leaving Electron in unresponsive state. Mitigation: Electron main process monitors subprocess exit code and surfaces a restart dialog. Tradeoff: msgpack stdin/stdout bridge vs. Electron contextBridge JSON — msgpack approach adds subprocess management complexity but is the only path to meeting the 60-second N-1 screening constraint on 15,000-bus networks. Tradeoff: SQLite per-case-file vs. shared PostgreSQL — local SQLite enables offline operation and easy case file sharing over corporate file server but precludes concurrent multi-user edits to the same case.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected msgpack stdin/stdout subprocess bridge over Electron contextBridge JSON to resolve 60-second N-1 screening constraint. PSS/E RAW version auto-detection specified via @-comment header token with version registry dict fallback to v33. IEEE 14-bus and 118-bus reference cases chosen as pytest solver validation benchmarks. puppeteer-core selected for PDF generation over electron-pdf to avoid unmaintained dependency. Compensation theorem confirmed as N-1 screening algorithm to reuse cached base-case LU factors rather than re-solving full Newton-Raphson per contingency.

metadata.notes: Desktop transmission load flow tool; round 2 resolves critical msgpack IPC fix for 60-second N-1 screening constraint on 15K-bus networks.

profile.domain: Utility Metering — Smart Meter Interval Data Ingestion Pipeline

project_goal.value: Ingest raw 15-minute interval meter readings from hundreds of thousands of smart meters via SFTP drops and MDMS APIs, normalize and validate readings, and load clean interval data into a ClickHouse time-series warehouse for billing and analytics consumption
project_goal.rationale: Current manual SFTP-to-spreadsheet process cannot scale to 500K meter estate and introduces billing errors from unvalidated data

target_users.value: Billing system, analytics team, and demand forecasting models consuming validated interval data from ClickHouse
target_users.rationale: Downstream consumers identified in data architecture review

project_class.rationale: Pure data ingestion and transformation workload with no user-facing UI

capabilities.rationale: Batch Spark processing, SFTP/MDMS integrations, and ClickHouse data platform

complexity_level.rationale: 500K meters generating 48 readings per meter per day equals 24 million records per day requiring distributed processing

risk_level.rationale: Pipeline processes personal customer energy consumption data; billing errors from corrupt data have regulatory consequences

data_sensitivity.rationale: 15-minute interval readings reveal household occupancy patterns and appliance usage; classified as personal data under CCPA

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline runs entirely within AWS VPC; no public endpoints

access_model.value: IAM role-based access; pipeline service roles scoped to S3 staging bucket and ClickHouse write; billing team read-only on ClickHouse interval_readings table
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege IAM for all pipeline components

feature_scope.value: SFTP polling and MDMS API ingestion, PSV/CSV format normalization to canonical interval schema, anomaly detection (zero reads, stuck meters, impossible consumption spikes), gap detection for missing intervals, ClickHouse bulk load, billing-ready daily summary generation, data quality reporting dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for metering data operations

mvp_scope.value: SFTP ingestion for top 5 meter vendors, PSV normalization, basic anomaly flagging (zero reads and 3-sigma spikes), ClickHouse load, nightly billing summary export
mvp_scope.rationale: Covers 80% of meter estate with top 5 vendor formats for initial cutover

security_baseline.value: S3 staging bucket encrypted with SSE-KMS, ClickHouse TLS connections, IAM roles for all pipeline components, VPC-only networking, no PII in CloudWatch logs
security_baseline.rationale: Minimum controls for personal data pipeline on AWS

frontend_stack.rationale: Data pipeline has no user-facing frontend

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Spark on EMR Serverless for distributed processing
backend_stack.rationale: Spark handles 24M daily records; EMR Serverless eliminates cluster management

data_platform.value: ClickHouse on AWS EC2 (r6i.4xlarge cluster) for time-series interval data; S3 for raw file staging and archival
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar storage optimized for time-series analytics queries; S3 for durable raw file retention

hosting_target.value: AWS EMR Serverless for Spark jobs, EC2 ClickHouse cluster in VPC, ECS Fargate for orchestration service
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless Spark reduces idle cost; EC2 ClickHouse gives performance tuning control

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw meter files retained in S3 for 7 years per utility tariff accuracy standards; interval_readings in ClickHouse retained 3 years hot then archived to S3 Iceberg; customer PII fields tokenized before ClickHouse load
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Tariff standards require raw data retention; ClickHouse hot retention for billing lookback

future_scope.value: Real-time streaming path for AMI head-end push delivery, automated meter health scoring, integration with demand response event management system
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 enhancements after batch pipeline stabilizes

constraints.value: Must process prior-day meter files and load ClickHouse before 06:00 billing batch window; pipeline must handle 500K meters at full estate scale; raw files must never be deleted from S3 staging until load confirmed
constraints.rationale: Billing batch dependency and data durability requirements

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards on Prometheus metrics; per-run validation exception counts; pipeline completion alerts via PagerDuty before 06:00 deadline
observability_baseline.rationale: Ops team monitors nightly pipeline health before billing batch starts

execution_preference.value: Trunk-based development with daily deploys; staging environment with sampled production data for integration testing
execution_preference.rationale: Fast feedback loop for pipeline correctness validation

compliance_context.value: CCPA data minimization and retention compliance; utility tariff accuracy standards requiring validated interval data for billing; customer PII fields (name, address) tokenized; no interval data in CloudWatch logs
compliance_context.rationale: Personal data classification and billing regulatory requirements

project.goal: Automated ingestion and validation of 15-minute smart meter interval data for billing and analytics
project.target_users: Billing system and analytics consumers of ClickHouse interval data

security.auth: IAM roles for all pipeline components

data.archival: S3 Iceberg for cold interval data

constraints.durability: Raw files retained in S3 until load confirmed

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP scope covering top 5 meter vendor formats is pragmatic. These vendors represent 80% of the 500K meter estate, enabling billing cutover on schedule. MDMS API integration can follow in the next sprint once SFTP path is stable.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Spark on EMR Serverless is appropriate for 24M daily records but job startup latency (60-90 seconds) must be accounted for in the 06:00 deadline SLA. Pre-warming the EMR application or scheduling jobs 2 hours before the deadline provides adequate buffer. ClickHouse MergeTree table partitioned by toYYYYMM(interval_timestamp) with an ORDER BY (meter_id, interval_timestamp) gives optimal query performance for billing lookback queries.
reasoner_reviews.security: Customer PII tokenization before ClickHouse load is correctly specified. CloudWatch log filtering to strip meter serial numbers from job logs is essential. S3 staging bucket should enforce object lock in governance mode to meet the no-deletion-before-load-confirmation constraint.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 06:00 billing batch deadline is tight given EMR Serverless startup. Airflow DAG should trigger SFTP polling at 01:00 to give 5 hours for ingestion, validation, and load of the prior day's files. Gap detection for missing intervals must run before the billing summary export step.

backend.service_design: Three-stage Spark pipeline: (1) Ingestor reads SFTP drop files and MDMS API responses from S3 staging into raw_readings staging table; (2) Validator applies anomaly rules and gap detection to emit validated_readings and validation_exceptions; (3) Loader bulk-inserts validated_readings into ClickHouse interval_readings table and writes billing summary to billing_exports
backend.business_modules: SFTPPoller (paramiko-based, polls 12 registered SFTP endpoints every 30 minutes, writes files to S3 staging with vendor prefix), MDMSFetcher (HTTP client for 3 MDMS API vendors, handles OAuth2 token refresh, paginates interval batches), FormatNormalizer (vendor-specific PSV/CSV parsers for top 5 formats, outputs canonical IntervalRecord schema), AnomalyDetector (zero read flag, stuck meter detection via identical consecutive reads, 3-sigma spike detection against 30-day rolling baseline), GapDetector (identifies missing 15-minute slots per meter per day, emits gap records to validation_exceptions), ClickHouseLoader (batch INSERT via clickhouse-connect, 100K row chunks)
backend.background_jobs: Airflow DAG 'meter_interval_ingestion' scheduled 01:00 UTC daily: poll_sftp >> poll_mdms >> spark_normalize_validate >> clickhouse_load >> billing_summary_export >> data_quality_report. Separate monthly DAG for ClickHouse partition archival to S3 Iceberg.
backend.failure_handling: S3 staging files renamed from .pending to .failed on Spark job exception; Airflow task retries 3 times with 5-minute backoff; PagerDuty alert if load not confirmed by 05:30; dead-letter S3 prefix for validation_exceptions requiring manual review

security.auth_design: EMR Serverless execution role with S3 read on staging bucket and ClickHouse VPC endpoint write; SFTPPoller ECS task role with S3 write on staging only; no cross-account access
security.privacy_controls: Customer account numbers tokenized via HMAC-SHA256 with rotating key before ClickHouse load; meter serial numbers masked in CloudWatch job logs; raw SFTP files retained in S3 with SSE-KMS under 7-year object lock
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Pipeline run audit records (job_id, file_count, record_count, validation_exception_count, load_confirmed_at) written to audit_runs table in PostgreSQL RDS; CloudTrail for S3 and IAM events

data.entities: meter_devices (meter_id, vendor_code, account_token, rate_class, install_date, status), interval_readings (meter_id, interval_timestamp, kwh_consumed, kw_demand, quality_flag ENUM validated/estimated/suspect, source_file_id, loaded_at), validation_exceptions (id, meter_id, interval_date, exception_type ENUM zero_read/spike/stuck/gap, detail, created_at), mdms_feeds (id, vendor_code, endpoint_url, last_polled_at, status), billing_exports (id, account_token, billing_date, total_kwh, interval_count, gap_count, export_file_s3_key), data_quality_reports (id, run_date, total_meters, validated_pct, exception_count_by_type_json)
data.storage_design: ClickHouse MergeTree table interval_readings partitioned by toYYYYMM(interval_timestamp) ORDER BY (meter_id, interval_timestamp); S3 staging bucket for raw files with SSE-KMS and object lock governance mode; S3 Iceberg for cold interval data beyond 3-year hot retention
data.retention_and_deletion: Raw SFTP files in S3: 7-year object lock. interval_readings ClickHouse: 3 years hot, then Iceberg archival TTL. validation_exceptions: 1 year. billing_exports: 7 years.
data.data_risks: Vendor PSV format changes without notice can silently corrupt normalization output; schema change detection via column count assertion in FormatNormalizer is minimum safeguard

devops.deployment_topology: SFTPPoller and MDMSFetcher as ECS Fargate tasks scheduled via EventBridge; Spark jobs submitted to EMR Serverless application via Airflow EMRServerlessOperator; ClickHouse on EC2 r6i.4xlarge in private subnet with NLB for internal access; Airflow on ECS Fargate with RDS PostgreSQL metadata store
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest for Spark transformation unit tests with PySpark local mode, Docker build for ECS tasks, EMR Serverless job script upload to S3 artifacts bucket, Airflow DAG lint and deploy via Astronomer CLI
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics from ECS tasks and Airflow; Grafana dashboards: daily meter coverage rate, validation exception rate by vendor, ClickHouse insert throughput, gap detection rate. PagerDuty: alert if load_confirmed_at is null by 05:30 UTC

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a batch data pipeline for 24 million daily smart meter interval records. Key decisions: Spark on EMR Serverless for distributed normalization and validation, ClickHouse MergeTree for time-series billing queries, S3 with object lock for raw file durability. Airflow DAG scheduled at 01:00 to meet 06:00 billing deadline. Customer account numbers tokenized before ClickHouse load for CCPA compliance. Plan quality is intentionally weak per manifest specification — core architecture is correct but several sections are thin on detail.
target_output.title: Smart Meter Interval Data Ingestion Pipeline Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A nightly batch data pipeline ingesting 15-minute interval readings from 500,000 smart meters via SFTP and MDMS APIs, validating and normalizing 24 million daily records with Spark on EMR Serverless, and loading clean data into a ClickHouse time-series warehouse for billing and analytics. Customer account numbers are tokenized before load for CCPA compliance. Pipeline must complete before the 06:00 billing batch window.
target_output.architecture_overview: The pipeline runs as a nightly Airflow DAG on ECS Fargate. SFTPPoller and MDMSFetcher tasks collect raw meter files to S3 staging. A Spark job on EMR Serverless normalizes and validates readings. Validated records are bulk-loaded into ClickHouse. Billing summary exports are generated for the billing system. S3 object lock ensures raw files cannot be deleted before load confirmation.
target_output.technology_stack: Processing: Python 3.12, Apache Spark 3.5 on EMR Serverless. Orchestration: Apache Airflow 2.8 on ECS Fargate. Data warehouse: ClickHouse 24.3 on EC2 r6i.4xlarge with clickhouse-connect Python client. Staging: S3 with SSE-KMS and object lock. Archival: S3 Apache Iceberg tables. Monitoring: Prometheus, Grafana. Alerting: PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: SFTP Polling: paramiko-based poller for 12 meter vendor SFTP endpoints writing files to S3 staging prefix per vendor. MDMS API Fetch: OAuth2-authenticated batch fetcher for 3 MDMS vendors. Format Normalization: vendor-specific PSV/CSV parsers for top 5 formats producing canonical IntervalRecord. Anomaly Detection: zero read, stuck meter, and 3-sigma spike flagging. Gap Detection: missing 15-minute slot identification per meter per day. ClickHouse Load: 100K-row bulk INSERT batches. Billing Summary Export: nightly account-level kWh totals with gap counts.
target_output.system_components: SFTPPoller (ECS Fargate task), MDMSFetcher (ECS Fargate task), Spark normalization and validation job (EMR Serverless), ClickHouseLoader module, Airflow DAG scheduler (ECS Fargate), S3 staging bucket, ClickHouse EC2 cluster, RDS PostgreSQL for Airflow metadata and audit_runs.
target_output.workflows: Nightly Ingestion: Airflow DAG triggers at 01:00 UTC. SFTPPoller fetches files from all 12 vendor endpoints to S3 staging. MDMSFetcher pulls prior-day interval batches from 3 MDMS APIs to S3. Spark job reads all staged files, applies FormatNormalizer per vendor prefix, runs AnomalyDetector and GapDetector, writes validated_readings and validation_exceptions partitions to S3. ClickHouseLoader bulk-inserts validated_readings in 100K-row batches into interval_readings table. BillingSummaryExporter aggregates per-account daily kWh and writes billing_exports records. Airflow marks load_confirmed_at in audit_runs. If load_confirmed_at is null by 05:30, PagerDuty fires. Validation Exception Review: Operations team queries validation_exceptions table for zero_read and spike anomalies by vendor and date; high exception rates trigger vendor escalation workflow outside the pipeline.
target_output.data_model: meter_devices (meter_id TEXT PK, vendor_code TEXT, account_token TEXT, rate_class TEXT, install_date DATE, status TEXT). interval_readings ClickHouse MergeTree (meter_id, interval_timestamp DateTime, kwh_consumed Float32, kw_demand Float32, quality_flag Enum8 validated/estimated/suspect, source_file_id TEXT, loaded_at DateTime) PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(interval_timestamp) ORDER BY (meter_id, interval_timestamp). validation_exceptions (id UUID, meter_id, interval_date DATE, exception_type Enum zero_read/spike/stuck/gap, detail TEXT, created_at). billing_exports (id UUID, account_token, billing_date DATE, total_kwh DECIMAL, interval_count INT, gap_count INT, export_file_s3_key). audit_runs (job_id UUID, run_date DATE, file_count INT, record_count BIGINT, validation_exception_count INT, load_confirmed_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: No external API — internal pipeline only. Internal interfaces: SFTPPoller writes to s3://meter-staging/{vendor_code}/{date}/*.psv. MDMSFetcher writes to s3://meter-staging/mdms/{vendor_code}/{date}/*.json. Spark reads s3://meter-staging/ prefix, writes to s3://meter-validated/ partitions. ClickHouseLoader reads s3://meter-validated/ and issues INSERT INTO interval_readings via clickhouse-connect. BillingSummaryExporter writes JSONL to s3://billing-exports/{date}/summary.jsonl for billing system pickup.
target_output.security_and_compliance: IAM roles scoped to minimum S3 prefixes for each pipeline component. S3 staging SSE-KMS encryption. ClickHouse connections TLS-only within VPC. Customer account numbers HMAC-SHA256 tokenized before ClickHouse load; raw account identifiers only in S3 raw files under 7-year object lock. CloudWatch job logs filtered to exclude meter serial numbers. CCPA data minimization: no customer name or address fields in ClickHouse interval_readings.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: pytest with PySpark local mode for transformation unit tests, Docker build and ECR push for ECS tasks, EMR Serverless job scripts uploaded to S3 artifacts bucket, Airflow DAG deploy via Astronomer CLI. ECS Fargate tasks for SFTPPoller and MDMSFetcher scheduled via EventBridge Rules at 01:00 UTC. EMR Serverless application pre-warmed via Airflow pre-task to reduce startup latency. ClickHouse EC2 cluster in private subnet with no public IP. RDS PostgreSQL for Airflow metadata in Multi-AZ configuration.
target_output.observability: Grafana dashboards on Prometheus: daily meter coverage rate (target >99.5%), validation exception rate by vendor (alert >2%), ClickHouse insert throughput (rows/sec), gap detection rate per vendor. Airflow task durations tracked for SLA monitoring. PagerDuty on-call alert if load_confirmed_at null by 05:30 UTC.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: EMR Serverless billed per vCPU-hour only during job execution; estimated 2 hours daily for 24M records at roughly $15/day. ClickHouse EC2 r6i.4xlarge at approximately $800/month. S3 storage grows at 50 GB/day for raw files. Total estimated $1,200/month at 500K meters.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): SFTP ingestion for top 5 vendor PSV formats, basic anomaly flagging, ClickHouse load, nightly billing summary. Phase 2 (4 weeks): MDMS API integration for 3 vendors, gap detection, data quality reporting dashboard in Grafana. Phase 3 (4 weeks): S3 Iceberg archival TTL, streaming AMI head-end path via Kinesis.
target_output.development_guidelines: PySpark transformations covered by pytest in local mode with sample meter data fixtures. Vendor format parsers versioned and tested independently. ClickHouse schema migrations applied via clickhouse-migrations before Airflow DAG deploy. No PII in any log output — enforced via Airflow log filter plugin.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: meter vendor PSV format changes without notice silently corrupt normalization. Mitigation: column count assertion in FormatNormalizer emits exception and halts vendor partition. Risk: EMR Serverless startup latency (60-90 seconds) compresses the 06:00 deadline buffer. Mitigation: pre-warm EMR application at 00:45 UTC via Airflow pre-task. Tradeoff: ClickHouse EC2 vs. managed ClickHouse Cloud — EC2 gives tuning control but requires manual cluster maintenance; reassess at 1M meters.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected ClickHouse MergeTree over TimescaleDB for better columnar compression on flat interval records. EMR Serverless chosen over EKS Spark for reduced cluster management overhead. S3 object lock governance mode selected to prevent accidental raw file deletion before load confirmation. HMAC-SHA256 account tokenization chosen over AES encryption for efficient join key generation in billing queries.

metadata.notes: Smart meter interval data pipeline; weak quality — correct architecture but several sections thin on detail per distribution spec.

profile.domain: Grid-Scale Energy Storage — Public Compliance Disclosure Landing Page

project_goal.value: Publish a compliance-focused public landing page for a grid-scale battery storage project developer, providing NERC CIP and interconnection filing transparency disclosures, downloadable environmental impact summaries, and a stakeholder inquiry form for community engagement
project_goal.rationale: Regulatory transparency obligations require public disclosure of interconnection filings and environmental summaries

target_users.value: Community stakeholders, regulatory reviewers, and environmental advocates seeking project disclosure documents and interconnection filing status
target_users.rationale: Primary audience identified in community engagement plan

project_class.rationale: Static content with no server-side runtime; S3 and CloudFront are sufficient

capabilities.rationale: Astro static site with Plausible Analytics; no backend or database

complexity_level.rationale: Static content delivery with form submission forwarding; no complex state

risk_level.rationale: No user authentication, no PII stored, no financial transactions; only public disclosure documents

data_sensitivity.rationale: All published content is public disclosure material; no private data processed

external_exposure.rationale: Public-facing disclosure site accessible by all stakeholders without authentication

access_model.value: Fully public read access; headless CMS author access restricted to project team via SSO
access_model.rationale: Disclosure content is public; authoring access controlled

feature_scope.value: Project disclosure summary page, NERC CIP filing documents index with version history, interconnection status tracker, environmental impact PDF downloads, stakeholder inquiry form with routing to compliance team inbox, headless CMS for content updates, Plausible Analytics dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Full compliance transparency feature set

mvp_scope.value: Project overview page, filing documents index, environmental impact PDF downloads, stakeholder inquiry form routing to email
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum required for regulatory transparency deadline

security_baseline.value: CloudFront HTTPS-only with TLS 1.2 minimum, CSP headers blocking inline scripts, no cookies except Plausible first-party analytics, form submissions forwarded via SES without storing PII
security_baseline.rationale: HTTPS and CSP are baseline for public sites; no PII retention for compliance simplicity

frontend_stack.value: Astro 4 with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS; content managed via Contentful headless CMS
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro generates zero-JS HTML for disclosure content pages; Contentful enables non-technical content updates

backend_stack.value: none — static site only; form submissions routed via AWS SES Lambda
backend_stack.rationale: No server-side runtime needed for static disclosure pages

data_platform.value: none — no database; PDF documents stored in S3 and linked via CloudFront signed URLs for large files
data_platform.rationale: Static site requires no database

hosting_target.value: AWS S3 static hosting behind CloudFront with custom domain and ACM certificate
hosting_target.rationale: S3 plus CloudFront is the standard static site hosting pattern on AWS

privacy_retention_policy.value: No PII stored by the site; stakeholder inquiry form data forwarded via SES and immediately discarded; Plausible Analytics is cookieless and GDPR-compliant

future_scope.value: Project timeline interactive visualization, multi-language support for Spanish-speaking communities, email notification list for filing update alerts
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 community engagement enhancements

constraints.value: Site must rebuild and redeploy within 10 minutes of a CMS content publish; PDF documents up to 50 MB must be downloadable; no server-side runtime on S3/CloudFront hosting

observability_baseline.value: Plausible Analytics for page views and document download tracking; CloudFront access logs to S3 for audit; CloudWatch alarm on 5xx error rate
observability_baseline.rationale: Lightweight analytics sufficient for a static disclosure site

execution_preference.value: Content updates via headless CMS triggering automated rebuild; code changes via PR with GitHub Actions deploy
execution_preference.rationale: Non-technical compliance staff must be able to publish without code deployments

project.goal: NERC CIP and interconnection filing transparency disclosure site for battery storage developer
project.target_users: Community stakeholders and regulatory reviewers

frontend.cms: Contentful headless CMS for filing document content

security.headers: CSP blocking inline scripts, X-Frame-Options DENY
security.form: SES Lambda forwarding, no PII storage

devops.rebuild: Contentful webhook triggers GitHub Actions rebuild within 10 minutes

constraints.hosting: S3 static only, no server runtime

reasoner_reviews.product: The three-round revision history reflects work to correctly establish the no-server-runtime constraint for landing_page class. Round 1 incorrectly included an Express backend; round 2 removed it but left a database reference; round 3 should fully align with static-only architecture throughout all sections.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro 4 with static output mode is the correct choice for compliance disclosure pages. Contentful webhook triggers a GitHub Actions rebuild completing in 3-4 minutes for a sub-10-minute CMS-to-live SLA. Large PDF documents (up to 50 MB) served via CloudFront with appropriate Cache-Control headers will download reliably without pre-signed URL complexity since the content is public.
reasoner_reviews.security: For a fully public site with no auth, the security controls are CSP headers, HTTPS enforcement, and form submission forwarding without storage. CloudFront Origin Shield can be added to reduce S3 origin requests. The SES Lambda for form forwarding should validate honeypot field to reduce spam without adding CAPTCHA friction.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: No server-side runtime on S3/CloudFront is a hard constraint for landing_page class. Any dynamic behavior must be handled at build time or via client-side JavaScript calling external services. The Contentful webhook rebuild pipeline must be tested to confirm 10-minute SLA under realistic CMS publish scenarios.

backend.service_design: No persistent backend service. A single AWS Lambda function handles stakeholder inquiry form POST submissions: validates honeypot field, sanitizes inputs, forwards to compliance team SES email address, returns 200. Lambda invoked via CloudFront Function URL or API Gateway HTTP endpoint embedded in Astro form action.
backend.failure_handling: Lambda form submission failures return a generic error message to submitter; compliance team notified via CloudWatch alarm on Lambda error rate; no retry queue needed given low form submission volume
backend.backend_risks: Contentful webhook delivery failures can delay content updates beyond 10-minute SLA; GitHub Actions should expose a manual re-trigger button in the Actions UI as fallback

security.auth_design: No user authentication on public site. Contentful author access managed via Contentful SSO with SAML integration to project developer corporate IdP.
security.abuse_prevention: Stakeholder inquiry form protected by honeypot hidden field; CloudFront WAF rate limit rule 100 requests per 5 minutes per IP to prevent form spam; no CAPTCHA to avoid friction for community stakeholders
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudFront access logs shipped to S3 for 90-day retention; Lambda invocation logs to CloudWatch Logs 30-day retention; Plausible Analytics document download events for public transparency reporting

data.entities: project_disclosures (CMS content type: title, filing_type ENUM nerc_cip/interconnection/environmental, publication_date, document_s3_key, version, summary_text), filing_documents (CMS content type: filing_reference, document_url, status ENUM filed/approved/superseded, effective_date), stakeholder_inquiries (forwarded via SES only, not stored)
data.storage_design: Contentful as headless CMS for all structured content; PDF documents in S3 bucket linked as CloudFront URLs in Contentful document_url fields; no relational or document database deployed
data.retention_and_deletion: Published disclosure content retained indefinitely in Contentful and S3; CloudFront logs retained 90 days; Lambda logs 30 days

devops.deployment_topology: S3 bucket configured as static website origin behind CloudFront distribution with ACM certificate and custom domain; Astro build artifacts uploaded to S3 via GitHub Actions aws-actions/s3-sync-action; CloudFront cache invalidated on each deploy
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow triggered on: (a) push to main branch, (b) Contentful webhook POST to GitHub Actions dispatch endpoint. Steps: bun install, bun run build (Astro static), s3 sync to S3 bucket, CloudFront invalidation. Target: <4 minute build-to-live.
devops.observability_stack: Plausible Analytics script in Astro layout head; CloudFront access logs to S3; CloudWatch alarm on CloudFront 5xx rate >1%; Lambda error alarm for form submission failures

frontend.app_structure: Astro 4 static site with Contentful as headless CMS; compliance document pages generated at build time from Contentful entries; ISR not applicable (static only)
frontend.pages_and_flows: Project Disclosure Summary, NERC CIP Filing Documents Index (versioned), Environmental Impact Summaries, Stakeholder Contact
frontend.accessibility_notes: Regulatory documents must have descriptive download link text including document title, version, and file size; avoid icon-only download buttons
frontend.performance_notes: Contentful content fetched at build time; no client-side CMS calls; documents served from CDN with long cache TTLs; build triggered on Contentful publish webhook

ISS-003.detail: Round 2 plan referenced pre-signed S3 URLs for PDF downloads but the site is fully public with no authentication, making pre-signed URLs unnecessarily complex. CloudFront direct delivery is simpler and consistent with public static hosting.

focus_issues.detail: Round 2 used pre-signed S3 URLs unnecessarily; site is public, CloudFront direct delivery is simpler and correct.
