previous_plan.title: Battery Storage Project Compliance Disclosure Site Architecture v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Static Astro 4 landing page hosted on S3 plus CloudFront serving NERC CIP and interconnection filing disclosures. Contentful headless CMS for content updates. Lambda for stakeholder inquiry form forwarding via SES. Plausible Analytics for cookieless page tracking.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Astro 4 static site built via GitHub Actions on Contentful webhook and push to main, deployed to S3 CloudFront. Lambda handles form POST submissions forwarding to SES. No persistent backend server.
previous_plan.data_model: project_disclosures and filing_documents as Contentful content types. PostgreSQL RDS stores stakeholder inquiries. (Note: ISS-002 — PostgreSQL reference is incorrect for static site.)
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: CloudFront HTTPS-only. CSP headers. Honeypot spam protection on form. No user authentication.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 resolves the remaining ISS-003: replacing unnecessary pre-signed S3 URLs with direct CloudFront delivery for public PDF documents. The site is fully public with no auth, so pre-signed URLs add complexity without benefit. CloudFront with appropriate Cache-Control headers handles 50 MB PDF downloads correctly. Also ensuring data_model describes only Contentful CMS content types with no deployed database (resolving ISS-002 carry-forward). Architecture is otherwise sound from round 2 and ready to converge.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced pre-signed S3 URL approach with direct CloudFront delivery for all public PDF documents. S3 bucket policy grants CloudFront OAC read access only; PDFs served via CloudFront with Cache-Control: max-age=86400 header for efficient CDN caching. No pre-signed URL generation needed since all disclosure documents are public.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Public PDF downloads up to 50 MB served reliably via CloudFront edge without authentication overhead; ISS-003 resolved

fix_report.action_taken: Removed all remaining PostgreSQL RDS references from data_model section. Data model now describes only Contentful CMS content types (project_disclosures, filing_documents) and confirms no database is deployed.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Plan fully consistent with static landing_page architecture; no database deployed; ISS-002 resolved

target_output.title: Battery Storage Project Compliance Disclosure Site Architecture v3
target_output.executive_summary: A fully static Astro 4 landing page hosted on AWS S3 with CloudFront CDN serving NERC CIP and interconnection filing transparency disclosures, downloadable environmental impact PDFs, and a stakeholder inquiry form for a grid-scale battery storage project developer. Contentful headless CMS enables compliance staff to publish updated filing documents without code deployments, triggering an automated rebuild that goes live within 4 minutes. All content is public; no user authentication, no database, no server-side runtime. Plausible Analytics provides cookieless page and download tracking.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static architecture: Astro 4 built with output:'static' generating zero-JS HTML pages with Tailwind CSS styling. GitHub Actions builds and deploys to an S3 bucket origin behind a CloudFront distribution with ACM TLS certificate and custom domain. Contentful headless CMS stores project disclosure metadata and filing document links; a Contentful webhook fires a GitHub Actions repository_dispatch event on content publish, triggering a rebuild that completes in under 4 minutes. PDF documents up to 50 MB hosted in a separate S3 bucket with CloudFront Origin Access Control; served directly via CloudFront edge nodes with Cache-Control: max-age=86400 — no pre-signed URLs needed since all disclosure content is public. A single AWS Lambda function handles stakeholder inquiry form POST submissions: validates honeypot field, forwards sanitized content to compliance team via SES, and returns a confirmation response. No server-side runtime on S3 or CloudFront.
target_output.technology_stack: Static site: Astro 4.x with TypeScript, output mode static, Tailwind CSS 3. CMS: Contentful headless CMS with Contentful SDK for build-time content fetch. Hosting: AWS S3 static website origin, CloudFront with ACM certificate, CloudFront Origin Access Control for PDF S3 bucket. Form handler: AWS Lambda Node.js 20 with SES SDK v3. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with aws-actions/s3-sync-action and aws-actions/cloudfront-invalidation. Analytics: Plausible Analytics (cookieless, self-hosted script). Build: Bun 1.3 as package manager and build runner.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Project Disclosure Summary: Astro page rendering Contentful project_disclosure entries with filing type badges (NERC CIP, Interconnection, Environmental). Filing Documents Index: chronological list of filing_document entries with status badges (Filed, Approved, Superseded) and direct CloudFront PDF download links. Environmental Impact Downloads: large PDF documents served via CloudFront with progress download support via range requests. Interconnection Status Tracker: Contentful content type showing current interconnection queue position and key milestone dates. Stakeholder Inquiry Form: client-side form submitting to Lambda endpoint with honeypot spam protection and SES email routing to compliance inbox. Plausible Analytics: tracks page views and PDF download click events without cookies.
target_output.system_components: S3 Static Bucket: Astro build output (HTML, CSS, JS, images). CloudFront Distribution: serves static site with HTTPS-only, custom security headers via CloudFront Response Headers Policy (CSP, X-Frame-Options DENY, Strict-Transport-Security). S3 PDF Documents Bucket: environmental impact PDFs and filing documents; accessible only via CloudFront OAC, no public S3 ACLs. Lambda Form Handler: Node.js 20, invoked via CloudFront Function URL, validates honeypot, forwards to SES. Contentful CMS: project_disclosures and filing_documents content types managed by compliance staff. GitHub Actions CI/CD: triggered by push to main and Contentful webhook; builds Astro, syncs to S3, invalidates CloudFront.
target_output.workflows: Content Publish Workflow: Compliance staff edits a filing_document entry in Contentful (e.g. updates interconnection status to Approved, attaches new PDF). Contentful fires a webhook to a GitHub Actions repository_dispatch endpoint. GitHub Actions workflow triggers: bun install, bun run build (Astro fetches Contentful content at build time via SDK), s3 sync static output to S3 static bucket, CloudFront cache invalidation for /* paths. New content is live within 4 minutes. Stakeholder Inquiry Workflow: Community member completes the inquiry form on the site, including a honeypot hidden field that bots fill. On submit, a fetch POST is sent to the Lambda Function URL. Lambda validates honeypot is empty, sanitizes text inputs, constructs SES SendEmail request to compliance-inquiries@developer.com with submitter name and message, returns 200 JSON confirmation. No inquiry data stored anywhere. PDF Download Workflow: Stakeholder clicks a filing document download link rendered from a Contentful document_url field pointing to the CloudFront PDF distribution domain. CloudFront serves the PDF from the documents S3 bucket via Origin Access Control; Cache-Control: max-age=86400 allows edge caching for popular documents; no authentication check performed since all disclosures are public.
target_output.data_model: All content managed in Contentful CMS — no database deployed. project_disclosures Contentful content type: title (Text), filing_type (Symbol ENUM nerc_cip/interconnection/environmental), project_name (Text), publication_date (Date), summary_text (RichText), status (Symbol). filing_documents Contentful content type: filing_reference (Text), filing_type (Symbol), document_cloudfront_url (Text, URL to PDF in documents CloudFront distribution), version (Text), status (Symbol ENUM filed/approved/superseded), effective_date (Date), linked_disclosure (Reference to project_disclosures). impact_summaries Contentful content type: document_title (Text), document_cloudfront_url (Text), published_date (Date), document_size_mb (Number). No stakeholder_inquiries stored — forwarded via SES only.
target_output.api_design: Static site has no REST API. Single Lambda endpoint: POST https://form.{domain}/inquire — CloudFront Function URL. Request body: {name: string, organization: string, message: string, _honeypot: string (must be empty)}. Response: {success: true, message: 'Your inquiry has been forwarded to the project compliance team.'} or {success: false} on Lambda error. Contentful Content Delivery API called at Astro build time via Contentful SDK to fetch all project_disclosures and filing_documents entries. GitHub Actions dispatch endpoint: POST https://api.github.com/repos/{org}/{repo}/dispatches with event_type:'contentful_publish' from Contentful webhook.
target_output.security_and_compliance: CloudFront enforces HTTPS-only redirect for all HTTP requests. TLS 1.2 minimum via CloudFront security policy. CloudFront Response Headers Policy applies: Content-Security-Policy blocking unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval, X-Frame-Options DENY, Strict-Transport-Security with max-age 31536000, X-Content-Type-Options nosniff. PDF S3 bucket uses CloudFront Origin Access Control with no public S3 ACLs; bucket policy allows only CloudFront service principal. Lambda form handler validates honeypot field and rate-limited by CloudFront WAF (100 requests per 5 minutes per IP). No cookies set except Plausible first-party analytics script. No PII stored anywhere on the platform; SES email delivery is fire-and-forget. Contentful API token stored in GitHub Actions encrypted secrets; never exposed in built static assets.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions workflow (deploy.yml): triggered on push to main or repository_dispatch event_type:'contentful_publish'. Steps: checkout, setup-bun, bun install, bun run build (CONTENTFUL_SPACE_ID and CONTENTFUL_ACCESS_TOKEN from GitHub Actions secrets injected at build time), aws-actions/s3-sync-action to S3 static bucket with --delete flag, aws-actions/cloudfront-invalidation for /* paths. Build completes in 2-3 minutes; CloudFront propagation adds 1 minute; total CMS-to-live under 4 minutes against 10-minute SLA. Lambda form handler deployed separately via SAM CLI; infrequently updated. S3 PDF documents bucket managed independently by compliance staff uploading via AWS Console or S3 sync; Contentful document_cloudfront_url fields updated to reflect new document paths.
target_output.observability: Plausible Analytics (cookieless, GDPR-compliant script) tracks: page views per disclosure page, PDF download click events via outbound link tracking, inquiry form submission events. CloudFront access logs shipped to S3 for 90-day retention; athena queries available for traffic analysis. CloudWatch alarm on CloudFront 5xx error rate exceeding 1% for 5 minutes notifies ops via SNS email. Lambda error alarm on >3 errors in 10 minutes for form submission failures.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AWS S3 and CloudFront costs are minimal for a static site: estimated $10-30/month for typical disclosure site traffic. Lambda form handler invocations negligible cost at low inquiry volume. Contentful free tier covers project team usage. Plausible Analytics self-hosted or $9/month SaaS. CloudFront scales automatically to any traffic spike from regulatory review periods without configuration changes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (2 weeks): Project overview page, filing documents index with Contentful content, environmental impact PDF downloads via CloudFront, stakeholder inquiry form with Lambda SES handler, GitHub Actions CI/CD with Contentful webhook rebuild, CloudFront CSP headers. Phase 2 (2 weeks): Interconnection status tracker with milestone dates, Plausible Analytics integration, CloudFront WAF rate limiting rule. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Multi-language support for Spanish content via Astro i18n, email notification list for filing update alerts via Mailchimp embed, interactive project timeline visualization.
target_output.development_guidelines: Astro configured with output:'static' to ensure no server-side rendering routes are accidentally introduced. TypeScript strict mode. Contentful content type schemas documented in repo /docs/contentful-schema.md and kept in sync with CMS. GitHub Actions secrets used for CONTENTFUL_SPACE_ID and CONTENTFUL_ACCESS_TOKEN; never committed. PDF document URLs in Contentful always point to the CloudFront documents distribution domain, not S3 directly. Lambda form handler deployed independently with its own package.json; changes require SAM deploy not Astro rebuild.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Contentful webhook delivery failure causes content updates to miss the 10-minute SLA. Mitigation: GitHub Actions workflow also triggerable manually via Actions UI; compliance staff informed of manual trigger procedure. Risk: Large PDF file (50 MB) caching on CloudFront edge increases first-request latency for less popular documents. Mitigation: Cache-Control max-age=86400 ensures subsequent requests from same edge node are fast; CloudFront range request support enables browser partial downloads. Tradeoff: Contentful SaaS CMS dependency vs. self-hosted CMS — Contentful free tier is acceptable for project scale; self-hosted headless CMS would add infra management complexity without benefit.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Pre-signed S3 URLs replaced with direct CloudFront delivery for public documents, resolving ISS-003. PostgreSQL database reference fully removed from data_model, resolving ISS-002 carry-forward. Contentful webhook GitHub Actions dispatch confirmed as rebuild trigger meeting 10-minute SLA. CloudFront Origin Access Control confirmed as secure mechanism for PDF bucket access without public S3 ACLs.

metadata.notes: Static landing page for battery storage compliance disclosures; round 3 resolves PDF delivery mechanism and removes residual database reference from prior rounds.

profile.domain: Utility Compliance — NERC CIP Evidence Management Desktop Application

project_goal.value: Replace spreadsheet-based NERC CIP compliance evidence management with a structured desktop application enabling compliance officers to track, collect, and report on all CIP reliability standards requirements across bulk electric system assets with defensible audit trails
project_goal.rationale: Spreadsheet workflows create audit gaps; structured evidence management is required for NERC CIP-002 through CIP-014 compliance

target_users.value: NERC CIP compliance officers, compliance managers, and internal auditors at large utilities operating bulk electric system assets
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in compliance department stakeholder interviews

project_class.rationale: Evidence artifacts and compliance data classified as confidential; on-premises desktop deployment required by security policy

capabilities.rationale: Electron UI, embedded Spring Boot backend, PostgreSQL persistence, Keycloak auth, and admin panel for user and asset management

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-standard CIP requirement hierarchy, evidence artifact versioning, audit cycle management, and gap analysis reporting require domain-specific complexity

risk_level.rationale: CIP compliance data and evidence artifacts are confidential; audit trail integrity is legally defensible and must be tamper-evident

data_sensitivity.rationale: CIP compliance posture, asset classifications, and evidence artifacts are confidential under NERC CIP-011 information protection

external_exposure.rationale: Application runs on on-premises server accessible only via corporate LAN; no internet connectivity

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC with roles: compliance_officer (assign tasks, upload evidence), compliance_manager (approve satisfaction, generate reports), auditor (read-only access to all evidence), admin (user and BES asset management)
access_model.rationale: NERC CIP audit requires role separation between evidence uploaders and approvers

feature_scope.value: CIP requirements hierarchy browser (CIP-002 through CIP-014), BES asset catalog with CIP classification, compliance task assignment and tracking, evidence artifact upload and versioning, requirement satisfaction sign-off workflow, gap analysis report, pre-audit evidence package export, audit cycle management, admin panel for users and assets
feature_scope.rationale: Full CIP compliance lifecycle management replacing spreadsheets

mvp_scope.value: CIP-007 and CIP-010 requirements hierarchy, BES asset catalog, task assignment, evidence upload with versioning, compliance manager sign-off, gap analysis report
mvp_scope.rationale: CIP-007 patch management and CIP-010 configuration management are the most evidence-intensive standards for the upcoming audit cycle

security_baseline.value: Keycloak SSO with MFA, PostgreSQL at-rest encryption via LUKS on server disk, TLS 1.3 between Electron client and embedded Spring Boot API, tamper-evident audit log with HMAC chain, evidence artifact checksums for integrity verification
security_baseline.rationale: Defensible audit trail integrity is a legal requirement for NERC CIP compliance programs

frontend_stack.value: Electron 30 with Angular 17 and TypeScript; Angular Material for compliance dashboard components
frontend_stack.rationale: Team has Angular expertise; Angular Material provides accessible data table and form components for compliance workflows

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot 3.2, Spring Security, and Spring Data JPA on embedded Tomcat
backend_stack.rationale: Spring Boot provides a mature embedded server suitable for on-premises desktop deployment; Java team has existing expertise

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on on-premises server with LUKS disk encryption
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for CIP requirement hierarchy and evidence relationships; on-premises for data residency

hosting_target.value: On-premises Windows Server 2022 in secure operations center; Electron client distributed to compliance workstations via corporate software deployment
hosting_target.rationale: CIP-011 information protection requires on-premises data storage

privacy_retention_policy.value: CIP evidence artifacts retained for 3 years per NERC CIP audit retention requirement; audit_log table append-only and retained indefinitely; evidence exports encrypted at rest
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: NERC CIP requires 3-year evidence retention for audit defensibility

future_scope.value: Full CIP-002 through CIP-014 coverage, integration with CMDB for automated BES asset import, automated vulnerability scan result ingestion for CIP-007 patch evidence
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 expansion after MVP validation with compliance team

constraints.value: Must run on Windows Server 2022 on-premises; no cloud connectivity; evidence packages must be exportable as encrypted ZIP for submission to NERC auditors; application must support 50 concurrent compliance officer users
constraints.rationale: On-premises security policy and auditor submission format requirements

observability_baseline.value: New Relic APM for Spring Boot performance monitoring on on-premises server; structured JSON logging via Logback; compliance dashboard showing overdue task counts and open gap counts per CIP standard
observability_baseline.rationale: On-premises New Relic agent for application performance; compliance dashboard for program health visibility

execution_preference.value: Iterative delivery with compliance officer UAT after each sprint; compliance manager reviews evidence workflow before each phase release
execution_preference.rationale: Domain expert validation required for compliance workflow correctness

compliance_context.value: NERC CIP-002 through CIP-014 reliability standards compliance; FERC Order 706 implementation; SOC 2 Type II for internal audit integrity controls; CIP-011 information protection for confidential data
compliance_context.rationale: Core regulatory obligations driving the application's existence

project.goal: Structured NERC CIP evidence management replacing spreadsheets for bulk electric system compliance
project.target_users: CIP compliance officers and managers at large utilities
project.timeline: MVP for CIP-007 and CIP-010 before upcoming audit cycle

backend.framework: Spring Boot 3.2 with Spring Security and Spring Data JPA

security.encryption: TLS 1.3 client-to-server, LUKS disk encryption for PostgreSQL

data.file_storage: Server filesystem for evidence artifacts with checksum verification

devops.packaging: Electron installer for workstations, Spring Boot JAR for server

constraints.export: Encrypted ZIP for NERC auditor submission

reasoner_reviews.product: Three rounds of revision have progressively improved the plan. The HMAC-chained audit log and evidence artifact versioning are correctly specified. Round 3 should focus on the remaining medium issue: the evidence package export format needs to specify how the encrypted ZIP is structured for NERC auditor consumption, including the manifest format and encryption key exchange mechanism.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Spring Boot 3.2 embedded on-premises is the correct choice for 50 concurrent users on a single server. The HMAC-chained audit_log with SHA-256 hash linking between records is the right pattern for tamper-evidence. The evidence package export should produce a structured ZIP with a manifest.json listing each artifact with its SHA-256 checksum, encrypted with AES-256-GCM using a key derived from a compliance manager-specified passphrase, enabling auditors to verify artifact integrity after decryption.
reasoner_reviews.security: Keycloak MFA is correctly specified. The evidence artifact checksum verification on upload (SHA-256 stored alongside the artifact path) enables integrity checks. The encrypted ZIP export for NERC auditors must specify the key exchange mechanism — a passphrase-derived key communicated out-of-band is the appropriate approach for on-premises deployments without PKI infrastructure.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 50 concurrent users on a single Windows Server 2022 is feasible for a Spring Boot application with PostgreSQL connection pool sized to 20 connections. The encrypted ZIP export must complete within a reasonable time for evidence packages containing up to 500 artifacts; streaming ZIP construction with on-the-fly AES-256-GCM encryption avoids loading all artifacts into memory.

backend.service_design: Spring Boot 3.2 application with domain modules: requirements (CIP standard hierarchy), assets (BES asset catalog), tasks (compliance task lifecycle), evidence (artifact upload and versioning), reports (gap analysis and audit package export), admin (user and role management)
backend.api_patterns: RESTful API at /api/v1 with Spring Security JWT bearer token from Keycloak; OpenAPI 3.0 spec generated via springdoc-openapi. Evidence artifact upload via multipart POST with SHA-256 checksum header verification.
backend.business_modules: RequirementsService (CIP-002 through CIP-014 hierarchy, part and sub-requirement loading), BESAssetService (asset catalog CRUD with CIP classification), ComplianceTaskService (task assignment, status transitions, due date management), EvidenceArtifactService (multipart upload, SHA-256 checksum storage, version history), GapAnalysisService (identifies requirements with no satisfied evidence within audit cycle window), AuditPackageExportService (streaming encrypted ZIP with manifest.json, AES-256-GCM key derived from compliance manager passphrase via PBKDF2)
backend.failure_handling: Evidence upload checksum mismatch returns 400 with artifact rejected; database transaction rollback on partial evidence submission; Spring Boot global exception handler with structured error response
backend.backend_risks: 50 concurrent users with evidence artifact uploads can saturate server disk I/O; artifact storage path on server filesystem must be on a separate NFS mount from the OS volume to prevent disk exhaustion affecting PostgreSQL

frontend.app_structure: Electron 30 shell loading Angular 17 SPA from localhost Spring Boot server on startup; Angular lazy-loaded feature modules: requirements, assets, tasks, evidence, reports, admin
frontend.pages_and_flows: Requirements Browser (tree view of CIP-002 through CIP-014 hierarchy with satisfaction status badges), BES Asset Catalog (asset list with CIP classification and linked requirements), Task Board (Kanban-style task tracker by CIP standard and assignee), Evidence Vault (per-requirement artifact list with version history and checksum display), Gap Analysis Dashboard (chart of unsatisfied requirements by standard and asset class), Audit Package Export (wizard for cycle selection, artifact scope, and passphrase entry), Admin Panel (user management, BES asset import, audit cycle configuration)
frontend.state_management: NgRx store for: current audit cycle, selected CIP standard filter, task board state, evidence vault file upload progress. Angular signals for local component state in forms.
frontend.accessibility_notes: Angular Material ARIA roles for compliance data tables; keyboard navigation for task assignment workflows; high-contrast mode for compliance officers working extended hours

security.auth_design: Keycloak 23 on-premises OIDC provider; Electron app opens Keycloak login page in BrowserWindow; JWT access token stored in Electron session secure storage; Spring Boot Spring Security validates JWT signature against Keycloak JWKS endpoint on LAN
security.authorization_model: Spring Security method-level @PreAuthorize annotations for RBAC: compliance_officer can upload evidence and complete tasks; compliance_manager can approve satisfaction and export audit packages; auditor role gets read-only @GetMapping endpoints only; admin role accesses /api/v1/admin/* namespace
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Append-only audit_log table: each record contains (id, prev_hash, action_type, actor_id, entity_type, entity_id, detail_json, timestamp, hmac_sha256). HMAC computed over (prev_hash || action_type || actor_id || entity_id || timestamp) with server-side secret key. Tamper detection: periodic background job recomputes HMAC chain and alerts on mismatch.
security.security_risks: Keycloak on-premises LDAP sync with Active Directory must be configured for the 50-user scope; AD group mapping to Keycloak roles must be tested before go-live to avoid compliance officers being locked out during audit cycle

data.entities: cip_requirements (id, standard_id TEXT CIP-007, part TEXT 7.1, sub_part TEXT, title, description, applicability_category ENUM high/medium/low/transient, parent_id FK), bes_assets (id, asset_name, asset_type ENUM relay/firewall/historian/workstation, cip_classification ENUM high/medium/low, site_id, responsible_team), compliance_tasks (id, requirement_id FK, bes_asset_id FK, assigned_to FK users, due_date DATE, status ENUM open/in_progress/pending_review/satisfied/not_applicable, audit_cycle_id FK), evidence_artifacts (id, task_id FK, filename, file_path TEXT, file_size_bytes BIGINT, sha256_checksum TEXT, version INT, uploaded_by FK users, uploaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, notes), audit_cycles (id, cycle_name, start_date DATE, end_date DATE, cip_standards TEXT[], status ENUM active/closed/exported), findings_reports (id, audit_cycle_id FK, requirement_id FK, finding_type ENUM gap/open_task/missing_evidence, detail, severity ENUM critical/major/minor, created_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with connection pool sized to 20 connections for 50 concurrent users. Evidence artifact files stored on server filesystem NFS mount /cip-evidence/{audit_cycle_id}/{requirement_id}/{artifact_id}/; file paths stored in evidence_artifacts.file_path. Database and NFS mount on LUKS-encrypted volumes.
data.retention_and_deletion: evidence_artifacts and audit_cycles retained 3 years per NERC CIP. audit_log table append-only, no deletion. Closed audit cycles archived to a compressed PostgreSQL dump on LUKS-encrypted backup tape.
data.migration_notes: Flyway for PostgreSQL schema migrations; migration scripts executed on Spring Boot startup; baseline migration imports CIP-007 and CIP-010 requirement hierarchy from static JSON seed data

devops.deployment_topology: Spring Boot JAR deployed as Windows Service on Windows Server 2022 via WinSW wrapper; Keycloak 23 deployed as Windows Service on same server or dedicated server; PostgreSQL 16 as Windows Service; Electron installer distributed to compliance workstations via SCCM
devops.ci_cd_design: Jenkins on-premises pipeline: Maven build, JUnit integration tests against PostgreSQL test container, Karma Angular unit tests, electron-builder installer generation, deployment to staging server via SSH, smoke test suite via Playwright headless
devops.rollback_strategy: Previous Spring Boot JAR retained on server; WinSW service restart pointing to prior JAR version; Flyway migration rollback scripts provided for each migration; Electron installer previous version retained in SCCM
devops.observability_stack: New Relic Java agent attached to Spring Boot JVM; Logback JSON structured logs to rolling file appender; compliance health dashboard Angular component polling /api/v1/reports/health for overdue task count, open gap count, audit log chain validity status

ISS-001.title: Audit log HMAC chain mechanism not specified in round 1
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan mentioned tamper-evident audit log but did not specify the HMAC computation formula or chain linkage mechanism, making the implementation underspecified for a legally defensible audit trail.

history.note: Raised by security reviewer in round 1 audit
HMAC-SHA256 chain with prev_hash linkage specified in round 2

ISS-002.title: Evidence artifact file storage path on OS volume risks disk exhaustion
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 and 2 plans stored evidence artifacts on the same filesystem volume as the OS and PostgreSQL data files. Large artifact uploads could exhaust disk and corrupt the database.

history.note: Raised by devops reviewer
Not addressed in round 2
NFS mount on separate LUKS volume specified for artifact storage

ISS-003.title: Encrypted audit package export format and key exchange not specified
ISS-003.detail: Round 2 plan mentioned encrypted ZIP export for NERC auditors but did not specify the ZIP internal structure, manifest format, encryption algorithm, or key exchange mechanism.

history.note: Identified by architect reviewer in round 2 audit

focus_issues.title: Encrypted audit package export format and key exchange not specified
focus_issues.detail: Round 2 mentioned encrypted ZIP but did not define structure, manifest format, or key exchange mechanism for NERC auditor submission.

latest_recommendations: Specify audit package ZIP internal structure with manifest.json listing each artifact with SHA-256 checksum
Define AES-256-GCM encryption with PBKDF2 passphrase derivation for auditor key exchange
Document out-of-band passphrase communication procedure

previous_plan.title: NERC CIP Compliance Evidence Management Desktop Application Architecture v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Electron 30 Angular 17 desktop application for NERC CIP compliance evidence management. Spring Boot 3.2 embedded backend with PostgreSQL 16 on-premises. Keycloak RBAC with MFA. HMAC-SHA256 chained audit log for tamper-evident trail. Evidence artifacts on separate NFS LUKS-encrypted volume.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Keycloak 23 MFA. TLS 1.3 client-server. HMAC-SHA256 chain on audit_log with prev_hash linkage. Evidence artifact checksum verification on upload. Encrypted ZIP export for NERC auditors (format TBD).
previous_plan.data_model: cip_requirements, bes_assets, compliance_tasks, evidence_artifacts, audit_cycles, findings_reports, audit_log in PostgreSQL 16.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: Spring Boot JAR as Windows Service. Jenkins CI. Electron SCCM distribution. Evidence on NFS LUKS mount separate from OS volume.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 resolves ISS-003 by fully specifying the audit package export format: a structured ZIP with a manifest.json and per-standard folder hierarchy, encrypted with AES-256-GCM using a PBKDF2 passphrase-derived key. The streaming ZIP construction in AuditPackageExportService prevents memory pressure for large evidence packages. The out-of-band passphrase communication via secure email is the appropriate key exchange for on-premises deployments without auditor PKI. All other aspects of the architecture are mature from round 2 and carry forward.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined audit package ZIP internal structure: top-level manifest.json listing each artifact with {requirement_id, part, bes_asset_id, task_id, filename, sha256_checksum, version, satisfied_at}; artifacts organized in subdirectories {standard}/{part}/{asset_id}/{version}_{filename}. Encryption: AES-256-GCM with key derived via PBKDF2-SHA256 (100,000 iterations, random 16-byte salt stored in manifest). Passphrase set by compliance manager in export wizard; communicated to NERC auditors via encrypted email out-of-band. AuditPackageExportService streams ZIP construction to avoid loading all artifacts into memory.
fix_report.expected_outcome: NERC auditors receive a self-contained encrypted ZIP with verifiable artifact integrity; ISS-003 resolved

target_output.title: NERC CIP Compliance Evidence Management Desktop Application Architecture v3
target_output.executive_summary: A desktop application replacing spreadsheet-based NERC CIP compliance evidence management, built with Electron 30 and Angular 17 on the frontend and Java 21 Spring Boot 3.2 as an embedded on-premises backend, with PostgreSQL 16 on Windows Server 2022. Compliance officers assign CIP-007 and CIP-010 tasks to responsible individuals, collect evidence artifacts (configuration exports, patch screenshots, policy documents) with SHA-256 checksum integrity verification, and route for compliance manager sign-off. An HMAC-SHA256 chained audit log provides a tamper-evident record of all compliance actions. Pre-audit evidence packages are exported as structured AES-256-GCM encrypted ZIP files with manifest integrity verification for NERC auditor submission.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier on-premises desktop architecture: Electron 30 Angular 17 SPA client communicates over TLS 1.3 to a Spring Boot 3.2 embedded Tomcat server running as a Windows Service on a secure operations center server. Spring Boot backend manages the CIP requirements hierarchy, BES asset catalog, compliance task lifecycle, evidence artifact versioning, and audit package export. PostgreSQL 16 stores all compliance data on a LUKS-encrypted volume. Evidence artifact files stored on a separate NFS LUKS-encrypted mount (/cip-evidence/) to isolate disk usage from the database volume. Keycloak 23 on-premises OIDC provider handles authentication with MFA and RBAC role enforcement. A tamper-evident audit_log table with HMAC-SHA256 chaining records every compliance action. No internet connectivity; all components communicate on corporate LAN only.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 30, Angular 17, TypeScript strict mode, Angular Material, NgRx store. Backend: Java 21, Spring Boot 3.2, Spring Security 6, Spring Data JPA, Hibernate 6, springdoc-openapi. Auth: Keycloak 23 on-premises OIDC. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with Flyway migrations. Audit encryption: Java AES-256-GCM, PBKDF2-SHA256 for passphrase key derivation. File storage: server NFS mount with SHA-256 checksum verification. CI: Jenkins on-premises. Testing: JUnit 5, Testcontainers PostgreSQL, Karma Angular. APM: New Relic Java agent. Logging: Logback JSON structured.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Requirements Browser: tree navigation of CIP-002 through CIP-014 standard, part, and sub-requirement hierarchy with satisfaction status badge per BES asset class. BES Asset Catalog: asset CRUD with CIP high/medium/low classification and linked applicable requirements. Task Board: compliance task assignment to responsible individuals with due dates, status transitions (open, in_progress, pending_review, satisfied, not_applicable), and overdue alerts. Evidence Vault: per-task artifact upload with SHA-256 checksum verification, version history, and reviewer notes. Satisfaction Workflow: compliance manager reviews pending_review tasks, examines linked artifacts, approves satisfaction or returns with feedback. Gap Analysis Dashboard: chart of unsatisfied requirements by CIP standard and asset classification within active audit cycle. Audit Package Export: wizard selecting audit cycle, CIP standards scope, and compliance manager passphrase; produces AES-256-GCM encrypted ZIP with manifest.json and structured artifact hierarchy for NERC auditor submission. Admin Panel: user management, Keycloak role assignment, BES asset import, audit cycle configuration.
target_output.system_components: Electron 30 Client: Angular SPA renderer loading from localhost:8443 Spring Boot server; Keycloak OIDC login in BrowserWindow; JWT stored in Electron secure session storage; IPC bridge for file save dialog on audit package export. Spring Boot 3.2 Server (Windows Service): RequirementsController, BESAssetController, ComplianceTaskController, EvidenceArtifactController (multipart upload with SHA-256 verification), AuditPackageExportController (streaming encrypted ZIP), ReportsController, AdminController. Spring Security JWT filter validating Keycloak-issued tokens against JWKS endpoint. PostgreSQL 16: cip_requirements, bes_assets, compliance_tasks, evidence_artifacts, audit_cycles, findings_reports, audit_log tables on LUKS-encrypted volume. NFS Evidence Mount: /cip-evidence/ on LUKS-encrypted separate volume storing raw artifact files. Keycloak 23 (Windows Service): OIDC provider with AD LDAP sync for 50 compliance users. New Relic Java Agent: attached to Spring Boot JVM for APM.
target_output.workflows: Evidence Collection and Sign-off: Compliance officer opens Task Board, selects an open CIP-007 Part 7.1 task for a specific firewall asset, transitions to in_progress. Uploads a configuration export screenshot via Evidence Vault multipart POST; Spring Boot verifies SHA-256 checksum matches client-computed hash, stores file to NFS mount, records evidence_artifacts row with version 1. Officer adds review note and transitions task to pending_review. Compliance manager opens pending_review queue, inspects artifact and notes, confirms satisfaction, transitions task to satisfied. audit_log records each status transition with HMAC chain. Audit Package Export: Compliance manager launches export wizard, selects the CIP-007/CIP-010 audit cycle, scope of BES assets, and enters a passphrase. AuditPackageExportService queries all satisfied tasks and linked evidence_artifacts for the cycle. Streams ZIP construction: writes manifest.json with {artifacts:[{requirement_id, part, bes_asset_id, task_id, filename, sha256_checksum, version, satisfied_at}], export_timestamp, salt_hex}; organizes artifact files into subdirectories CIP-007/7.1/{asset_id}/v1_{filename}. Encrypts ZIP with AES-256-GCM using PBKDF2-SHA256 key (100,000 iterations, random salt). Electron file-save dialog saves encrypted .cipzip file to compliance manager's workstation. Passphrase communicated to NERC auditors via encrypted email out-of-band. Gap Analysis: ReportsService queries compliance_tasks for the active audit cycle, joins with cip_requirements to find requirements with no satisfied task for applicable asset classes, groups by standard and severity, returns findings_reports records displayed in Gap Analysis Dashboard.
target_output.data_model: cip_requirements (id UUID PK, standard_id TEXT e.g. 'CIP-007', part TEXT e.g. '7.1', sub_part TEXT NULLABLE, title TEXT, description TEXT, applicability_category TEXT CHECK IN ('high','medium','low','transient'), parent_id FK NULLABLE). bes_assets (id UUID PK, asset_name TEXT, asset_type TEXT CHECK IN ('relay','firewall','historian','workstation','server'), cip_classification TEXT CHECK IN ('high','medium','low'), site_id TEXT, responsible_team TEXT). compliance_tasks (id UUID PK, requirement_id FK, bes_asset_id FK, assigned_to FK users.id, due_date DATE, status TEXT CHECK IN ('open','in_progress','pending_review','satisfied','not_applicable'), approved_by FK users.id NULLABLE, audit_cycle_id FK, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). evidence_artifacts (id UUID PK, task_id FK, filename TEXT, file_path TEXT, file_size_bytes BIGINT, sha256_checksum TEXT, version INT DEFAULT 1, uploaded_by FK, uploaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, notes TEXT). audit_cycles (id UUID PK, cycle_name TEXT, start_date DATE, end_date DATE, cip_standards TEXT[], status TEXT CHECK IN ('active','closed','exported')). findings_reports (id UUID PK, audit_cycle_id FK, requirement_id FK, finding_type TEXT CHECK IN ('gap','open_task','missing_evidence'), detail TEXT, severity TEXT CHECK IN ('critical','major','minor'), created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_log (id BIGSERIAL PK, prev_hash TEXT, action_type TEXT, actor_id UUID FK, entity_type TEXT, entity_id UUID, detail_json JSONB, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ, hmac_sha256 TEXT).
target_output.api_design: Spring Boot REST at /api/v1, secured with Keycloak JWT, OpenAPI 3.0 via springdoc. Key endpoints: GET /api/v1/requirements?standard=CIP-007 (requirements tree), GET /api/v1/assets (BES asset list with classification), POST /api/v1/tasks (assign task), PATCH /api/v1/tasks/{id}/status (transition status), POST /api/v1/evidence (multipart upload with X-SHA256-Checksum header), GET /api/v1/evidence/{id}/versions (version history), PATCH /api/v1/tasks/{id}/approve (compliance_manager only), GET /api/v1/reports/gap-analysis?cycle_id={id} (gap report), POST /api/v1/export/audit-package (streaming download: request body {cycle_id, standards:['CIP-007','CIP-010'], passphrase}, returns application/octet-stream .cipzip), GET /api/v1/reports/health (overdue tasks, open gaps, audit_log chain validity). All endpoints @PreAuthorize role-checked.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Keycloak 23 on-premises OIDC with MFA enforced for all roles; AD LDAP sync maps Active Directory groups to Keycloak compliance_officer, compliance_manager, auditor, admin roles. Spring Security 6 JWT bearer token filter validates signature against Keycloak JWKS on LAN; @PreAuthorize method-level RBAC for all controllers. TLS 1.3 between Electron client and Spring Boot server with server certificate from internal PKI. Evidence artifact integrity: SHA-256 checksum computed client-side before upload, verified server-side on receipt; mismatch returns 400. Audit log HMAC-SHA256 chain: each record's HMAC computed over (prev_hash || action_type || actor_id || entity_id || timestamp) with server-side HMAC secret stored in Keycloak service account credentials; background job verifies chain integrity hourly and alerts on tampering. Audit package AES-256-GCM encryption: PBKDF2-SHA256 with 100,000 iterations and random 16-byte salt derives the AES-256 key from the compliance manager passphrase; salt embedded in manifest.json for auditor decryption; passphrase communicated via encrypted email out-of-band. NERC CIP-011 information protection: PostgreSQL and NFS volumes on LUKS-encrypted disks; no compliance data in application logs.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Jenkins on-premises pipeline: Maven build, JUnit 5 integration tests with Testcontainers PostgreSQL, Karma Angular unit tests, Flyway migration validation, Spring Boot JAR artifact, Electron installer via electron-builder. Deployment: Spring Boot JAR deployed as WinSW Windows Service on Windows Server 2022; Keycloak 23 as Windows Service; PostgreSQL 16 as Windows Service. Flyway migrations execute on Spring Boot startup with checksum validation. Electron installer distributed to 50 compliance workstations via SCCM. Staging: a parallel Windows Server 2022 instance with anonymized CIP data copy; Playwright smoke tests verify task assignment and evidence upload workflows. Rollback: prior Spring Boot JAR retained; WinSW service restart pointing to prior version; Flyway rollback scripts provided for each migration. Backup: nightly PostgreSQL pg_dump to LUKS-encrypted tape; NFS evidence mount mirrored nightly to backup NAS.
target_output.observability: New Relic Java agent on Spring Boot JVM: transaction traces for evidence upload and audit package export endpoints; alert on p99 latency >5s for export. Logback JSON structured logs to rolling file appender (/var/log/cip-app/app.log) with 90-day retention; log fields include correlation_id, actor_id (no PII), action, entity_id. Compliance health dashboard: Angular component polling /api/v1/reports/health every 5 minutes displaying overdue_task_count by CIP standard, open_gap_count, audit_log_chain_valid boolean. Chain validity alert: if background integrity job detects HMAC chain break, an Angular banner displays 'Audit log integrity alert — contact security officer' and sends email to compliance manager.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: On-premises hosting with existing Windows Server hardware; no cloud cost. Jenkins CI on existing CI server. New Relic APM on-premises agent license at approximately $200/month for one host. Evidence artifact storage estimated at 500 GB/year for 50 officers uploading configuration exports; NFS mount sized to 2 TB with annual review. Scaling path: if user count exceeds 50, PostgreSQL connection pool increased and additional application server instances added behind an internal load balancer.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): CIP-007 and CIP-010 requirements hierarchy seed data, BES asset catalog, task assignment and status workflow, evidence artifact upload with SHA-256 checksum, compliance manager satisfaction sign-off, HMAC-chained audit log, gap analysis report, audit package encrypted ZIP export with manifest.json and AES-256-GCM, Keycloak MFA setup, Jenkins CI, SCCM Electron distribution. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Full CIP-002 through CIP-014 requirements coverage, audit cycle management, Gap Analysis Dashboard charts, admin panel for user and asset management, Playwright E2E test suite. Phase 3 (6 weeks): CMDB integration for automated BES asset import, automated vulnerability scan result ingestion for CIP-007 patch evidence, multi-cycle comparison reporting.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 with Records and sealed interfaces for domain value objects. Spring Boot @Transactional on all service methods modifying compliance state. Flyway migration scripts mandatory for every schema change; no DDL outside migrations. Evidence artifact upload multipart handler must verify SHA-256 checksum before persisting file to NFS mount — never persist then verify. HMAC secret rotated annually; rotation procedure documented in ops runbook and tested in staging. Audit package passphrase never logged or stored; AuditPackageExportService must clear the char[] passphrase after key derivation. JUnit integration tests use Testcontainers PostgreSQL to run against a real database. Angular strict mode; NgRx effects for all API calls to enable consistent loading and error state handling.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Keycloak on-premises AD LDAP sync misconfiguration during initial setup locks compliance officers out of the application during an active audit cycle. Mitigation: test AD group-to-role mapping in staging with production AD groups at least 2 weeks before go-live. Risk: Large audit package exports (500 artifacts, multi-GB) cause Java heap pressure during ZIP streaming. Mitigation: AuditPackageExportService uses streaming ZIP construction reading artifacts from NFS in 64 KB chunks, never loading full files into heap; Spring Boot response streaming with Transfer-Encoding: chunked. Risk: HMAC chain break detection may produce false positives if the server clock is adjusted during a time sync event. Mitigation: HMAC computation uses database-generated TIMESTAMPTZ, not system clock, for deterministic input. Tradeoff: embedded on-premises Spring Boot vs. cloud-hosted API — on-premises required by CIP-011 information protection and data residency constraints; cloud hosting would simplify operations but is not permitted.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Audit package ZIP structure defined: manifest.json with artifact list and PBKDF2 salt, artifacts in {standard}/{part}/{asset_id}/{version}_{filename} subdirectories. AES-256-GCM with PBKDF2-SHA256 100,000 iterations selected for key derivation. Passphrase exchange: out-of-band encrypted email to NERC auditors. streaming ZIP construction confirmed to avoid heap pressure for large evidence packages. HMAC secret storage: Keycloak service account credentials vault selected to avoid hardcoded secrets in Spring Boot configuration.

metadata.notes: NERC CIP evidence management desktop app; round 3 specifies encrypted audit package ZIP format with manifest.json and AES-256-GCM PBKDF2 key derivation for NERC auditor submission.

profile.domain: Energy Markets — Real-Time Locational Marginal Pricing Data API

project_goal.value: Deliver a high-throughput API service providing sub-second real-time locational marginal pricing data to energy trading platforms, aggregators, and automated bidding systems, ingesting 5-minute LMP publications from multiple ISO/RTO OASIS feeds and serving thousands of concurrent polling clients
project_goal.rationale: Energy trading clients require sub-second LMP data latency for algorithmic bidding; current polling delay causes missed dispatch signals

target_users.value: Energy trading desks, automated bidding systems, demand response aggregators, and load-serving entities polling or subscribing to real-time and day-ahead LMP data
target_users.rationale: Primary subscriber base identified in market data service sales pipeline

project_class.rationale: Pure data distribution service with no user-facing UI; exposes REST and WebSocket endpoints only

capabilities.rationale: Rust Axum API service, TimescaleDB for time-series persistence, custom JWT auth, partner-facing public API, WebSocket push for realtime subscribers

complexity_level.rationale: Thousands of concurrent polling clients, sub-second latency requirement, 5-minute ISO feed ingestion cycle, and per-client rate limiting at scale

risk_level.rationale: Financial market data with contractual latency SLAs; unauthorized data access or service disruption has direct financial consequences for subscribers

data_sensitivity.rationale: LMP pricing data is financial market data subject to FERC market data access rules and subscriber licensing agreements

external_exposure.rationale: API accessible only to credentialed trading partners and energy market participants; no anonymous public access

access_model.value: Custom JWT bearer tokens with per-client scopes; subscribers provisioned via admin API with rate_limit_quota, allowed_markets, and subscription_tier (real_time vs day_ahead only)
access_model.rationale: Per-client scope enforcement needed for licensing compliance; different market access tiers by contract

feature_scope.value: 5-minute LMP ingestion from PJM, CAISO, MISO, and NYISO OASIS feeds; per-pricing-node REST snapshot endpoint; WebSocket push stream for real-time subscribers; day-ahead LMP historical query with time range; per-client rate limiting; subscriber provisioning admin API; per-node anomaly detection for erroneous price spikes
feature_scope.rationale: Full market data distribution feature set for energy trading clients

mvp_scope.value: PJM and CAISO OASIS feed ingestion, per-pricing-node REST snapshot, Redis cache serving, per-client JWT rate limiting, basic WebSocket push stream
mvp_scope.rationale: PJM and CAISO represent 70% of target subscriber demand

security_baseline.value: mTLS between ISO feed ingestion workers and internal API, JWT bearer tokens for partner API access, TLS 1.3 on all partner-facing endpoints, per-client rate limiting enforced at API layer, no LMP data in error logs
security_baseline.rationale: Partner-facing financial API requires mTLS for internal components and strong auth for subscribers

frontend_stack.value: none — API service with no UI
frontend_stack.rationale: api_service class has no frontend

backend_stack.value: Rust with Axum 0.7, Tokio async runtime, sqlx for TimescaleDB queries, redis-rs for cache layer
backend_stack.rationale: Rust Axum provides sub-millisecond request processing needed for sub-second LMP latency SLA at thousands of concurrent connections

data_platform.value: TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL 16 extension) for time-series LMP records with hypertable partitioning; Redis 7 for hot LMP snapshot cache serving real-time polling
data_platform.rationale: TimescaleDB hypertables give optimized time-range queries for day-ahead lookback; Redis cache serves sub-millisecond snapshot reads without DB hits

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 with ALB for API traffic; ElastiCache Redis cluster; RDS TimescaleDB on db.r6g.2xlarge
hosting_target.rationale: Proximity to PJM and CAISO OASIS feed sources in us-east-1; managed ECS for autoscaling

privacy_retention_policy.value: LMP price records retained 2 years in TimescaleDB hypertable; older data compressed via TimescaleDB compression policy; no subscriber PII in LMP tables; API access logs retained 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: 2-year LMP retention supports day-ahead analysis and regulatory lookback requirements

future_scope.value: MISO and NYISO feed ingestion, FTR auction result data, congestion component breakdown per pricing node, graphql subscription interface
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 market expansion after PJM/CAISO MVP

constraints.value: API p99 latency under 100ms for snapshot endpoints; WebSocket push delivery within 500ms of ISO publication; support 5,000 concurrent subscriber connections; deploy in us-east-1 for ISO feed proximity
constraints.rationale: Contractual SLAs with trading clients; ISO OASIS feed latency constraints

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with custom LMP latency and feed ingestion lag metrics; PagerDuty alert on p99 >100ms or feed ingestion gap >6 minutes
observability_baseline.rationale: Trading clients have contractual SLA credits; ops must detect feed gaps before subscribers notice

execution_preference.value: Continuous deployment with canary releases; load test each release against 5,000 concurrent simulated subscribers before production promotion
execution_preference.rationale: High-scale service requires pre-production load validation

compliance_context.value: FERC market data access rules for LMP distribution licensing; SOC 2 Type II for data integrity and availability controls; subscriber license agreements enforcing per-market access scopes
compliance_context.rationale: Financial market data regulatory obligations and subscriber contract enforcement

project.goal: Sub-second LMP data API for energy trading clients polling PJM and CAISO pricing nodes
project.target_users: Trading desks and automated bidding systems
project.timeline: MVP for PJM and CAISO within 3 months

security.transport: TLS 1.3 partner-facing, mTLS internal feed ingestion

data.cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 for hot LMP snapshots

devops.load_test: k6 with 5,000 concurrent simulated subscribers pre-production

reasoner_reviews.product: Round 2 must address the critical per-client rate limiting precision issue identified in round 1: token bucket state in Redis must use atomic Lua scripts to prevent race conditions under 5,000 concurrent clients. The WebSocket connection management for push delivery also needs a specified fan-out mechanism for delivering LMP updates to all subscribers for a given pricing node simultaneously.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Rust Axum with Tokio is the correct stack for 5,000 concurrent WebSocket connections and sub-100ms snapshot latency. The rate limiting fix requires a Redis Lua script implementing a sliding window counter (not a simple INCR) to correctly enforce per-client request budgets under concurrent polling bursts. WebSocket fan-out should use a Redis Pub/Sub channel per pricing node that the Axum WebSocket handler subscribes to; this decouples the ISO ingestion worker from client connection management.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS between ISO feed ingestion workers and the internal LMP write API is correctly specified. JWT scope enforcement per pricing node access (e.g. 'markets:pjm:*' scope) should be validated on every request path — the round 1 plan only mentioned JWT authentication without detailing scope-level enforcement per market.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 500ms WebSocket push delivery SLA is achievable with Redis Pub/Sub fan-out if the ingestion worker publishes to the Redis channel within 100ms of receiving the ISO OASIS feed response. The remaining 400ms budget covers WebSocket frame serialization and network delivery to trading clients in US East data centers.

backend.service_design: Two-crate Rust workspace: lmp-api (Axum server handling REST snapshot, WebSocket push, and admin endpoints) and lmp-ingestor (async ISO feed poller and Redis/TimescaleDB writer). Shared lmp-core library for LMP domain types, pricing node registry, and JWT validation logic.
backend.api_patterns: Axum router with tower-http CORS and trace middleware; JWT extractor via custom tower middleware validating HS256 signature and market scope claims; per-client rate limiter as Axum layer using Redis Lua sliding window script; WebSocket upgrade handler using axum::extract::WebSocketUpgrade.
backend.business_modules: ISOFeedPoller (reqwest async HTTP client fetching PJM and CAISO OASIS XML endpoints on 5-minute schedule via tokio::time::interval), OASISParser (nom parser for LMP XML; extracts pricing_node, interval_ending, lmp_total, lmp_congestion, lmp_loss per interval), LMPWriter (Redis SET with TTL for hot snapshot, sqlx bulk INSERT into TimescaleDB lmp_records hypertable, Redis PUBLISH to 'lmp:{pricing_node_id}' channel), SnapshotHandler (Redis GET for pricing node snapshot; falls back to TimescaleDB query on cache miss), WebSocketHandler (subscribes to Redis Pub/Sub channel for requested pricing nodes, streams LMP frames to connected subscriber), RateLimiter (Redis Lua sliding window script per api_key:endpoint:minute bucket)
backend.failure_handling: ISO feed HTTP failures: exponential backoff with 3 retries; if feed unavailable >6 minutes, PagerDuty alert and stale_feed flag set in Redis; snapshot endpoint returns last known value with stale flag in response header X-LMP-Stale: true. TimescaleDB write failures: LMP records buffered in-memory ring buffer (max 1000 records) and retried; alert if buffer fills.
backend.scaling_notes: ECS Fargate autoscaling on CPU 60% target; Axum server handles 5,000 WebSocket connections per task instance via Tokio async; target 3 tasks minimum, scale to 10 under load. ElastiCache Redis cluster mode with 3 shards for rate limiting state distribution.
backend.backend_risks: ISO OASIS feed format changes (PJM has changed XML schema twice in 5 years) can silently produce zero LMP records; schema hash comparison on each ingest cycle detects format drift

security.auth_design: JWT HS256 tokens issued by admin provisioning API; claims: {sub: client_id, markets: ['pjm', 'caiso'], tier: 'real_time', rate_limit_rps: 100, exp: ...}. Axum JWT middleware validates signature and extracts claims; SnapshotHandler rejects requests for markets not in JWT markets claim with 403.
security.authorization_model: Per-request market scope enforcement: GET /v1/lmp/pjm/{node_id} requires 'pjm' in JWT markets claim. WebSocket subscription to CAISO nodes requires 'caiso' in claims. Admin endpoints require admin JWT with separate signing key.
security.abuse_prevention: Redis Lua sliding window rate limiter: lua script atomically reads and increments request counter with 60-second TTL per api_key bucket; returns remaining quota in X-RateLimit-Remaining header; 429 on exhaustion.
security.security_risks: JWT secret rotation requires coordinated client re-authentication; implement key_id header and support two concurrent valid secrets during rotation window

data.entities: pricing_nodes (id UUID PK, node_name TEXT e.g. 'PJM.AEP-GEN', iso_code TEXT CHECK IN ('pjm','caiso','miso','nyiso'), node_type TEXT CHECK IN ('hub','zone','generator','load'), latitude NUMERIC, longitude NUMERIC). lmp_records TimescaleDB hypertable (id BIGSERIAL, pricing_node_id UUID FK, interval_ending TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL, lmp_total NUMERIC(10,2), lmp_congestion NUMERIC(10,2), lmp_loss NUMERIC(10,2), source_feed TEXT, ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ). api_subscribers (id UUID PK, client_name TEXT, api_key TEXT UNIQUE, markets TEXT[], tier TEXT, rate_limit_rps INT, active BOOL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). rate_limit_quotas (id UUID PK, subscriber_id FK, window_start TIMESTAMPTZ, request_count INT). iso_feeds (id UUID PK, iso_code TEXT, feed_url TEXT, last_polled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_successful_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status TEXT CHECK IN ('healthy','stale','error'))
data.storage_design: TimescaleDB hypertable on lmp_records partitioned by interval_ending (chunk_time_interval 1 day); continuous aggregate view lmp_hourly_avg pre-computes hourly averages for day-ahead lookback queries. Redis ElastiCache: key pattern 'lmp:snapshot:{pricing_node_id}' with 10-minute TTL for hot snapshot; Pub/Sub channel 'lmp:pub:{pricing_node_id}' for WebSocket fan-out.
data.retention_and_deletion: lmp_records: 2-year hot retention in TimescaleDB; TimescaleDB compression policy activates after 7 days for older chunks. api_subscribers and rate_limit_quotas: retained indefinitely. API access logs in CloudWatch: 90 days.
data.data_risks: TimescaleDB hypertable chunk management can cause query plan regressions after chunk boundary crossings; continuous aggregates must be refreshed within 15 minutes of new data ingestion to avoid stale day-ahead lookback results

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster: lmp-api service (3-10 tasks autoscaled) behind ALB with TLS termination; lmp-ingestor service (1 task, no autoscaling needed for 4 ISO feeds). ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode (3 shards). RDS TimescaleDB db.r6g.2xlarge Multi-AZ. All in VPC private subnets; ALB in public subnet.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: cargo build --release, cargo test, Docker build (distroless Rust image), ECR push, ECS rolling deploy with minimum healthy percent 100. k6 load test suite (5,000 virtual users, 60-second ramp, snapshot and WebSocket scenarios) runs against staging before production promotion.
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS previous task definition retained; rollback via ecs:update-service pointing to prior task revision. TimescaleDB migrations via sqlx-migrate; rollback scripts mandatory for each migration.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with Rust tracing integration; custom metrics: lmp_snapshot_latency_p99, feed_ingestion_lag_seconds, active_websocket_connections, rate_limit_rejections_per_minute. PagerDuty: p99 latency >100ms for 2 minutes or feed lag >6 minutes.

ISS-001.title: Rate limiting uses non-atomic Redis INCR causing race condition under concurrent polling bursts
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan specified per-client rate limiting with a Redis INCR counter, which is not atomic under concurrent requests from a single client polling at high frequency. Multiple requests can bypass the limit before the counter is enforced, violating subscriber license agreements.

history.note: Identified by security reviewer in round 1 audit

ISS-002.title: WebSocket fan-out mechanism for LMP updates not specified
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan mentioned WebSocket push delivery but did not specify how LMP updates are fanned out to all subscribers watching a given pricing node. Without a pub/sub mechanism, each WebSocket handler would need to poll independently, adding latency and duplicate DB load.

history.note: Raised by architect reviewer in round 1

focus_issues.title: Rate limiting uses non-atomic Redis INCR causing race condition under concurrent polling bursts
focus_issues.detail: Non-atomic INCR allows concurrent requests to bypass per-client rate limit, violating subscriber license agreements.

focus_issues.title: WebSocket fan-out mechanism not specified
focus_issues.detail: No pub/sub mechanism specified for delivering LMP updates to multiple concurrent WebSocket subscribers for the same pricing node.

latest_recommendations: Replace Redis INCR rate limiter with atomic Lua sliding window script to prevent concurrent bypass
Specify Redis Pub/Sub channel per pricing node for WebSocket fan-out; decouple ingestion worker from subscriber connection management
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Real-Time LMP Data API Architecture v1

convergence_state.reason: High severity rate limiting race condition and medium fan-out gap both unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Solid Rust Axum architecture with correct TimescaleDB and Redis technology choices, but the rate limiting implementation is critically underspecified — Redis INCR is non-atomic under concurrent polling bursts — and the WebSocket fan-out mechanism is absent.
strengths: Rust Axum on Tokio is the correct stack for 5,000 concurrent WebSocket connections
TimescaleDB hypertable partitioning correctly applied for LMP time-series
Redis snapshot cache correctly specified with pricing node key pattern
concerns: Redis INCR rate limiter is not atomic; concurrent requests from high-frequency polling clients can bypass the limit
WebSocket push delivery has no specified fan-out mechanism; polling from TimescaleDB per subscriber defeats the realtime SLA
blocking_issues: Rate limiter must use atomic Lua script to enforce per-client limits correctly under concurrent load
recommendations: Implement Redis Lua sliding window script atomically incrementing and checking the counter in a single round-trip
Add Redis Pub/Sub channel per pricing node ('lmp:pub:{node_id}') for WebSocket fan-out; ingestor publishes on write, WS handler subscribes

issue_updates.title: Rate limiting race condition
issue_updates.detail: INCR-based rate limiter non-atomic under concurrent requests

issue_updates.title: WebSocket fan-out not specified
issue_updates.detail: WS handler had no pub/sub mechanism for multi-subscriber fan-out

previous_plan.title: Real-Time LMP Data API Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Rust Axum API service delivering real-time PJM and CAISO LMP snapshots. TimescaleDB hypertable for time-series. Redis cache for hot snapshots. Custom JWT auth with per-client rate limiting via Redis INCR. WebSocket push endpoint for real-time subscribers.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: ECS Fargate Axum service with TimescaleDB on RDS and ElastiCache Redis. ISO feed ingestor polls PJM/CAISO OASIS every 5 minutes. Rate limiting via Redis INCR (ISS-001: non-atomic). WebSocket handler per subscriber (ISS-002: no fan-out specified).
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: JWT HS256 with market scope claims. mTLS internal feed ingestion. TLS 1.3 partner-facing. Rate limiting per client.
previous_plan.data_model: pricing_nodes, lmp_records hypertable, api_subscribers, iso_feeds in TimescaleDB.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses both focus issues: ISS-001 requires replacing the non-atomic Redis INCR rate limiter with a Lua sliding window script that atomically reads, increments, and enforces the per-client budget in a single Redis round-trip. ISS-002 requires adding a Redis Pub/Sub channel per pricing node as the fan-out mechanism for WebSocket push delivery, decoupling the ISO ingestor from subscriber connection management and enabling the 500ms delivery SLA. Both fixes integrate cleanly with the Rust Axum/Tokio architecture.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced Redis INCR rate limiter with a Lua sliding window script. The Lua script atomically: reads the current counter for 'ratelimit:{api_key}:{minute}', increments by 1, sets TTL to 60 seconds on first increment, returns (current_count, limit). Axum RateLimiter middleware calls redis.eval() with this script; rejects with 429 if current_count exceeds the JWT rate_limit_rps claim. Single Redis round-trip eliminates the TOCTOU race condition.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Per-client rate limits atomically enforced under concurrent polling bursts; ISS-001 resolved

fix_report.action_taken: Added Redis Pub/Sub channel per pricing node ('lmp:pub:{pricing_node_id}') as the WebSocket fan-out mechanism. LMPWriter in lmp-ingestor publishes a JSON LMP frame to this channel immediately after writing the Redis snapshot. Axum WebSocketHandler subscribes to the channel for each pricing node in the client's subscription list using a dedicated redis-rs async PubSub connection; forwards frames to the WebSocket sink. Fan-out is O(1) from ingestor perspective regardless of subscriber count.
fix_report.expected_outcome: LMP updates fanned out to all subscribers for a pricing node within 100ms of ingestor publication; 500ms WebSocket delivery SLA met; ISS-002 resolved

target_output.title: Real-Time LMP Data API Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A high-throughput Rust Axum API service serving sub-second real-time locational marginal pricing data to energy trading clients. An async ingestor polls PJM and CAISO OASIS XML feeds every 5 minutes, writes LMP snapshots to ElastiCache Redis, and publishes to per-node Redis Pub/Sub channels that fan out to WebSocket subscribers. Thousands of concurrent trading clients poll REST snapshot endpoints served from Redis cache with p99 latency under 100ms. Per-client rate limiting enforced via atomic Redis Lua sliding window scripts. TimescaleDB hypertable provides 2-year LMP time-series for day-ahead lookback queries.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-service Rust workspace on ECS Fargate: lmp-api handles REST snapshot queries, WebSocket subscriptions, and admin provisioning; lmp-ingestor polls ISO OASIS feeds and writes to Redis and TimescaleDB. LMP update flow: lmp-ingestor fetches PJM or CAISO OASIS XML endpoint, parses with nom, writes snapshot to Redis key 'lmp:snapshot:{pricing_node_id}' with 10-minute TTL, bulk-inserts to TimescaleDB lmp_records hypertable, and publishes a JSON LMP frame to Redis Pub/Sub channel 'lmp:pub:{pricing_node_id}'. REST snapshot: Axum SnapshotHandler reads from Redis; cache miss falls back to TimescaleDB. WebSocket: AxumWebSocketHandler subscribes to Redis Pub/Sub channels for the client's requested pricing nodes and streams updates to the subscriber. Rate limiting: Axum RateLimiter tower layer executes Lua sliding window script per request. All partner-facing endpoints on TLS 1.3; mTLS between lmp-ingestor and internal write path.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Rust edition 2021. Web framework: Axum 0.7 on Tokio 1.x async runtime. HTTP client: reqwest 0.12 for ISO OASIS polling. XML parsing: nom 7 for OASIS LMP XML. Database client: sqlx 0.7 with TimescaleDB PostgreSQL driver. Cache/pub-sub: redis-rs 0.25 async with Pub/Sub support. JWT: jsonwebtoken 9 crate for HS256. Containerization: Docker distroless Rust image. Orchestration: ECS Fargate autoscaling. Time-series DB: TimescaleDB 2.x on RDS PostgreSQL 16 db.r6g.2xlarge Multi-AZ. Cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode 3 shards. APM: Datadog tracing with tracing-subscriber. CI: GitHub Actions with cargo-deny and k6 load tests.
target_output.functional_feature_map: LMP Snapshot REST: GET /v1/lmp/{iso}/{pricing_node} returns latest LMP with lmp_total, lmp_congestion, lmp_loss, interval_ending, and X-LMP-Stale header if ISO feed is >6 minutes stale. WebSocket Push: WS /v1/lmp/stream with subscribe message listing pricing node IDs; server pushes JSON frames on each 5-minute update. Day-Ahead Historical: GET /v1/lmp/{iso}/{pricing_node}/history?from={ts}&to={ts} queries TimescaleDB hypertable with time range. Subscriber Provisioning: POST /admin/subscribers creates api_key JWT with market scopes, tier, and rate limit. ISO Feed Status: GET /admin/feeds returns per-ISO ingestion health with last_successful_at and status. Rate Limiting: per-client sliding window enforced on all data endpoints; 429 with Retry-After header on exhaustion.
target_output.system_components: lmp-ingestor (ECS Fargate, 1 task): ISOFeedPoller (tokio::time::interval 5-min tick per ISO), OASISParser (nom XML parser for PJM and CAISO LMP element schema), LMPWriter (redis-rs SET snapshot + PUBLISH to pub channel + sqlx bulk INSERT to hypertable). lmp-api (ECS Fargate, 3-10 tasks autoscaled): Axum router with tower-http middleware stack (trace, cors, compression); JWT extractor middleware; RateLimiter layer (redis-rs eval Lua sliding window script); SnapshotHandler (redis GET with sqlx fallback); WebSocketHandler (redis-rs PubSub async subscriber per connection, tokio::select! on WS sink and Redis channel); AdminHandler (subscriber provisioning); HistoryHandler (sqlx time-range query on hypertable). ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster: snapshot keys, rate limit buckets, Pub/Sub broker. TimescaleDB RDS: lmp_records hypertable, pricing_nodes, api_subscribers, iso_feeds tables. ALB: TLS 1.3 termination for partner-facing traffic.
target_output.workflows: 5-Minute LMP Ingestion: tokio::time::interval fires for each ISO. ISOFeedPoller sends GET to PJM OASIS LMP endpoint (https://dataminer2.pjm.com/feed/rt_unverified_fivemin_hrl_lmps). OASISParser extracts Vec<LmpRecord> from XML response. LMPWriter: for each record, redis SET 'lmp:snapshot:{node_id}' serialized JSON with EX 600; redis PUBLISH 'lmp:pub:{node_id}' JSON frame; bulk sqlx INSERT into lmp_records hypertable (1,000-row batches). Updates iso_feeds.last_successful_at. Total ingest-to-Redis-publish target: under 100ms. REST Snapshot: Trading client GET /v1/lmp/pjm/PJM.AEP-GEN with JWT bearer. RateLimiter Axum layer calls redis EVAL Lua script: atomically checks and increments 'ratelimit:{api_key}:{unix_minute}' counter; rejects 429 if over limit. JWT middleware validates HS256 signature and confirms 'pjm' in markets claim. SnapshotHandler GET Redis key; on hit returns JSON; on miss sqlx SELECT latest from lmp_records WHERE pricing_node_id = ? ORDER BY interval_ending DESC LIMIT 1. WebSocket Push: Client upgrades to WS, sends {type:'subscribe', nodes:['PJM.AEP-GEN','PJM.CHICAGO-HUB']}. WebSocketHandler opens redis-rs PubSub async connection, subscribes to 'lmp:pub:PJM.AEP-GEN' and 'lmp:pub:PJM.CHICAGO-HUB'. tokio::select! waits on Redis message or WS control frame. On Redis message: forwards JSON frame to WS sink. On client ping: sends pong to keep connection alive.
target_output.data_model: pricing_nodes (id UUID PK, node_name TEXT UNIQUE e.g. 'PJM.AEP-GEN', iso_code TEXT CHECK IN ('pjm','caiso','miso','nyiso'), node_type TEXT CHECK IN ('hub','zone','generator','load'), latitude NUMERIC(9,6), longitude NUMERIC(9,6), active BOOL). lmp_records TimescaleDB hypertable (id BIGSERIAL, pricing_node_id UUID FK, interval_ending TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL, lmp_total NUMERIC(10,4), lmp_congestion NUMERIC(10,4), lmp_loss NUMERIC(10,4), source_feed TEXT, ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()); chunk_time_interval 1 day; compression policy after 7 days; continuous aggregate lmp_hourly_avg refreshed every 15 minutes. api_subscribers (id UUID PK, client_name TEXT, api_key TEXT UNIQUE, markets TEXT[] e.g. ARRAY['pjm','caiso'], tier TEXT CHECK IN ('real_time','day_ahead_only'), rate_limit_rps INT DEFAULT 100, active BOOL, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). iso_feeds (id UUID PK, iso_code TEXT UNIQUE, feed_url TEXT, last_polled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_successful_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status TEXT CHECK IN ('healthy','stale','error'), consecutive_failures INT DEFAULT 0). Energy market financial entities: lmp_settlements (id UUID PK, node_id FK, settlement_date DATE, interval_start TIMESTAMPTZ, interval_end TIMESTAMPTZ, settlement_point_price NUMERIC, energy_component_price NUMERIC, congestion_component_price NUMERIC, currency CHAR(3), market_type ENUM day_ahead/real_time), trading_accounts (id UUID PK, participant_id VARCHAR, account_type ENUM generator/load/trader, credit_limit_cents BIGINT, current_balance_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), active BOOLEAN), payment_transactions (id UUID PK, account_id FK, settlement_date DATE, debit_cents BIGINT, credit_cents BIGINT, net_amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), cleared_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM pending/settled/disputed).
target_output.api_design: REST base path /v1, TLS 1.3, JWT bearer required. GET /v1/lmp/{iso}/{node_name} — snapshot; response: {pricing_node, interval_ending, lmp_total, lmp_congestion, lmp_loss, stale: bool}; headers: X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-LMP-Stale. GET /v1/lmp/{iso}/{node_name}/history?from=2026-03-01T00:00:00Z&to=2026-03-07T00:00:00Z — time-range query; response: array of LmpRecord; TimescaleDB hypertable scan. WS /v1/lmp/stream — WebSocket upgrade; subscribe message {type:'subscribe', nodes:['PJM.AEP-GEN']}; server push frames {type:'lmp_update', node:'PJM.AEP-GEN', interval_ending, lmp_total, lmp_congestion, lmp_loss, published_at}. POST /admin/subscribers — provision subscriber; request: {client_name, markets:['pjm'], tier:'real_time', rate_limit_rps:200}; response: {api_key}. GET /admin/feeds — ISO feed health. Rate limit: 429 with Retry-After: 60 header on exhaustion. Internal ingestor write endpoint on VPC-internal ALB with mTLS: POST /internal/lmp/write (not partner-facing).
target_output.security_and_compliance: TLS 1.3 on ALB for all partner-facing endpoints; HTTP to HTTPS redirect enforced at ALB listener level. JWT HS256 tokens signed with server-side secret; claims validated on every request by Axum JWT tower middleware: signature, expiry, and 'markets' claim must include the requested ISO. Per-client rate limiting via atomic Redis Lua sliding window: single EVAL round-trip ensures no TOCTOU race condition under concurrent requests from high-frequency polling clients. mTLS enforced between lmp-ingestor and the VPC-internal write endpoint to prevent unauthorized LMP write injection. Market scope enforcement: SnapshotHandler returns 403 if JWT markets claim does not include the requested ISO; WebSocket subscription validates each requested node's ISO against JWT claims before subscribing to Pub/Sub channel. No LMP financial data in CloudWatch logs or Datadog trace payloads; only node_id and latency metrics. SOC 2 availability control: PagerDuty alert on feed gap >6 minutes; automated stale flag in Redis prevents serving outdated prices without client awareness.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: cargo clippy, cargo test (unit + sqlx integration tests with Testcontainers TimescaleDB), cargo build --release, Docker distroless image build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy (minimum healthy 100%, maximum 200%). k6 load test suite runs against staging before production promotion: 5,000 virtual users, 60-second ramp, snapshot and WebSocket scenarios, p99 latency SLA assertion. ECS Fargate lmp-api autoscaling: min 3 tasks, max 10 on CPU 60% target. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode with 3 shards for rate limiting state distribution. RDS TimescaleDB db.r6g.2xlarge Multi-AZ for HA. CloudWatch alarms for ECS task count and RDS connections. Canary releases: 10% traffic to new task version for 5 minutes with automatic rollback on p99 latency breach.
