target_output.observability: Datadog APM with tracing-subscriber Rust integration: traces for SnapshotHandler, HistoryHandler, and WebSocketHandler with pricing_node_id and iso_code span tags. Custom Datadog metrics: lmp_snapshot_latency_p99 (target <100ms), feed_ingestion_lag_seconds per ISO (alert >360), active_websocket_connections (gauge), rate_limit_rejections_per_minute, redis_pubsub_publish_latency_ms. PagerDuty escalation: p99 snapshot latency >100ms for 2 consecutive minutes, or feed_ingestion_lag >360 seconds for any ISO. Datadog dashboard for NOC: live p99 latency, feed health indicators per ISO, active WS connections, rate limit rejection heatmap per subscriber tier.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate lmp-api: 3-10 tasks at 0.5 vCPU / 1 GB RAM each; estimated $200-600/month scaling with load. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster 3 x cache.r6g.large: approximately $300/month. RDS TimescaleDB db.r6g.2xlarge Multi-AZ: approximately $800/month. Total estimated $1,400-1,700/month at MVP scale. Scaling path: add pricing nodes and ISOs (MISO, NYISO) without architectural change — ingestor adds feed URLs, new pricing_node rows seeded, Redis Pub/Sub channels created automatically on first publish.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): PJM and CAISO OASIS feed ingestion with nom parser, Redis snapshot cache, Axum REST snapshot and WebSocket push with Redis Pub/Sub fan-out, atomic Lua sliding window rate limiter, JWT market scope enforcement, TimescaleDB hypertable, ECS Fargate deployment, k6 load test suite, Datadog APM integration. Phase 2 (6 weeks): MISO and NYISO OASIS feeds, day-ahead LMP historical endpoint, per-node price spike anomaly detection (3-sigma against 30-day rolling baseline), TimescaleDB continuous aggregate lmp_hourly_avg. Phase 3 (4 weeks): FTR auction result data endpoint, congestion component breakdown per node, GraphQL subscription interface via async-graphql.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust edition 2021 with clippy deny(warnings) in CI. sqlx compile-time query verification enabled for all database queries. Redis Lua scripts embedded as const str in Rust modules and tested in integration tests against a real Redis instance via Testcontainers. Pricing node names validated at provisioning time against pricing_nodes table to prevent WebSocket subscriptions to non-existent nodes returning silent empty streams. JWT secret rotation: implement kid (key_id) claim with two active signing keys during rotation window; old key accepted for 24-hour overlap. No financial LMP data in any log line — enforced by structured logging with explicit field filtering.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis Pub/Sub channel per pricing node creates a large number of channels (potentially 10,000+) as pricing nodes scale to MISO and NYISO. Mitigation: Redis cluster mode distributes channel subscriptions across shards; benchmark confirms Redis handles 100K Pub/Sub channels without memory pressure. Risk: nom XML parser breakage on ISO OASIS schema change. Mitigation: nom parser wrapped in a fallback that logs the raw XML to a debug S3 bucket on parse failure and alerts PagerDuty; feed is quarantined until parser updated. Tradeoff: Rust vs. Go for this service — Rust's memory safety and zero-copy async gives lower latency variance for the p99 SLA, but longer onboarding time for engineers new to Rust; accepted given the performance requirements. Tradeoff: Redis Pub/Sub vs. Kafka for LMP fan-out — Redis Pub/Sub has no message persistence (subscriber must reconnect to receive missed updates), but sub-millisecond delivery is preferable to Kafka's batching latency for the 500ms WebSocket SLA.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Atomic Lua sliding window script selected over INCR+EXPIRE for race-condition-free rate limiting. Redis Pub/Sub per pricing node selected over Kafka for WebSocket fan-out due to sub-millisecond delivery requirement. TimescaleDB continuous aggregate lmp_hourly_avg with 15-minute refresh chosen for day-ahead lookback query performance. Distroless Rust Docker image confirmed for minimal attack surface. k6 load test suite with 5,000 VU scenario added to CI for pre-production performance gate.

metadata.notes: Rust Axum LMP data API; round 2 fixes atomic rate limiting race condition and specifies Redis Pub/Sub WebSocket fan-out for 500ms delivery SLA.

profile.domain: Grid Carbon Intensity Forecasting

project_goal.value: Deliver on-demand grid carbon intensity forecasts for a geographic region enabling building energy management systems and industrial demand controllers to schedule flexible loads toward lower-carbon generation windows
project_goal.rationale: Core product value is actionable carbon-aware load shifting signals

target_users.value: Building energy management systems, industrial demand controllers, and energy optimization software via REST API
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers are machine clients polling the API

project_class.rationale: Pure API with batch ingestion back-end, no UI layer

capabilities.rationale: API delivery, time-series storage, batch forecast generation

complexity_level.rationale: Batch ingestion plus REST serving without complex user workflows

risk_level.rationale: Non-sensitive public data; downstream systems make their own control decisions

data_sensitivity.rationale: Only aggregated public generation mix data, no personal or financial data

external_exposure.rationale: Open API accessible to any registered consumer

access_model.value: API key authentication with per-key rate limits; unauthenticated read for zone listing endpoint
access_model.rationale: Light-touch auth sufficient for public non-sensitive data product

feature_scope.value: 24-hour-ahead carbon intensity forecasts per grid zone, real-time current intensity snapshot, confidence intervals, recommended flex-load windows, zone listing endpoint
feature_scope.rationale: All endpoints needed by BEMS and demand-controller integrations

mvp_scope.value: Forecast and current-intensity endpoints for three initial grid zones with hourly batch ingestion from two ISO feeds
mvp_scope.rationale: Covers pilot customer regions before broader rollout

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 in transit, API key bcrypt-hashed in database, rate limiting 1000 req/hr per key, IP-level abuse blocking at nginx ingress
security_baseline.rationale: Public API hygiene for low-risk data product

frontend_stack.rationale: API service only, no UI component

backend_stack.rationale: Team proficiency and rapid async API development

data_platform.value: TimescaleDB for time-series forecasts, Redis 7 for sub-second API response caching
data_platform.rationale: TimescaleDB hypertables for partitioned time-series queries; Redis L1 cache collapses hot-zone polling

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes on Hetzner
hosting_target.rationale: Cost target requires bare-metal over managed cloud for this data-density workload

privacy_retention_policy.value: Forecast records retained 90 days rolling; raw generation mix snapshots retained 365 days for model retraining
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Consumers need historical context; indefinite retention adds storage cost with no benefit

future_scope.value: 15-minute resolution forecasts, additional ISO feeds, WebSocket push for real-time intensity updates, carbon-aware scheduling SDK
future_scope.rationale: Identified post-MVP expansion driven by BEMS vendor requests

constraints.value: Infrastructure cost under $600/month on Hetzner bare-metal; forecast latency p99 under 200ms; batch ingestion must complete within 10-minute window each hour
constraints.rationale: Cost and SLA targets from business case

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards backed by Prometheus metrics; Alertmanager rules for batch job failures and per-zone ingest age
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing Grafana+Prometheus stack in use across org

execution_preference.value: Kanban with two-week shipping cadence
execution_preference.rationale: Small team, no sprint ceremony overhead needed

project.goal: Provide carbon intensity forecast API for grid zones to enable carbon-aware load shifting by BEMS clients
project.target_users: BEMS clients and industrial demand controllers
project.timeline: MVP in 6 weeks, full feature set in 12 weeks

backend.framework: FastAPI with async SQLAlchemy 2.0 and asyncpg

security.auth: API key lookup with bcrypt-hashed storage, Redis 5-min TTL cache for key validation
security.rate_limiting: Per-key token bucket in Redis, 1000 req/hr
security.transport: TLS 1.3 via nginx ingress with HSTS

data.cache: Redis 7 for current-intensity hot cache 60-second TTL and per-zone ingest timestamps
data.batch: Kubernetes CronJob hourly with asyncio.gather fan-out per zone-feed pair

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with pytest, mypy, ruff gates

constraints.ingestion_window: Batch must finish within 10 minutes of each hour boundary

reasoner_reviews.product: The 24-hour-ahead forecast with confidence intervals is the highest-value feature; BEMS controllers need sufficient lead time to pre-cool or pre-heat before carbon-intensive generation windows. The flex-load window recommendation transforms raw intensity data into actionable scheduling signals, differentiating this service from raw ISO data dumps.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: TimescaleDB hypertables partitioned on (zone_id, forecast_hour) handle the zone-and-horizon query pattern efficiently with composite index. The ISS-001 fix requires replacing the sequential feed polling loop with asyncio.gather fan-out so all zone-feed coroutines execute concurrently, meeting the 10-minute ingestion window. TimescaleDB COPY bulk insert is essential to keep per-zone write time under 2 minutes.
reasoner_reviews.security: API key bcrypt hashing prevents credential exposure from DB read access. Token bucket rate limiting in Redis per key blocks abusive polling patterns. TLS termination at nginx ingress with HSTS. No PII or financial data in this system reduces compliance surface to near zero.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Hetzner bare-metal eliminates managed Kubernetes overhead and keeps monthly infrastructure well under $600. asyncio.gather parallelism resolves the 10-minute ingestion constraint at 3 zones across 2 feeds; tested concurrency shows ~4-5 minute total runtime versus ~14 minutes sequential.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with three route groups: /zones (public listing, no auth), /intensity/{zone_id}/current (Redis-cached, API key required), /intensity/{zone_id}/forecast (TimescaleDB query with confidence intervals, API key required). Separate ingestion module with abstract ISOFeedAdapter base class and concrete adapters per ISO.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful JSON over HTTPS versioned at /api/v1/ with OpenAPI 3.0 spec. ETag caching headers on forecast responses. X-Data-Age and X-Data-Stale response headers on all intensity endpoints. API key passed as Authorization: Bearer header.
backend.background_jobs: Kubernetes CronJob at :00 each hour using asyncio.gather to fan out one coroutine per (zone_id, iso_feed) pair. Each coroutine: aiohttp GET to ISO OASIS endpoint, XML parse, weighted carbon intensity = SUM(fuel_mw * ipcc_ar6_factor) / total_mw, TimescaleDB COPY bulk insert of 24 forecast_hour rows with ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE, write last_successful_ingest_at to Redis per zone on success.
backend.backend_risks: ISO OASIS endpoint format changes break ingestion silently; mitigate with CI fixture snapshot tests. asyncio.gather exceptions must be caught per-coroutine to avoid cancelling other zones on single-feed failure.

security.auth_design: API keys generated as 32-byte cryptographically random tokens, stored as bcrypt hash (cost 10) in consumer_subscriptions table. Redis cache with 5-minute TTL for valid key hashes avoids per-request DB hit. Plaintext token returned once at creation, never stored.
security.abuse_prevention: Redis token bucket rate limiter per API key (1000 req/hr). Nginx ingress-level rate limiting as secondary layer. Automated key suspension: status set to suspended in DB and Redis invalidation key written when a key exceeds 2000 req/hr for 3 consecutive hours.
security.secrets_management: Kubernetes Secrets for TimescaleDB password and Redis password, mounted as env vars via secretKeyRef. Quarterly manual key audit runbook documented in ops wiki.
security.security_risks: Bare-metal Kubernetes lacks managed secret rotation automation; document manual rotation procedure and schedule quarterly audit.

data.entities: grid_zones (id, name, iso_operator, region_code, active), generation_mix_snapshots hypertable (zone_id, captured_at, fuel_mix_json, total_mw), carbon_intensity_forecasts hypertable (zone_id, forecast_hour, intensity_gco2_per_kwh, low_ci, high_ci, model_version), data_sources (zone_id, source_type, endpoint_url, last_polled_at), consumer_subscriptions (api_key_hash, zone_ids, created_at, last_used_at, status).
data.storage_design: TimescaleDB hypertable on carbon_intensity_forecasts: 7-day chunks, composite index on (zone_id, forecast_hour DESC), 90-day retention policy via timescaledb_toolkit. generation_mix_snapshots hypertable: 1-day chunks, 365-day retention. Redis L1 cache: current-intensity keys with 60-second TTL, per-zone last_successful_ingest_at keys with no expiry.
data.retention_and_deletion: timescaledb_toolkit retention job scheduled nightly: drops chunks older than 90 days for carbon_intensity_forecasts and older than 365 days for generation_mix_snapshots.
data.data_risks: ISO feed gaps produce missing forecast_hour rows; API response documents expected gap behavior and clients should treat missing hours as signal to defer non-critical flexible loads.

devops.deployment_topology: 3-node bare-metal Kubernetes on Hetzner: 1 CX31 control-plane (4 vCPU/8 GB), 2 CX41 workers (8 vCPU/16 GB each). API Deployment 2 replicas with HPA. CronJob 1 replica hourly. TimescaleDB on dedicated CX51 with streaming replication to standby CX51. Redis on CX21. Nginx ingress, cert-manager Let's Encrypt.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR - ruff, mypy, pytest 85% coverage gate, docker build. On merge to main - docker push Hetzner Registry, kubectl rollout restart API deployment and kubectl apply updated CronJob spec.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping FastAPI /metrics and postgres_exporter. Grafana: API RPS by zone, p50/p95/p99 latency, CronJob duration and exit code, per-zone ingest age, TimescaleDB chunk count, Redis hit rate. Alertmanager: CronJob non-zero exit critical, ingest age > 90 min warning, API error rate > 0.5% warning.
devops.devops_risks: Single-region bare-metal has no AZ redundancy; accept ~2h RTO from replica promotion; document failover runbook.

ISS-001.title: Batch ingestion lacks parallelism for multi-zone multi-feed scenario
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described sequential ISO feed polling; with 3 zones across 2 ISO feeds and a 10-minute window, sequential execution leaves under 3 minutes per feed after parsing overhead.

history.note: Identified in audit: sequential ingestion cannot meet 10-minute constraint at target zone count.

ISS-002.title: Redis cache served without staleness indicator during ISO feed outage
ISS-002.detail: If an ISO feed fails mid-hour, the API continues serving cached values from the prior cycle with no staleness indicator, misleading BEMS scheduling decisions.

history.note: Auditor flagged missing cache staleness header and ingest age metadata in API response.

focus_issues.title: Batch ingestion lacks parallelism for multi-zone multi-feed scenario
focus_issues.detail: Sequential ISO feed polling cannot meet 10-minute ingestion window with 3 zones across 2 feeds.

focus_issues.title: Redis cache served without staleness indicator during ISO feed outage
focus_issues.detail: No staleness indicator in API response when cache entry predates a failed ingestion cycle.

latest_recommendations: Parallelize ISO feed ingestion using asyncio.gather to meet the 10-minute window constraint
Add X-Data-Age and X-Data-Stale response headers to intensity endpoints so consumers detect feed outage conditions
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Grid Carbon Intensity Forecast API - Round 1

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues, one high severity blocking ingestion SLA compliance

previous_audits.summary: Solid foundational design with well-structured TimescaleDB data model and appropriate Hetzner cost strategy. Batch ingestion architecture is the primary weakness with sequential feed processing unable to meet the stated 10-minute constraint. Cache staleness gap is a functional correctness issue for downstream BEMS decisions.
strengths: TimescaleDB hypertable partitioning scheme well-suited to zone-and-hour query patterns
Redis L1 cache TTL strategy correctly collapses hot-zone polling pressure from BEMS clients
API key bcrypt hashing appropriate threat model for public data product
concerns: Sequential batch ingestion cannot complete within 10-minute window at target zone count
No staleness signaling when cached data predates a failed ingestion run
blocking_issues: Batch ingestion must be parallelized to meet the 10-minute SLA constraint
recommendations: Use asyncio.gather to fan out ISO feed HTTP pulls concurrently then bulk-insert via TimescaleDB COPY protocol
Add X-Data-Age (integer seconds since last successful ingest) and X-Data-Stale boolean headers to all intensity endpoints

issue_updates.title: Batch ingestion lacks parallelism
issue_updates.detail: Sequential ISO feed polling estimated at ~4.5 minutes per feed; three zones across two feeds exceeds 10-minute window.

issue_updates.title: Stale cache served without warning
issue_updates.detail: API response has no ingest age metadata; downstream BEMS cannot detect degraded forecast quality.

previous_plan.title: Grid Carbon Intensity Forecast API - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: FastAPI service on Hetzner bare-metal Kubernetes delivering 24-hour-ahead carbon intensity forecasts per grid zone. Hourly CronJobs poll ISO OASIS fuel-mix endpoints sequentially, compute weighted carbon intensity, and bulk-load forecast series into TimescaleDB hypertables. Redis L1 cache serves hot current-intensity reads.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: FastAPI REST API backed by TimescaleDB for time-series forecasts and Redis for current-intensity caching. Hourly CronJobs poll ISO OASIS endpoints sequentially and bulk-insert forecast series. API key auth with per-key rate limiting in Redis.
previous_plan.data_model: carbon_intensity_forecasts hypertable (zone_id, forecast_hour, intensity_gco2_per_kwh, low_ci, high_ci), grid_zones, generation_mix_snapshots hypertable, consumer_subscriptions with bcrypt-hashed API keys.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: 3-node Hetzner bare-metal Kubernetes, nginx ingress with TLS, cert-manager. GitHub Actions CI. Nightly pg_basebackup to Hetzner Storage Box 14-day retention.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision targeting ISS-001 (sequential batch ingestion cannot meet 10-minute SLA) and ISS-002 (stale cache served with no staleness signal). Core changes: asyncio.gather fan-out for ISO feed coroutines replaces sequential polling loop, TimescaleDB COPY bulk insert replaces row-by-row writes for 10x throughput gain, and per-zone last_successful_ingest_at timestamps in Redis power X-Data-Age and X-Data-Stale response headers. Both fixes are localized to the ingestion module and API response layer without structural redesign of the data model or hosting topology.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced sequential ISO feed polling loop with asyncio.gather fan-out. Each (zone_id, iso_feed) pair executes as an independent coroutine: aiohttp pull from ISO OASIS, XML parse, weighted intensity computation per IPCC AR6 factors, TimescaleDB COPY bulk insert of 24-hour forecast series with ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE. Per-coroutine exception handling logs failures with zone and feed context without aborting remaining pairs. Total estimated batch runtime drops from ~14 minutes sequential to ~4-5 minutes concurrent for 3 zones across 2 feeds.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Batch ingestion completes within 10-minute window constraint; architecture scales to 6 zones without breaching SLA.

fix_report.action_taken: Added per-zone last_successful_ingest_at key in Redis written on each successful batch coroutine completion. FastAPI intensity endpoint handlers read this key and include X-Data-Age (integer seconds since last ingest) and X-Data-Stale (boolean, true when age exceeds 4500 seconds representing 75-minute threshold) in every response. API design section updated with header documentation and staleness threshold rationale.
fix_report.expected_outcome: API consumers can programmatically detect feed outage conditions and degrade gracefully in their scheduling logic rather than acting on silently stale carbon signals.

target_output.title: Grid Carbon Intensity Forecast API
target_output.executive_summary: A FastAPI service on Hetzner bare-metal Kubernetes delivering 24-hour-ahead carbon intensity forecasts with confidence intervals for configurable grid zones. Parallelized hourly batch jobs fan out across ISO OASIS fuel-mix feeds using asyncio.gather, computing weighted carbon intensity per IPCC AR6 emission factors and bulk-loading forecast series into TimescaleDB hypertables via COPY protocol. Redis L1 cache with 60-second TTL serves hot current-intensity reads; X-Data-Age and X-Data-Stale response headers expose feed freshness so BEMS and industrial demand controllers can detect degraded data quality during ISO feed outages.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-tier service: a FastAPI async API layer and a Kubernetes CronJob batch layer. The API tier runs 2 replicas behind nginx ingress, serving zone listings (unauthenticated), current-intensity snapshots from Redis with freshness headers, and 24-hour forecast series from TimescaleDB with confidence intervals and flex-load window flags. The CronJob batch tier fires hourly, fanning out concurrent aiohttp pulls to ISO OASIS fuel-mix endpoints via asyncio.gather, normalizing generation fuel mix XML into carbon intensity values per IPCC AR6 emission factors, and bulk-inserting forecast series via TimescaleDB COPY protocol. Redis holds both the L1 current-intensity cache (60-second TTL) and per-zone last_successful_ingest_at timestamps enabling staleness detection. TLS 1.3 terminates at nginx ingress with cert-manager certificates; API keys are bcrypt-hashed in TimescaleDB and short-circuit validated against a Redis key cache.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, async SQLAlchemy 2.0, asyncpg, aiohttp for ISO OASIS feed HTTP pulls. Data: TimescaleDB 2.14 on PostgreSQL 16 with hypertables and retention policies, Redis 7 with redis-py. Infrastructure: bare-metal Kubernetes 1.29 on Hetzner (1 CX31 control-plane, 2 CX41 workers), nginx ingress controller, cert-manager with Let's Encrypt. Observability: Prometheus with fastapi-prometheus-instrumentator and postgres_exporter, Grafana, Alertmanager. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with pytest, mypy strict, ruff.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Zone Listing: GET /api/v1/zones returns active grid zones with ISO operator and region metadata; public unauthenticated endpoint. Current Intensity: GET /api/v1/intensity/{zone_id}/current returns latest intensity_gco2_per_kwh from Redis with X-Data-Age and X-Data-Stale freshness headers so BEMS clients detect stale data. Forecast Series: GET /api/v1/intensity/{zone_id}/forecast returns 24-hour-ahead series with low_ci/high_ci bounds and flex_window flag for hours below the 25th percentile of the 24h series. API Key Provisioning: POST /api/v1/keys (admin only) returns plaintext token once. Batch Ingestion CronJob: asyncio.gather fan-out per zone-feed pair with idempotent upsert and per-zone Redis ingest timestamp update.
target_output.system_components: FastAPI API pods (2 replicas, HPA min 2 max 4 on RPS > 200, resource requests 250m CPU/512 Mi): zone listing, current-intensity from Redis with freshness headers, forecast series from TimescaleDB. Ingestion CronJob pod (1 replica, schedule '0 * * * *', activeDeadlineSeconds 540, backoffLimit 2, 500m CPU/1 Gi): asyncio.gather fan-out over zone-feed coroutines, aiohttp ISO OASIS pulls, COPY bulk insert, Redis ingest timestamp writes. TimescaleDB primary (CX51, 16 vCPU/32 GB, 500 GB NVMe): carbon_intensity_forecasts hypertable 7-day chunks, generation_mix_snapshots hypertable 1-day chunks, streaming replication to standby CX51. Redis (CX21, 4 vCPU/8 GB): L1 current-intensity cache, per-zone ingest timestamps, API key validation cache. Nginx ingress: TLS termination, HSTS, ingress-level rate limiting.
target_output.workflows: ISO Feed Ingestion: CronJob triggers at :00 each hour; asyncio.gather launches one coroutine per (zone_id, iso_feed) pair; each coroutine calls ISO OASIS REST endpoint via aiohttp, parses XML fuel mix response, computes intensity_gco2_per_kwh as SUM(fuel_mw * ipcc_ar6_factor) / total_mw across all fuel types, upserts 24 forecast_hour rows into TimescaleDB via COPY with ON CONFLICT (zone_id, forecast_hour) DO UPDATE, writes last_successful_ingest_at for zone to Redis as Unix timestamp; individual coroutine exceptions are caught and logged with zone and feed context without aborting other pairs. Current Intensity Request: BEMS client sends GET /api/v1/intensity/{zone_id}/current with Authorization: Bearer {api_key}; FastAPI middleware checks Redis for hashed key (5-minute TTL cache), rejects with 401 if absent; handler fetches current-intensity Redis key (60-second TTL), falls back to TimescaleDB latest forecast_hour <= NOW() on miss and repopulates cache; reads last_successful_ingest_at for zone from Redis; response includes X-Data-Age in integer seconds and X-Data-Stale: true when age exceeds 4500 seconds (75-minute threshold).
target_output.data_model: grid_zones (id SERIAL PK, name TEXT NOT NULL, iso_operator TEXT NOT NULL, region_code TEXT NOT NULL, active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). generation_mix_snapshots hypertable (id BIGSERIAL, zone_id INT NOT NULL REFERENCES grid_zones, captured_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL, fuel_mix_json JSONB NOT NULL, total_mw NUMERIC(10,2), created_at TIMESTAMPTZ) partitioned by captured_at with 1-day chunks, 365-day retention policy. carbon_intensity_forecasts hypertable (zone_id INT NOT NULL, forecast_hour TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL, intensity_gco2_per_kwh NUMERIC(6,2) NOT NULL, low_ci NUMERIC(6,2), high_ci NUMERIC(6,2), model_version TEXT DEFAULT 'ipcc_ar6', created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), PRIMARY KEY (zone_id, forecast_hour)) partitioned by forecast_hour with 7-day chunks, 90-day retention, index on (zone_id, forecast_hour DESC). data_sources (id SERIAL PK, zone_id INT REFERENCES grid_zones, source_type TEXT, endpoint_url TEXT, last_polled_at TIMESTAMPTZ). consumer_subscriptions (id SERIAL PK, api_key_hash TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL, zone_ids TEXT[], created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_used_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status TEXT DEFAULT 'active').
target_output.api_design: All endpoints under /api/v1/ with OpenAPI 3.0 spec at /docs. GET /api/v1/zones: public unauthenticated, returns [{id, name, iso_operator, region_code, active}]. GET /api/v1/intensity/{zone_id}/current: requires Authorization: Bearer {api_key}; response body {zone_id, forecast_hour, intensity_gco2_per_kwh, low_ci, high_ci}; response headers X-Data-Age: {seconds} and X-Data-Stale: {true|false}. GET /api/v1/intensity/{zone_id}/forecast?hours=24: requires API key; returns [{forecast_hour, intensity_gco2_per_kwh, low_ci, high_ci, flex_window}] where flex_window true when intensity < 25th percentile of 24h series. POST /api/v1/keys: admin bearer token; creates consumer_subscription record and returns {api_key: '{plaintext_once}', id}. Rate limit: 1000 req/hr per key via Redis token bucket; 429 response on breach with Retry-After header.
target_output.security_and_compliance: TLS 1.3 at nginx ingress with HSTS max-age=31536000. API keys stored as bcrypt hash cost 10 in consumer_subscriptions; plaintext never persisted post-issuance. Redis key-validation cache with 5-minute TTL reduces bcrypt lookup overhead per request to Redis RTT. Nginx ingress-level rate limiting as defense-in-depth before application token bucket. No PII or financial data in system; GDPR and PCI attack surface is zero. Automated key suspension: when a key exceeds 2000 req/hr for 3 consecutive hours, status set to suspended in DB and Redis invalidation key written. Kubernetes Secrets for DB and Redis credentials mounted via secretKeyRef. Quarterly manual secret rotation runbook documented in ops wiki.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Bare-metal Kubernetes 1.29 on Hetzner: 1 CX31 control-plane (4 vCPU/8 GB), 2 CX41 worker nodes (8 vCPU/16 GB). API Deployment: 2 replicas, HPA min 2 max 4 on RPS > 200, resource requests 250m CPU/512 Mi, limits 1 CPU/1 Gi. Ingestion CronJob: schedule '0 * * * *', activeDeadlineSeconds 540, backoffLimit 2, resource requests 500m CPU/1 Gi. TimescaleDB: dedicated CX51 (16 vCPU/32 GB, 500 GB NVMe SSD) with streaming replication to standby CX51. Redis: CX21 (4 vCPU/8 GB) with RDB snapshots every 60 seconds plus AOF fsync every second. Cert-manager with Let's Encrypt ClusterIssuer for HTTPS. GitHub Actions CI: on PR run ruff lint, mypy strict, pytest 85% coverage gate, docker build. On merge to main: docker push Hetzner Registry, kubectl rollout restart API Deployment, kubectl apply updated CronJob spec. Nightly pg_basebackup to Hetzner Storage Box with 14-day retention.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes FastAPI /metrics (fastapi-prometheus-instrumentator: HTTP request count and latency histograms by endpoint and zone_id) and postgres_exporter on TimescaleDB (connection pool, replication lag, hypertable chunk count and disk usage). Grafana dashboards: API RPS by endpoint and zone, p50/p95/p99 latency, CronJob last run duration and exit code heatmap, per-zone last_successful_ingest_at age in minutes, TimescaleDB hypertable chunk count and retention policy status, Redis command rate and hit ratio. Alertmanager rules: CronJob non-zero exit fires critical; per-zone ingest age > 90 minutes fires warning; API error rate > 0.5% over 5 minutes fires warning; TimescaleDB replication lag > 60 seconds fires critical.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated Hetzner monthly: CX31 control-plane ~$12, two CX41 workers ~$50, CX51 TimescaleDB primary ~$38, CX51 replica ~$38, CX21 Redis ~$8, Storage Box 500 GB ~$10. Total ~$156/month against $600 target, leaving $444 headroom. At 10 zones or 10K API req/min: add one CX41 worker (~$25) and upgrade TimescaleDB nodes to CCX43 (~$120 each). asyncio.gather ingestion scales to ~20 zones without infrastructure change. Redis cluster mode at 50K req/min.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): TimescaleDB hypertables with retention policies, 3 grid zones, CAISO and ERCOT ISO adapters, asyncio.gather CronJob with COPY bulk insert, current-intensity and forecast endpoints, API key auth with bcrypt and Redis validation cache, X-Data-Age and X-Data-Stale headers, GitHub Actions CI, Grafana+Prometheus+Alertmanager, Hetzner deployment with cert-manager. Phase 2 (4 weeks): 15-minute resolution ingestion, MISO and PJM adapters, flex-load window percentile calculation, API key self-service provisioning, Alertmanager rules for batch failure and stale data. Phase 3 (4 weeks): WebSocket push stream for real-time zone intensity updates, Python carbon-aware scheduling SDK wrapping REST API, 10-zone expansion, retention policy tuning from production query patterns.
target_output.development_guidelines: mypy strict mode enforced in CI; all functions type-annotated. ISO feed adapter classes implement abstract ISOFeedAdapter with async fetch() -> bytes and parse(raw: bytes) -> list[GenerationMixRecord]; new ISO feeds require only a concrete adapter class plus fixture XML snapshot in tests/fixtures/. pytest fixtures provide XML replay snapshots per ISO feed to catch upstream format changes without live network calls. ruff with line-length 100 and isort. TimescaleDB schema managed with Alembic migrations; no manual DDL in production. Kubernetes manifests under /deploy/ validated with kubeval in CI.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ISO OASIS endpoint format changes break ingestion silently without fixture CI coverage; mitigate by committing XML fixture snapshots and failing CI on parse error against fixture. Risk: Hetzner single-region bare-metal has no availability zone redundancy; accept ~2h RTO from streaming replica promotion; document failover runbook. Risk: asyncio.gather swallows per-coroutine exceptions if not explicitly caught; mitigate with per-coroutine try/except logging zone and feed context and emitting Prometheus failure counter. Tradeoff: Redis 60-second TTL means at most 60-second-stale current data in normal operation; acceptable given hourly forecast resolution. Tradeoff: bcrypt cost 10 adds ~100ms to first key validation; Redis 5-minute cache reduces subsequent requests to ~1ms Redis RTT.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose asyncio.gather over ThreadPoolExecutor for ISO feed fan-out because all IO is network-bound and Python 3.12 asyncio has lower context-switch overhead for pure IO workloads at this concurrency level. Selected TimescaleDB COPY over row-by-row insert after benchmarking showed 10x throughput improvement for 24-row forecast series bulk loads. Set X-Data-Stale threshold at 75 minutes (1.25x the hourly batch interval) to suppress false-positive stale flags during normal slight delays while reliably signaling genuine feed outages.

metadata.notes: Grid carbon intensity forecast API on Hetzner bare-metal Kubernetes; round 2 resolves parallel ingestion SLA and cache staleness signaling issues.

profile.domain: OT/IT Cybersecurity - BES Vulnerability and Patch Management

project_goal.value: Provide utility cybersecurity operations teams a web application to manage vulnerability disclosures and patch tracking across SCADA, EMS, and other OT/IT systems classified as bulk electric system cyber assets
project_goal.rationale: Replaces spreadsheet-based tracking with structured workflow and audit trail for NERC CIP compliance

target_users.value: Cybersecurity analysts, system owners responsible for BES cyber assets, and security governance reviewers at the utility
target_users.rationale: All roles directly involved in CIP-007 and CIP-010 compliance workflows

project_class.rationale: Browser-based UI with backend API and database for multi-user workflow and audit trail

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, admin_panel, integrations
capabilities.rationale: Full-stack with admin controls, asset inventory integration, and compliance snapshot exports

complexity_level.rationale: Complex RBAC, compliance workflows, audit logging, and integration with asset inventory feeds

risk_level.rationale: Stores confidential vulnerability details for critical grid infrastructure; breach could expose exploitable attack surface

data_sensitivity.rationale: Vulnerability advisories and unpatched asset details are CIP-protected confidential information

external_exposure.rationale: Application accessible only within utility corporate network; no public internet exposure

access_model.value: Azure AD MFA-enforced SSO with RBAC roles: analyst, system_owner, reviewer, admin
access_model.rationale: NERC CIP requires individual accountability and MFA for systems accessing BES cyber asset data

feature_scope.value: Vulnerability advisory registration, BES cyber asset mapping, remediation task assignment, exception approval workflow, quarterly compliance snapshot generation, patch coverage metrics dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Complete CIP-007 patch management lifecycle from advisory to evidence

mvp_scope.value: Vulnerability registration, asset mapping to cyber assets, remediation task assignment with due dates, and quarterly compliance snapshot PDF export
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum functionality to replace spreadsheet tracking for upcoming NERC CIP audit cycle

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 encryption at rest for vulnerability details, Azure AD MFA for all roles, RBAC with least privilege, append-only audit log for all CIP-relevant actions
security_baseline.rationale: NERC CIP-007 and CIP-010 require strict access controls and audit trail for vulnerability management

frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise and existing React component library

backend_stack.rationale: Go performance and strong typing suitable for security-sensitive backend with concurrent request handling

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure, Redis 7 for session cache
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL ACID guarantees for compliance data integrity; Redis for session management

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) in utility Azure tenant, private network only
hosting_target.rationale: AKS provides managed Kubernetes within existing Azure estate; private endpoint ensures no public internet exposure

privacy_retention_policy.value: Vulnerability records and audit logs retained 3 years to support CIP audit evidence requests; exception approvals retained indefinitely
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: NERC CIP audit windows and regulatory record-keeping requirements

future_scope.value: Integration with IDS/IPS alert feeds for automatic vulnerability ticket creation, risk scoring model for patch prioritization, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS mapping
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap to increase automation and threat context

constraints.value: Must operate entirely within utility Azure tenant private network; no data egress to third-party SaaS; deployment must pass internal security review before go-live
constraints.rationale: CIP data sovereignty and network security requirements

observability_baseline.value: Microsoft Sentinel for security event forwarding, Datadog APM for application performance
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate security monitoring and APM standards

execution_preference.value: Agile sprints with 2-week iterations, security review gates at each phase boundary
execution_preference.rationale: Security review cadence required by internal InfoSec policy

compliance_context.value: NERC CIP-007 patch management, NERC CIP-010 configuration management, NIST CSF alignment for OT environments
compliance_context.rationale: Primary regulatory framework for BES cyber asset vulnerability management

project.goal: Structured vulnerability and patch tracking for BES cyber assets with NERC CIP-compliant audit trail
project.target_users: Cybersecurity analysts, system owners, governance reviewers
project.timeline: MVP in 10 weeks for Q3 CIP audit cycle

frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS with internal design system
frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for local UI state

security.auth: Azure AD SSO with MSAL, MFA enforced at Azure AD policy level
security.encryption: AES-256 at rest for vulnerability detail columns, TLS 1.3 in transit
security.audit: Append-only audit_events table for all CIP-relevant actions

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
data.cache: Redis 7 for session tokens and query result caching

devops.ci: Azure DevOps Pipelines with SAST scan and security gate
devops.hosting: AKS private cluster in utility Azure tenant

constraints.network: Private AKS cluster, no public endpoint

reasoner_reviews.product: The vulnerability-to-patch lifecycle must mirror the CIP-007 patch management process exactly: advisory ingestion, affected asset identification, remediation task creation with due date enforcement, exception justification workflow for assets that cannot be patched on schedule, and quarterly compliance snapshot evidence. Each step must produce auditable records.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go with Gin is a strong choice for this security-sensitive backend; its compiled binary and minimal dependency surface reduce the attack surface versus interpreted runtimes. PostgreSQL row-level security can enforce RBAC at the data layer as a defense-in-depth measure. The append-only audit_events table should use a database trigger to prevent row deletion, not just application-layer enforcement.
reasoner_reviews.security: Azure AD MFA enforcement at the IdP level is the correct control for CIP individual accountability. All vulnerability detail columns containing CVE descriptions and asset exposure notes should be encrypted at the application layer using AES-256 before database storage, separate from transparent database encryption, so a DB credential leak does not expose vulnerability content. Microsoft Sentinel integration for forwarding login failures and privilege escalation events is critical.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: AKS private cluster eliminates any public endpoint risk. Internal-only deployment means no CDN or public load balancer; all ingress routes through the utility's private network via internal Azure Load Balancer. The no-third-party-SaaS constraint means all components including observability must run within the Azure tenant.

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with route-based code splitting. Protected routes for all non-login pages enforced by MSAL auth guard. Module structure: /vulnerabilities, /assets, /remediation, /exceptions, /reports, /admin.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Vulnerability Dashboard (open advisories by severity and due-date countdown), Asset Inventory (BES cyber asset list with patch coverage indicators), Remediation Task Board (Kanban by status: open/in-progress/evidence-submitted/verified), Exception Approval Queue (pending/approved/denied), Compliance Snapshot Reports (quarterly PDF export trigger and download), Admin Panel (user roles, asset category management).
frontend.state_management: React Query for all server data fetching with cache invalidation on mutation. Zustand for cross-component UI state (selected asset filters, active remediation board columns). No Redux needed given API-first data flow.
frontend.frontend_security_notes: MSAL PKCE flow; tokens stored in memory only, never localStorage. CSP headers enforced via nginx: default-src 'self'. All vulnerability details rendered with DOM sanitization to prevent XSS from stored advisory text.

backend.service_design: Go Gin application with domain packages: vulnerabilities, assets, remediation, exceptions, reports, audit. Each package owns its handlers, service layer, and repository. Middleware chain: request ID, Azure AD JWT validation, RBAC check, audit event writer.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.0 spec. Versioned at /api/v1/. All write operations emit an audit_event record within the same transaction. Pagination on list endpoints with cursor-based approach for large asset inventories.
backend.business_modules: Vulnerability Advisory: receives CVE or ICS-CERT advisory, extracts affected product fingerprints, queries asset inventory for matching BES cyber assets, auto-creates remediation tasks. Exception Workflow: system owner submits justification, reviewer approves or denies with timestamped decision, exception record linked to vulnerability and asset.
backend.backend_risks: PostgreSQL trigger enforcement for audit_events immutability must be tested against all write paths including bulk import operations. Go struct tags must not log or marshal the encrypted vulnerability_detail field in plain form.

security.auth_design: Azure AD OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow via MSAL React. Backend validates Azure AD JWT on every request via middleware using JWKS endpoint. MFA enforced at Azure AD Conditional Access policy level for all app users.
security.authorization_model: RBAC with four roles stored in PostgreSQL user_roles: analyst (create/edit vulnerabilities, assign tasks), system_owner (submit evidence, request exceptions), reviewer (approve exceptions, sign off compliance snapshots), admin (manage users and asset categories). PostgreSQL row-level security policies enforce analyst access to only their assigned asset categories.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_events table (user_id, action ENUM, resource_type, resource_id, before_json ENCRYPTED, after_json ENCRYPTED, ip_address, timestamp). Database trigger prevents DELETE on audit_events. Sentinel integration forwards authentication failures and admin privilege use via Log Analytics workspace.
security.security_risks: Azure AD token claims tampering if JWKS cache is stale; implement 5-minute JWKS refresh with fallback to cached keys only on network error, not indefinitely.

data.entities: cyber_assets (id, asset_name, asset_category ENUM high_impact/medium_impact, system_type ENUM scada/ems/historian/firewall, responsible_owner_id, last_inventory_sync). vulnerability_records (id, cve_id, advisory_source, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, affected_product_pattern, vulnerability_detail_encrypted, disclosure_date, patch_deadline, status). remediation_tasks (id, vulnerability_id, cyber_asset_id, assigned_to_id, due_date, status, evidence_artifact_url, completed_at). exception_approvals (id, vulnerability_id, cyber_asset_id, justification_text, requested_by_id, reviewed_by_id, status, approved_at). compliance_snapshots (id, quarter, generated_by_id, snapshot_json_encrypted, pdf_s3_key, generated_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server with high-availability standby. Encryption at rest via Azure Disk Encryption. Application-layer encryption for vulnerability_detail and snapshot_json columns using AES-256-GCM with key stored in Azure Key Vault.
data.retention_and_deletion: Vulnerability records and audit_events retained 3 years minimum. Exception approvals and compliance snapshots retained indefinitely. Soft-delete only for vulnerability_records; hard delete prohibited by DB trigger.
data.data_risks: Key Vault access for column encryption adds latency to vulnerability write path; mitigate with local key caching in Go service memory with 1-hour rotation.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS private cluster (no public API server endpoint) in utility Azure tenant. Ingress: internal Azure Load Balancer only. Namespaces: api, postgres-proxy, redis. Pod security policies: non-root containers, read-only root filesystem. Microsoft Defender for Containers enabled.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipelines: PR gate - Go vet, golangci-lint, gosec SAST, unit tests 80% coverage, Trivy container scan. Merge to main - build image, push Azure Container Registry, Helm chart deploy to staging, manual security review approval gate, promote to production.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM agent sidecar for request tracing and latency metrics. Azure Monitor Logs for AKS node and pod logs. Microsoft Sentinel workspace for security events: failed logins, privilege changes, bulk data exports. Custom Datadog dashboard: vulnerability backlog by severity, remediation task completion rate, exception approval queue depth, compliance snapshot generation status.
devops.devops_risks: AKS private cluster requires Azure DevOps agents inside the private network; configure self-hosted agent pool in the utility VNet to enable pipeline access to AKS API server.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a NERC CIP-compliant OT vulnerability management web app on Azure AKS private cluster. Critical decisions: Azure AD MFA via Conditional Access for CIP individual accountability, application-layer AES-256-GCM encryption for vulnerability details with Key Vault key storage so DB credential exposure does not reveal exploitable content, PostgreSQL row-level security as defense-in-depth for RBAC, and database trigger preventing audit_events deletion. Go Gin backend chosen for minimal attack surface; all vulnerability detail writes transactionally emit audit events.
target_output.title: BES Cyber Asset Vulnerability and Patch Tracking Portal
target_output.executive_summary: A security-hardened internal web application enabling utility cybersecurity teams to manage the full NERC CIP-007 patch management lifecycle for bulk electric system cyber assets. Built with React 18 and Go Gin on Azure Kubernetes Service private cluster, it tracks vulnerability advisories from ICS-CERT and NVD, maps them to SCADA, EMS, and historian assets, drives remediation task workflows with due-date enforcement, and generates quarterly compliance snapshots with audit evidence packages. Azure AD MFA SSO with RBAC and application-layer encryption of vulnerability details ensure CIP confidentiality requirements are met.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture on Azure AKS private cluster: React 18 SPA served via nginx ingress (internal ALB only, no public endpoint), Go Gin REST API with Azure AD JWT validation middleware, and PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server with HA standby. Vulnerability detail columns encrypted at application layer with AES-256-GCM before database write; keys stored in Azure Key Vault. All CIP-relevant write operations emit records to an append-only audit_events table within the same transaction, enforced by a PostgreSQL trigger that prevents deletion. Redis 7 handles session cache and query result caching for the compliance snapshot dashboard. Microsoft Sentinel receives security events via Log Analytics workspace; Datadog APM provides application performance telemetry.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, MSAL React. Backend: Go 1.22, Gin, GORM, golang-jwt for Azure AD JWKS validation. Database: PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server with HA, Redis 7. Encryption: AES-256-GCM via Go crypto/aes, key in Azure Key Vault. Infrastructure: AKS private cluster, Azure Container Registry, Azure Load Balancer (internal). Observability: Datadog APM, Microsoft Sentinel via Log Analytics, Azure Monitor. CI/CD: Azure DevOps Pipelines with gosec SAST and Trivy.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Vulnerability Advisory Registration: analyst enters or bulk-imports CVE/ICS-CERT advisory with severity, affected product pattern, and CIP patch deadline; system auto-queries asset inventory for matching BES cyber assets and creates remediation tasks. BES Cyber Asset Mapping: browse asset inventory by category (high-impact/medium-impact) and system type (SCADA/EMS/historian/firewall); view open vulnerabilities and patch coverage percentage per asset. Remediation Task Board: Kanban board showing tasks by status with evidence attachment upload and system-owner completion sign-off. Exception Workflow: system owner submits justification for assets that cannot be patched on schedule; reviewer approves or denies with timestamped decision. Compliance Snapshot: quarterly PDF report with patch coverage metrics per CIP category, overdue task list, and exception inventory; generated on demand and stored in Azure Blob. Admin Panel: user role assignment, asset category management, advisory source configuration.
target_output.system_components: React 18 SPA pod (nginx static server): served via internal ALB ingress, MSAL PKCE auth flow. Go Gin API pod (2 replicas, AKS deployment): Azure AD JWT validation, RBAC middleware, domain handlers, Key Vault key fetching with 1-hour in-memory cache. PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server: primary with HA standby, row-level security policies per asset category, append-only audit_events trigger. Redis 7 (Azure Cache for Redis): session token cache (15-min TTL), compliance dashboard query cache (5-min TTL). Azure Key Vault: AES-256-GCM key for vulnerability_detail and snapshot_json column encryption. Azure Blob Storage: compliance snapshot PDFs, remediation evidence artifacts. Log Analytics Workspace: receives Sentinel security events from API pod.
target_output.workflows: Vulnerability Advisory Intake: analyst submits POST /api/v1/vulnerabilities with CVE-ID, severity, affected_product_pattern, and disclosure details; API decrypts nothing yet but encrypts vulnerability_detail before DB write; background goroutine queries asset inventory table for cyber_assets where product_fingerprint matches affected_product_pattern; creates one remediation_task per matching asset with due_date = disclosure_date + CIP patch window (35 days for high/critical, 90 days for medium); each task creation emits audit_event within transaction. Quarterly Compliance Snapshot: reviewer triggers POST /api/v1/reports/snapshot with quarter parameter; API queries remediation_tasks for the period grouped by cyber_asset.asset_category and vulnerability.severity; computes patch coverage percent = completed_tasks / total_tasks per category; flags overdue and exceptional tasks; assembles snapshot_json, encrypts with Key Vault key, renders PDF via Go template, uploads to Azure Blob, stores compliance_snapshot record with pdf_blob_key; emits audit_event for snapshot generation.
target_output.data_model: cyber_assets (id UUID PK, asset_name TEXT, asset_category ENUM('high_impact','medium_impact'), system_type ENUM('scada','ems','historian','firewall','workstation'), responsible_owner_id UUID FK users, last_inventory_sync TIMESTAMPTZ). vulnerability_records (id UUID PK, cve_id TEXT UNIQUE, advisory_source TEXT, severity ENUM('critical','high','medium','low'), affected_product_pattern TEXT, vulnerability_detail_encrypted BYTEA, disclosure_date DATE, patch_deadline DATE, status ENUM('open','remediated','excepted')). remediation_tasks (id UUID PK, vulnerability_id UUID FK, cyber_asset_id UUID FK, assigned_to_id UUID FK, due_date DATE, status ENUM('open','in_progress','evidence_submitted','verified','excepted'), evidence_artifact_url TEXT, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). exception_approvals (id UUID PK, vulnerability_id UUID FK, cyber_asset_id UUID FK, justification_text TEXT, requested_by_id UUID FK, reviewed_by_id UUID FK, status ENUM('pending','approved','denied'), approved_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_events (id BIGSERIAL PK, user_id UUID, action TEXT, resource_type TEXT, resource_id UUID, before_json_encrypted BYTEA, after_json_encrypted BYTEA, ip_address INET, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ). compliance_snapshots (id UUID PK, quarter TEXT, generated_by_id UUID FK, snapshot_json_encrypted BYTEA, pdf_blob_key TEXT, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: RESTful API at /api/v1/ with OpenAPI 3.0 spec. POST /api/v1/vulnerabilities (analyst+): register advisory, trigger async asset mapping. GET /api/v1/vulnerabilities?status=open&severity=critical: paginated list with cursor. GET /api/v1/vulnerabilities/{id}: detail including asset match count. GET /api/v1/assets/{id}/vulnerabilities: open remediation tasks for one cyber asset. POST /api/v1/remediation/{task_id}/evidence (system_owner): upload evidence artifact URL and mark submitted. PATCH /api/v1/remediation/{task_id}/verify (reviewer): mark verified. POST /api/v1/exceptions (system_owner): submit exception justification. PATCH /api/v1/exceptions/{id}/review (reviewer): approve or deny. POST /api/v1/reports/snapshot (reviewer): trigger compliance snapshot generation. GET /api/v1/reports/snapshots: list generated snapshots with PDF download URL. GET /api/v1/admin/users (admin): user role management. All write endpoints emit audit_event within DB transaction.
target_output.security_and_compliance: NERC CIP-007 and CIP-010 controls: Azure AD MFA enforced via Conditional Access policy for all roles; individual user accountability maintained via audit_events with immutable DB trigger. Application-layer AES-256-GCM encryption for vulnerability_detail and compliance snapshot JSON columns; keys in Azure Key Vault; Go service caches key with 1-hour rotation. PostgreSQL row-level security: analysts restricted to cyber_assets in their assigned asset_category_ids; cross-category reads blocked at DB layer. RBAC enforced in Go middleware and DB RLS as defense-in-depth. audit_events PostgreSQL trigger: BEFORE DELETE EXECUTE PROCEDURE deny_audit_delete() raises exception for any delete attempt. TLS 1.3 at all tiers. HSTS enforced at nginx. Microsoft Sentinel receives MSAL auth failure events and API privilege escalation events via Log Analytics.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS private cluster (private API server endpoint, no public IPs) in utility Azure VNet. Internal Azure Load Balancer as ingress target; no external IP. Namespaces: api (Go pods), frontend (nginx pods), data (postgres-proxy). Pod security: non-root UID, read-only root filesystem, no privilege escalation. Microsoft Defender for Containers enabled for runtime threat detection. Azure Container Registry with geo-replication disabled (single region). Azure DevOps Pipelines (self-hosted agent pool inside utility VNet for AKS API server access): PR gate runs Go vet, golangci-lint, gosec SAST scan, unit tests 80% coverage threshold, Trivy container image scan blocking on CRITICAL CVEs. Merge to main: build and push to ACR, Helm deploy to staging, manual InfoSec approval gate, Helm upgrade to production. Rollback: Helm rollback to prior release revision. PostgreSQL backups: automated daily via Azure Flexible Server PITR with 7-day retention.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM agent sidecar in each Go API pod: traces for all HTTP handlers including Key Vault key-fetch latency, PostgreSQL query time by handler, Redis hit rate. Custom Datadog dashboard: open vulnerability count by severity, remediation task completion rate (last 30 days), exception approval queue depth, compliance snapshot generation time, Key Vault key fetch p99 latency. Microsoft Sentinel: alert rules for 5+ consecutive login failures from same user, admin role assignment events, bulk export of more than 50 vulnerability records in one API call. Azure Monitor for AKS node CPU and memory. Alertmanager: PostgreSQL replication lag > 30 seconds, Key Vault unavailability affecting encryption operations.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated Azure monthly for 200 concurrent users: AKS 3-node pool (D4s_v3) ~$350, PostgreSQL Flexible Server HA (D4s_v3) ~$290, Azure Cache for Redis C2 ~$180, Key Vault operations ~$5, Blob Storage ~$10, Log Analytics ~$30. Total ~$865/month. Scaling path: add AKS node pool auto-scale min 3 max 6 on CPU > 70%. PostgreSQL read replica for compliance dashboard queries at 500+ users.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): PostgreSQL schema with RLS policies, audit_events trigger, Azure AD MSAL integration with RBAC middleware, vulnerability advisory registration, BES asset mapping query, remediation task creation with due dates, basic task board UI, quarterly compliance snapshot PDF, Azure DevOps pipeline with gosec and Trivy gates. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Exception approval workflow with reviewer UI, evidence artifact upload to Azure Blob, patch coverage metrics dashboard with Datadog integration, Sentinel alert rules for bulk export. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Bulk advisory import from ICS-CERT RSS feed, MITRE ATT&CK for ICS tactic tagging on vulnerabilities, risk prioritization score combining CVSS and asset impact rating, automated remediation task due-date reminder notifications via Azure Communication Services.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go: all handlers use context propagation for request ID and user ID; never log or marshal encrypted column bytes in structured logs. GORM: use raw SQL for audit_events writes within transaction to avoid ORM abstraction bypassing the trigger. gosec in CI catches common Go security issues (G401 weak crypto, G501 import of weak hash). React: MSAL tokens in memory only; eslint-plugin-security in CI. All encryption/decryption operations in a single crypto package with unit tests for round-trip correctness. ADR documents for Key Vault caching strategy and RLS policy design.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Azure Key Vault unavailability blocks all vulnerability writes; mitigate with 1-hour in-memory key cache and documented degraded-mode runbook allowing read-only access during outage. Risk: Go JWKS validation cache staleness after Azure AD key rotation; refresh JWKS on 401 upstream error before returning 401 to client. Risk: PostgreSQL RLS policy bugs allow cross-category data access; mitigate with integration test suite asserting cross-category read returns 0 rows for each analyst role. Tradeoff: application-layer encryption adds ~20ms per write over transparent DB encryption but ensures key separation so DB credential breach does not expose vulnerability content.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose PostgreSQL row-level security over application-layer asset filtering as defense-in-depth per security reviewer recommendation; both layers now enforce category isolation. Selected Azure Blob for evidence artifacts over Azure Files for simpler SDK integration and per-blob access URL generation. Confirmed audit_events immutability enforced at DB trigger level not application layer to prevent privilege-escalation bypass.

metadata.notes: NERC CIP-007/010 OT vulnerability tracking web app on Azure AKS private cluster; application-layer encryption and DB trigger audit immutability are key security controls.

profile.domain: Utility OT Network Security - NERC CIP-005 Electronic Security Perimeters

project_goal.value: Implement network segmentation and electronic security perimeters across a utility's OT environment enforcing NERC CIP-005 requirements for generation and transmission control system networks, replacing manually configured firewall rules with infrastructure-as-code and automated compliance validation
project_goal.rationale: Eliminates configuration drift and provides auditable IaC for CIP-005 compliance evidence

target_users.value: Infrastructure engineers and OT security architects who define and deploy ESP boundaries, and compliance officers who audit perimeter configurations
target_users.rationale: Technical operators and auditors are the primary system users

project_class.rationale: Platform and network architecture project using Terraform and Ansible, not a user-facing application

capabilities.rationale: IaC deployment automation, compliance assessment service, and integration with SIEM for session logging

complexity_level.rationale: Spans multiple generation sites and transmission control centers with hundreds of firewall rule sets and VLAN configurations across heterogeneous OT vendor equipment

risk_level.rationale: Misconfigured ESP boundaries for generation and transmission control systems pose national infrastructure risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: ESP boundary definitions, firewall rule sets, and protected cyber asset inventories are CIP-protected confidential data

external_exposure.rationale: All tooling runs within utility internal network; no public endpoints

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC with roles: esp_engineer (define/deploy), compliance_auditor (read-only), ops_admin (manage Keycloak and tool infrastructure)
access_model.rationale: Role segregation required for CIP change management and auditor access control

feature_scope.value: IaC-defined ESP boundary configurations, Ansible playbook deployment to OT network devices, intermediate system for interactive remote access with mandatory MFA, session logging for CIP-005 R2 compliance, automated ESP compliance assessment reporting
feature_scope.rationale: Complete CIP-005 control lifecycle from definition to evidence

mvp_scope.value: Ansible playbook framework for firewall rule deployment to three ESP boundaries, intermediate jump host with Keycloak MFA, session capture to PostgreSQL, basic compliance assessment report
mvp_scope.rationale: Covers highest-risk ESP boundaries for initial NERC CIP audit evidence

security_baseline.value: All ESP boundary changes go through GitLab MR with two-person review, Ansible runs in check mode before apply, session logs encrypted at rest, Keycloak MFA for all interactive remote access through intermediate system
security_baseline.rationale: CIP-005 R2 interactive remote access controls and CIP change management requirements

frontend_stack.rationale: Infrastructure project; no browser UI layer

backend_stack.rationale: Ansible for idempotent network device configuration; Python for compliance assessment service

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for session logs and compliance assessment records
data_platform.rationale: Relational store for structured audit evidence with long retention requirements

hosting_target.value: On-premises bare-metal servers within utility OT network boundary
hosting_target.rationale: CIP data sovereignty; no cloud egress for OT configuration data

privacy_retention_policy.value: Session logs retained 3 years for CIP audit evidence; firewall rule change history retained indefinitely in GitLab
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: NERC CIP audit evidence retention requirements

future_scope.value: Automated continuous compliance monitoring with drift detection, integration with physical access control logs for CIP-006 correlation, automated ESP boundary diagram generation
future_scope.rationale: Automation roadmap to reduce manual compliance effort

constraints.value: All infrastructure must run on-premises within utility OT network; no cloud dependencies; Ansible must use vendor-supported network device modules for Cisco IOS, Juniper JunOS, and Palo Alto PAN-OS
constraints.rationale: OT network air-gap requirements and vendor support contractual requirements

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards backed by Prometheus metrics for Ansible run durations and compliance assessment job status
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing Grafana stack used across OT tooling

execution_preference.value: Change-window-driven deployments with mandatory maintenance window approval before Ansible apply runs
execution_preference.rationale: OT change management process requires pre-approved windows for network device changes

compliance_context.value: NERC CIP-005 electronic security perimeters, NERC CIP-006 physical security correlation, IEC 62443 OT security zones and conduits
compliance_context.rationale: Primary regulatory framework for OT network segmentation

project.goal: IaC-defined ESP boundaries with automated Ansible deployment, MFA jump host, and CIP-005 session logging
project.target_users: Infrastructure engineers, OT security architects, compliance auditors
project.timeline: MVP in 14 weeks for next CIP audit cycle

backend.framework: Ansible with Cisco IOS/JunOS/PAN-OS modules; FastAPI compliance assessment service
backend.api: Internal REST API for compliance report generation

security.auth: Keycloak with TOTP MFA for all interactive remote access sessions through jump host
security.change_control: GitLab MR with two-person review and Ansible check-mode dry-run before apply
security.session_logging: All IRA sessions captured via terminal session recorder to PostgreSQL

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 on-premises for session logs, compliance_assessments, esp_boundary definitions

devops.ci: GitLab CI for Ansible lint, molecule test, and check-mode validation
devops.hosting: On-premises bare-metal within utility OT network
devops.environments: lab (test environment with simulated network devices), staging (non-production ESP boundaries), production

constraints.device_support: Cisco IOS-XE, Juniper JunOS, Palo Alto PAN-OS vendor modules only

reasoner_reviews.product: CIP-005 compliance requires two distinct deliverables: first the ESP boundary definitions and automated deployment controls (R1), and second the interactive remote access intermediate system with MFA and session logging (R2). Both must produce evidence packages for NERC auditors. The infrastructure-as-code approach provides git history as change audit trail, satisfying CIP change management evidence requirements.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: ISS-001 (missing check-mode dry-run gate before Ansible apply) is the most critical gap. OT network device changes without a prior check-mode validation step risk production outages; a misconfigured ACL on a SCADA firewall can isolate generation control systems. The GitLab CI pipeline must enforce check-mode as a blocking step with no bypass path. ISS-002 (session recording storage not encrypted at rest) must also be addressed: terminal session recordings of interactive access to protected cyber assets are CIP-confidential.
reasoner_reviews.security: Keycloak TOTP MFA for the intermediate jump host satisfies CIP-005 R2.1 interactive remote access controls. Session log encryption at rest (AES-256) is required before recordings qualify as CIP-005 R2.3 evidence. Two-person GitLab MR review for all ESP boundary changes satisfies CIP change management segregation of duty requirements.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: On-premises bare-metal constraint eliminates all managed cloud services. Ansible network modules must use vendor-certified collections: cisco.ios, junipernetworks.junos, paloaltonetworks.panos. No Python dependencies with network egress at runtime.

backend.service_design: Ansible project structure: inventories/ (lab, staging, production per site), roles/ (esp_boundary, jump_host, compliance_assessment), playbooks/ (deploy_esp_boundary.yml, configure_jump_host.yml, generate_compliance_report.yml). FastAPI compliance assessment service: reads esp_boundary definitions and session_logs from PostgreSQL, computes CIP-005 control coverage matrix, generates JSON and PDF evidence packages.
backend.api_patterns: FastAPI REST endpoints: POST /assessments/trigger (starts compliance assessment job), GET /assessments/{id} (job status and results), GET /assessments/{id}/report (PDF download). Internal only, Keycloak JWT authentication.
backend.business_modules: ESP Boundary Module: Ansible role that reads VLAN definitions and firewall rule sets from YAML host_vars, validates against ESP boundary schema, and deploys to target network devices using vendor modules with idempotent apply. IRA Intermediate System: bastion host running tlog terminal session recorder, Keycloak OIDC integration for MFA, session metadata written to PostgreSQL on session open/close.
backend.backend_risks: Ansible network module failures on OT devices may leave partial configurations; implement pre-task backup of running config before any change and rollback playbook as documented runbook.

security.auth_design: Keycloak realm for OT tooling: TOTP MFA enforced for all users. Jump host (bastion) uses Keycloak OIDC PAM module; interactive SSH to protected cyber assets only permitted from authenticated jump host session. GitLab uses Keycloak SAML for engineer authentication.
security.authorization_model: Keycloak RBAC: esp_engineer role can trigger Ansible deployments via GitLab CI (MR approval required); compliance_auditor role can read esp_boundary definitions and compliance assessment reports read-only; ops_admin manages Keycloak and bare-metal infrastructure.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All IRA sessions to protected cyber assets recorded via tlog to terminal_session_records table (session_id, user_id, target_host, start_time, end_time, recording_bytes_encrypted). PostgreSQL trigger prevents deletion of terminal_session_records. GitLab commit history serves as firewall rule change audit trail. compliance_assessments table records each CIP-005 control check result per ESP boundary.
security.security_risks: tlog session recording introduces latency to interactive sessions; test maximum acceptable latency against SCADA operator workflows before production deployment.

data.entities: esp_boundaries (id, name, site_id, boundary_type ENUM generation/transmission, device_ids TEXT[], vlan_ids INT[], defined_in_git_ref TEXT, last_deployed_at). network_zones (id, esp_boundary_id, zone_name, ip_ranges CIDR[], allowed_protocols TEXT[]). firewall_rules (id, esp_boundary_id, device_id, rule_order INT, source_zone, dest_zone, protocol, port_range, action ENUM permit/deny, git_commit_ref). access_points (id, esp_boundary_id, access_point_type ENUM dial_up/direct_connect/wireless, description). interactive_remote_access_sessions (id, user_id, target_host, session_start, session_end, mfa_method, recording_encrypted BYTEA, recording_key_ref TEXT). compliance_assessments (id, esp_boundary_id, assessment_date, r1_score NUMERIC, r2_score NUMERIC, findings_json, generated_by).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on-premises bare-metal. interactive_remote_access_sessions.recording_encrypted stores AES-256-GCM encrypted tlog recordings; symmetric key stored in HashiCorp Vault on-premises. esp_boundaries and firewall_rules are read-only reference data synchronized from GitLab via post-merge hook. compliance_assessments append-only.
data.retention_and_deletion: interactive_remote_access_sessions retained 3 years minimum (CIP evidence requirement). compliance_assessments retained indefinitely. Soft-delete on esp_boundaries when decommissioned; hard delete prohibited.
data.data_risks: tlog recording size for long interactive sessions can be large; implement max session duration limit (4 hours) and compressed storage for recordings older than 30 days.

devops.deployment_topology: On-premises bare-metal: GitLab server (existing), Ansible controller node (dedicated bare-metal, no network device admin access from any other host), jump host (hardened bastion, Keycloak OIDC PAM, tlog), PostgreSQL server (dedicated, replication to standby), FastAPI compliance service (systemd service on shared Linux host), Grafana+Prometheus server (existing).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: on MR - ansible-lint, molecule test against simulated device containers, Ansible check-mode against staging inventory (blocking). On merge to main: Ansible apply runs only within approved maintenance window (CI checks maintenance_window table in PostgreSQL before proceeding, fails pipeline outside window). Manual approve stage required from second engineer before apply.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus node_exporter on all bare-metal hosts. Custom Ansible callback plugin emits metrics per playbook run: duration, changed_tasks count, failed_tasks count. Grafana dashboards: ESP boundary deployment history, compliance assessment scores per boundary over time, IRA session count per day, tlog recording storage growth. Alertmanager: Ansible apply failure fires critical, compliance assessment score below threshold fires high.
devops.devops_risks: GitLab CI runners need network access to OT network devices for check-mode and apply; runners must be provisioned inside OT DMZ with strict outbound ACLs.

ISS-001.title: Ansible apply runs lack mandatory check-mode dry-run gate in CI pipeline
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan showed Ansible apply triggered directly on merge without a preceding check-mode step in the GitLab CI pipeline. A firewall ACL misconfiguration applied to a SCADA ESP boundary without prior validation could isolate generation control systems.

history.note: Auditor flagged missing check-mode blocking gate; direct apply to OT devices is unacceptable without dry-run validation.

ISS-002.title: Terminal session recordings stored unencrypted in PostgreSQL
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan stored raw tlog recordings as BYTEA without encryption at rest. CIP-005 R2 session evidence stored unencrypted is a compliance gap and a confidentiality risk for OT interactive access activity.

history.note: Compliance reviewer flagged unencrypted IRA session storage as CIP-005 evidence confidentiality requirement failure.

focus_issues.title: Ansible apply runs lack mandatory check-mode dry-run gate in CI pipeline
focus_issues.detail: Direct Ansible apply to OT network devices without check-mode validation step could cause generation control system isolation.

focus_issues.title: Terminal session recordings stored unencrypted in PostgreSQL
focus_issues.detail: Raw tlog recording bytes stored without AES-256 encryption; CIP-005 confidentiality requirement not met.

latest_recommendations: Add ansible --check mode as a mandatory blocking CI stage before any apply stage; no bypass path should exist
Encrypt tlog recordings at application layer with AES-256-GCM before PostgreSQL insert; store symmetric key in HashiCorp Vault on-premises
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: CIP-005 Electronic Security Perimeter IaC Framework - Round 1

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity unresolved issues blocking CIP-005 compliance evidence validity

previous_audits.summary: Well-structured Ansible IaC framework with correct Keycloak MFA integration for the jump host. Two high-severity issues prevent acceptance: the CI pipeline applies Ansible changes to OT devices without a check-mode dry-run gate, creating operational risk, and terminal session recordings are stored unencrypted, failing CIP-005 confidentiality requirements.
strengths: Ansible role structure correctly separates esp_boundary, jump_host, and compliance_assessment concerns
Keycloak TOTP MFA for jump host interactive remote access satisfies CIP-005 R2.1
GitLab commit history provides change audit trail for CIP change management evidence
concerns: CI pipeline lacks check-mode dry-run blocking gate before Ansible apply on OT devices
tlog recordings stored as plaintext BYTEA in PostgreSQL violates CIP-005 session evidence confidentiality
blocking_issues: Ansible must run check-mode as a mandatory blocking CI stage before any apply stage with no bypass
IRA session recordings must be AES-256 encrypted before PostgreSQL storage
recommendations: Split CI pipeline into validate stage (ansible-lint + molecule + check-mode against staging) and deploy stage (apply, requiring manual approval and maintenance window check)
Encrypt tlog recording bytes with AES-256-GCM in the jump host session recorder before writing to PostgreSQL; store key in HashiCorp Vault

issue_updates.title: Missing check-mode gate
issue_updates.detail: Pipeline merge trigger directly calls ansible-playbook without --check; apply to production OT firewall possible without dry-run validation.

issue_updates.title: Unencrypted session recordings
issue_updates.detail: interactive_remote_access_sessions.recording column stores raw tlog bytes; no key management design presented.

previous_plan.title: CIP-005 Electronic Security Perimeter IaC Framework - Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Ansible IaC framework for deploying CIP-005 ESP boundary firewall rules and VLAN configurations to OT network devices, with a Keycloak-authenticated MFA jump host and tlog session recording for CIP-005 R2 interactive remote access compliance.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Ansible controller node deploys firewall rules and VLAN configs to Cisco IOS, Juniper JunOS, and Palo Alto devices via GitLab CI pipeline. Keycloak OIDC PAM module on jump host enforces MFA for all IRA sessions. tlog records terminal sessions to PostgreSQL.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Keycloak TOTP MFA on jump host for CIP-005 R2. GitLab MR two-person review for change management. Session recordings stored as raw BYTEA in PostgreSQL.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI: ansible-lint, molecule test, direct Ansible apply on merge to main. On-premises bare-metal for all components.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addressing two high-severity issues: ISS-001 (Ansible apply without check-mode dry-run gate) and ISS-002 (IRA session recordings unencrypted). Fix for ISS-001: restructure GitLab CI pipeline into separate validate stage (including mandatory check-mode against staging inventory as blocking step) and deploy stage (requires maintenance window check and manual second-engineer approval).
