Fix for ISS-002: encrypt tlog recording bytes with AES-256-GCM in a Python wrapper on the jump host before PostgreSQL insert; symmetric key stored in HashiCorp Vault on-premises with per-session derived key wrapping. These changes address both CIP-005 operational safety and evidence confidentiality requirements.

fix_report.action_taken: Restructured GitLab CI pipeline into three stages: (1) validate - ansible-lint, molecule, and ansible-playbook --check against staging inventory (blocking, no bypass); (2) approve - requires manual approval from second engineer not on the MR and maintenance_window_check.py script confirming current time falls within approved maintenance window in PostgreSQL; (3) deploy - ansible-playbook apply runs only if both prior stages pass. Pipeline configuration removes any fast-path to deploy stage.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No Ansible apply to OT network devices can occur without a validated check-mode run and maintenance window approval, eliminating unvalidated configuration changes to ESP boundaries.

fix_report.action_taken: Added tlog-encrypt wrapper on the jump host: when a session ends, tlog recording is read as bytes, a per-session AES-256-GCM key is derived via HashiCorp Vault transit engine (on-premises Vault), recording is encrypted, and ciphertext plus Vault key reference are written to interactive_remote_access_sessions.recording_encrypted and recording_key_ref columns. Vault transit engine provides key derivation without exporting raw key material. PostgreSQL insert trigger verifies recording_encrypted is not NULL before insert succeeds.
fix_report.expected_outcome: All IRA session recordings stored as AES-256-GCM ciphertext; plaintext never persists in PostgreSQL; key material managed by on-premises Vault satisfying CIP-005 evidence confidentiality requirement.

target_output.title: CIP-005 Electronic Security Perimeter IaC Framework
target_output.executive_summary: An infrastructure-as-code framework using Ansible to define, deploy, and validate NERC CIP-005 electronic security perimeter configurations across utility generation and transmission OT network devices. GitLab CI enforces a three-stage pipeline: Ansible check-mode dry-run validation, maintenance-window-gated manual approval, and finally device apply. Interactive remote access to protected cyber assets routes through a Keycloak MFA jump host with tlog session recording encrypted via HashiCorp Vault transit engine before PostgreSQL storage. A FastAPI compliance assessment service generates CIP-005 control evidence packages from esp_boundary definitions and session records.
target_output.architecture_overview: Five on-premises components: (1) GitLab server hosting ESP boundary IaC repositories with two-person MR review policy; (2) Ansible controller node executing playbooks against OT network devices in three-stage CI pipeline (validate with check-mode, approve with maintenance window gate, deploy); (3) hardened jump host running Keycloak OIDC PAM for MFA and tlog-encrypt session recorder writing AES-256-GCM encrypted recordings to PostgreSQL via HashiCorp Vault transit; (4) PostgreSQL 16 server storing esp_boundary definitions, session records, and compliance assessments with streaming replication to standby; (5) FastAPI compliance assessment service generating CIP-005 R1 and R2 evidence packages on demand. All components run within utility OT network with no cloud egress. Grafana+Prometheus monitors Ansible run metrics and compliance score trends.
target_output.technology_stack: IaC: Ansible 9 with cisco.ios, junipernetworks.junos, paloaltonetworks.panos certified collections. CI/CD: GitLab CI with ansible-lint, molecule, Python maintenance window check script. Session Recording: tlog on jump host with Python AES-256-GCM encryption wrapper using HashiCorp Vault transit engine. Key Management: HashiCorp Vault on-premises (transit secrets engine). Backend: Python 3.12 FastAPI compliance assessment service. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with streaming replication. Auth: Keycloak 23 with TOTP MFA and OIDC PAM module on jump host. Observability: Prometheus with custom Ansible callback plugin, Grafana.
target_output.functional_feature_map: ESP Boundary Definition: engineers define esp_boundary YAML files in GitLab specifying VLAN IDs, device IDs, zone IP ranges, firewall rule sets per vendor. CI Validation Pipeline: MR triggers ansible-lint, molecule test against simulated Cisco/Juniper/Palo Alto containers, then ansible --check against staging inventory; all must pass before merge is allowed. Deployment Pipeline: post-merge triggers maintenance window check and manual second-engineer approval stage; on approval, Ansible apply runs against target site inventory. Interactive Remote Access: jump host enforces Keycloak TOTP MFA login; sessions recorded by tlog-encrypt and stored encrypted in PostgreSQL; session metadata (user, target, duration) visible to compliance auditors. Compliance Assessment: FastAPI service reads esp_boundary definitions and session_logs; computes CIP-005 R1 control coverage (boundary definitions, access points, ingress/egress rules) and R2 coverage (IRA sessions with MFA and recording evidence); outputs JSON and PDF evidence package.
target_output.system_components: GitLab server: ESP boundary IaC repos, CI/CD pipeline definitions, two-person MR review policy enforcement. Ansible controller node (dedicated bare-metal, OT DMZ): executes playbooks via GitLab CI runner installed on the controller node; only host with admin credentials to OT network devices. Jump host (hardened bastion, OT DMZ): Keycloak OIDC PAM module for SSH auth with TOTP MFA; tlog-encrypt Python wrapper encrypts recordings via Vault transit before PostgreSQL write; no direct admin access to OT devices permitted from any other host. HashiCorp Vault (on-premises, OT network): transit secrets engine for per-session recording key derivation; audit log forwarded to Prometheus via Vault telemetry. PostgreSQL 16 primary + standby: tables for esp_boundaries, firewall_rules, access_points, interactive_remote_access_sessions, compliance_assessments; DB trigger prevents deletion of session records. FastAPI compliance service (systemd on shared Linux host): reads PostgreSQL, generates CIP-005 evidence packages. Grafana+Prometheus: Ansible run metrics, compliance scores.
target_output.workflows: ESP Boundary Change Deployment: engineer creates GitLab MR modifying YAML firewall rule files for an ESP boundary; second engineer reviews; CI pipeline stage 1 runs ansible-lint, molecule tests, and ansible-playbook --check against staging inventory (any failure blocks merge); stage 2 requires manual approval from a second engineer distinct from MR author plus maintenance_window_check.py confirms current UTC time is within an approved window record in PostgreSQL; stage 3 runs ansible-playbook apply against target site production inventory using vendor-specific modules; Ansible callback plugin emits Prometheus metrics for task changed/failed counts; post-deploy compliance assessment triggered automatically. Interactive Remote Access Session: engineer connects to jump host via SSH; Keycloak OIDC PAM module redirects to Keycloak TOTP challenge; on success, tlog session recorder starts; engineer SSHs from jump host to target protected cyber asset; on session end, tlog-encrypt wrapper reads recording bytes, calls Vault transit encrypt API with per-session derived key path, writes encrypted bytes and Vault key path reference to interactive_remote_access_sessions; PostgreSQL insert trigger verifies recording_encrypted IS NOT NULL.
target_output.data_model: esp_boundaries (id UUID PK, name TEXT, site_id UUID, boundary_type ENUM('generation','transmission'), device_ids TEXT[], vlan_ids INT[], git_commit_ref TEXT, last_deployed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, active BOOLEAN). network_zones (id UUID PK, esp_boundary_id UUID FK, zone_name TEXT, ip_ranges CIDR[], allowed_protocols TEXT[]). firewall_rules (id UUID PK, esp_boundary_id UUID FK, device_id TEXT, rule_order INT, source_zone TEXT, dest_zone TEXT, protocol TEXT, port_range TEXT, action ENUM('permit','deny'), git_commit_ref TEXT, deployed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). access_points (id UUID PK, esp_boundary_id UUID FK, access_point_type ENUM('direct_connect','wireless','dialup'), description TEXT, ingress_device TEXT). interactive_remote_access_sessions (id UUID PK, user_id UUID, target_host TEXT, session_start TIMESTAMPTZ, session_end TIMESTAMPTZ, mfa_method TEXT DEFAULT 'totp', recording_encrypted BYTEA NOT NULL, recording_key_ref TEXT NOT NULL, session_duration_seconds INT). compliance_assessments (id UUID PK, esp_boundary_id UUID FK, assessment_date TIMESTAMPTZ, r1_score NUMERIC(4,2), r2_score NUMERIC(4,2), findings_json JSONB, pdf_blob_path TEXT, generated_by UUID).
target_output.api_design: FastAPI internal REST API at /api/v1/ with Keycloak JWT auth. POST /api/v1/assessments/trigger: starts async compliance assessment job for given esp_boundary_id; returns job_id. GET /api/v1/assessments/{job_id}: returns {status, r1_score, r2_score, findings_count} or 'in_progress'. GET /api/v1/assessments/{job_id}/report: returns PDF evidence package as binary response. GET /api/v1/sessions?boundary_id={id}&from={date}&to={date}: returns IRA session metadata for auditor review (no recording bytes in response). GET /api/v1/esp-boundaries: list all active boundaries with last deploy status. All endpoints require compliance_auditor or ops_admin role.
target_output.security_and_compliance: NERC CIP-005 R1 (ESP boundary): all boundary definitions version-controlled in GitLab with two-person review; Ansible check-mode mandatory before apply; automated compliance assessment computes R1 coverage score per boundary. CIP-005 R2 (interactive remote access): all IRA to protected cyber assets routed through Keycloak MFA jump host; tlog-encrypt records sessions with AES-256-GCM encryption via Vault transit; PostgreSQL trigger enforces encrypted storage (NOT NULL on recording_encrypted). Keycloak TOTP MFA for all tool users. GitLab two-person MR review for change management segregation of duty. Maintenance window gate prevents unplanned OT network changes. Vault audit log forwarded to Prometheus for secret access monitoring.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: On-premises bare-metal within utility OT network. All hosts: RHEL 8, CIS Level 2 hardened, SELinux enforcing. GitLab CI pipeline three stages: (1) Validate - GitLab runner on Ansible controller node runs ansible-lint, molecule, ansible --check against staging; (2) Approve - maintenance_window_check.py queries PostgreSQL for active window covering current UTC time, blocks if outside window; manual approval required from second engineer; (3) Deploy - ansible-playbook apply with full task output logged to PostgreSQL ansible_run_logs table; post-deploy compliance assessment trigger via FastAPI. PostgreSQL streaming replication to standby; pg_basebackup nightly to dedicated backup server, 90-day retention. HashiCorp Vault initialized with Shamir secret sharing (3-of-5 key holders); Vault auto-unseal not used given on-premises constraint; documented rekey runbook.
target_output.observability: Prometheus node_exporter on all bare-metal hosts plus custom Ansible callback plugin emitting per-playbook metrics: ansible_run_duration_seconds, ansible_changed_tasks_total, ansible_failed_tasks_total labeled by playbook and site. Vault telemetry emitting secret_access_count by path to Prometheus. Grafana dashboards: ESP boundary deployment history by site (last 90 days), compliance assessment R1/R2 scores per boundary over time, IRA session count and duration distribution per week, Ansible run failure rate. Alertmanager: Ansible apply failure on production site fires critical (OncallOps+Security); compliance assessment R1 or R2 score below 0.85 fires high; Vault seal event fires critical.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: On-premises infrastructure reuses existing bare-metal capacity for most components. New hardware: dedicated Ansible controller node (~$4K one-time), jump host bare-metal (~$3K), HashiCorp Vault server (~$4K), PostgreSQL standby server (if not already provisioned). HashiCorp Vault Enterprise license cost if required for on-premises support; Community Edition viable for transit engine at this scale. Ansible scales to additional sites by adding site-specific inventory files and GitLab CI matrix jobs per site.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (14 weeks): Ansible role framework for three highest-risk ESP boundaries (generation site A, transmission control center A, transmission control center B), GitLab CI three-stage pipeline with check-mode gate and maintenance window approval, Keycloak MFA jump host with tlog-encrypt and Vault transit integration, PostgreSQL schema with CIP-evidence tables and triggers, basic FastAPI compliance assessment for CIP-005 R1 and R2 scores. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Remaining ESP boundaries across all generation and transmission sites, Grafana compliance score dashboards, automated post-deploy compliance assessment trigger, GitLab compliance report download UI via FastAPI PDF endpoint. Phase 3 (8 weeks): Continuous compliance drift detection comparing deployed device configs to IaC definitions via daily Ansible check-mode runs, integration with physical access logs for CIP-006 correlation, automated ESP boundary topology diagram generation from esp_boundaries table.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Ansible playbooks must be idempotent and pass molecule tests against simulated device containers before MR approval. Ansible roles must include assert tasks at start to validate required variables are defined. Python maintenance_window_check.py has unit tests covering edge cases (boundary UTC times, DST transitions). FastAPI compliance service uses SQLAlchemy with explicit transactions; no ORM lazy loading on compliance_assessments joins to avoid N+1 on large boundary inventories. Vault transit key paths follow naming convention: osp/ira/{session_id}; key versions rotated every 90 days via Vault key rotation policy.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HashiCorp Vault seal event during IRA session prevents recording encryption and blocks new sessions; mitigate with Vault HA (two Vault nodes with Raft storage), Alertmanager critical on seal event, and documented emergency break-glass procedure allowing sessions with manual paper-based logging. Risk: Ansible check-mode does not perfectly simulate all OT vendor module behaviors; validate check-mode accuracy against each vendor module in lab before production. Risk: per-session Vault transit key derivation adds ~50ms latency to session start on the jump host; acceptable given OT operator workflow. Tradeoff: on-premises HashiCorp Vault over cloud KMS required by data sovereignty constraint; adds operational burden for seal/unseal and rekey procedures.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose HashiCorp Vault transit engine over local Python encryption with static key because Vault provides key versioning and rotation without re-encrypting stored ciphertext, satisfying CIP key management best practices. Selected PostgreSQL insert trigger to enforce recording_encrypted NOT NULL over application-level check because DB-layer constraint cannot be bypassed by future code changes. Confirmed molecule with network device simulators (network-tools containers) over live staging devices for CI validate stage to avoid CI runner needing OT device admin credentials.

metadata.notes: NERC CIP-005 OT ESP IaC framework; round 2 adds mandatory Ansible check-mode gate and Vault-encrypted IRA session recordings.

profile.domain: Grid OT Data Modernization - SCADA Historian Migration

project_goal.value: Modernize ingestion of SCADA historian data from legacy OSIsoft PI servers into a cloud-native time-series platform, enabling grid operations analytics teams to deprecate on-premise historian infrastructure over an 18-month migration
project_goal.rationale: On-premise PI historian EOL and cloud analytics modernization roadmap

target_users.value: Grid operations analytics engineers and data platform teams consuming SCADA historian time-series data for grid analytics workloads
target_users.rationale: Internal data consumers, not end users

project_class.rationale: Containerized ingestion pipeline, no user-facing UI

capabilities.rationale: Time-series data ingestion, backfill batch jobs, PI server integration, containerized deployment

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-PI-server connectivity, deduplication across backfill windows, schema normalization across heterogeneous tag namespaces

risk_level.rationale: Internal OT operational data; data loss in migration window has analytics impact but no grid control risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: SCADA tag data is internal operational data, not personal or financial

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline runs entirely within utility internal network and GCP private VPC

access_model.value: Custom JWT for pipeline service accounts; no end-user login required
access_model.rationale: Machine-to-machine pipeline authentication only

feature_scope.value: PI server connector with configurable backfill windows, tag metadata normalization to canonical schema, InfluxDB write with deduplication, migration batch tracking, data quality metrics, CI/CD for connector configuration changes
feature_scope.rationale: Full migration pipeline feature set for 18-month deprecation timeline

mvp_scope.value: Single PI server connector with configurable tag list, 30-day backfill window, InfluxDB write with basic deduplication check, GitHub Actions CI/CD
mvp_scope.rationale: Proves migration pipeline approach before scaling to remaining PI servers

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for PI server API connections, service account JWT for InfluxDB writes, GCP VPC private networking for GKE workloads
security_baseline.rationale: Minimum security controls for internal data pipeline

frontend_stack.rationale: Dashboard for monitoring migration progress

backend_stack.rationale: Kafka for decoupling PI connector from InfluxDB writer; Python for connector and transformer logic

data_platform.value: InfluxDB for time-series writes, PostgreSQL for migration batch state tracking
data_platform.rationale: InfluxDB for operational time-series; PostgreSQL for structured migration metadata

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot in GCP
hosting_target.rationale: GCP cloud-native destination for historian modernization

privacy_retention_policy.value: SCADA tag data retained 5 years in InfluxDB for analytics; raw PI extract files retained 90 days before deletion
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Grid operations analytics retention requirements

future_scope.value: Real-time streaming ingestion via PI Web API event frames, additional PI server connectors, automated tag mapping discovery
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 and 3 of historian modernization roadmap

constraints.value: PI server connections must use PI Web API (not AF SDK) to avoid Windows-only library; migration must not degrade PI server query performance for live SCADA operations
constraints.rationale: PI Web API is cross-platform; live SCADA operations take priority over migration workloads

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards backed by Prometheus metrics; alerts for ingestion job failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing Grafana stack used for grid analytics platform

execution_preference.value: Kanban with weekly migrations batches shipping incrementally
execution_preference.rationale: Incremental migration approach reduces risk of data gap

project.goal: Migrate SCADA historian time-series from OSIsoft PI servers to InfluxDB on GKE
project.target_users: Grid analytics engineers and data platform teams
project.timeline: MVP in 8 weeks, full migration in 18 months

backend.framework: Kafka Streams for pipeline, custom PI Web API client

security.auth: Service account JWT for InfluxDB and PostgreSQL
security.transport: TLS 1.3 for PI Web API connections

data.db: InfluxDB for time-series, PostgreSQL for migration batch state
data.batch: Kubernetes CronJobs for scheduled backfill windows

constraints.pi_api: PI Web API only, no AF SDK
constraints.pi_load: Rate-limit PI queries to avoid SCADA impact

open_questions.deduplication: How to handle overlapping backfill windows producing duplicate readings?

reasoner_reviews.product: The 18-month migration timeline requires a parallel-running approach where new SCADA data writes to both legacy PI and new InfluxDB until validation confirms equivalence. Tag metadata normalization is the most complex requirement because PI tag namespaces vary significantly across substations and generation sites. The data quality metrics capability will be essential for validating migration completeness before PI decommission milestones.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Kafka between PI connector and InfluxDB writer is justified for decoupling and retry resilience during InfluxDB write failures, but adds operational complexity. Deduplication logic must handle overlapping backfill windows where the same timestamp-tag combination is written more than once; InfluxDB's upsert behavior on identical timestamps within the same measurement handles this natively but connector logic must normalize timestamps to nanosecond precision consistently.
reasoner_reviews.security: PI Web API connections must use certificate-based authentication against PI server, not basic auth. InfluxDB tokens should be scoped to the specific bucket being written. GKE workload identity should be used instead of static service account key files for GCP resource access.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The plan has a significant structural flaw: the frontend_stack contract field specifies React 18 for a data_pipeline project with no frontend capability in the capabilities list. A data pipeline does not have a browser UI; any migration monitoring dashboard should be a separate project or a Grafana dashboard, not a React app embedded in this architecture. The api_design section describes user-facing CRUD endpoints inconsistent with a batch data pipeline architecture and should instead describe the internal job trigger and status API. The data_model references a 'users' entity which has no place in a SCADA historian migration pipeline.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 18-month migration timeline requires parallel writes to both legacy OSIsoft PI and new InfluxDB until the validation period confirms data fidelity — no hard cutover. OT network segmentation restricts the Kafka broker to a DMZ subnet; PI Web API connections must traverse a unidirectional data diode, constraining the connector to read-only polling rather than PI event-driven subscriptions. Single AWS region deployment in eu-central-1 for German grid operator data sovereignty requirements.

backend.service_design: Three pipeline components as separate GKE workloads: (1) pi_connector: Python service polling PI Web API for tag values in configurable time windows, publishing to Kafka topic scada-raw; (2) tag_transformer: Kafka consumer normalizing PI tag paths to canonical schema and publishing to scada-normalized topic; (3) influxdb_writer: Kafka consumer writing normalized records to InfluxDB with upsert semantics. Separate migration_tracker service updating PostgreSQL migration_batches table.
backend.background_jobs: Kubernetes CronJob: backfill_scheduler runs daily at 02:00 UTC, reads migration_batches for uncompleted windows, triggers pi_connector for each pending window via Kafka control message. Separate nightly data_quality_check CronJob compares InfluxDB record counts against PI Web API counts for completed windows.
backend.business_modules: PI Web API client: rate-limited aiohttp calls to PI server REST endpoints, pagination via next-link header, tag value streaming in 1-hour chunks. Tag mapper: reads tag_mappings YAML defining canonical name transformations from PI tag path to InfluxDB measurement and field key.
backend.backend_risks: PI Web API rate limiting is configurable but requires tuning per PI server to avoid impacting live SCADA queries; default starting rate of 100 req/min with monitoring to increase. Kafka consumer lag monitoring essential to detect influxdb_writer falling behind.

security.auth_design: PI Web API: certificate-based auth using service account certificate stored in GKE Secret. InfluxDB: token scoped to single bucket, stored in GKE Secret. GCP resources (GCS, Artifact Registry): GKE Workload Identity instead of static service account key files.
security.secrets_management: Kubernetes Secrets for PI certificate, InfluxDB token. Rotation: annual PI certificate renewal, quarterly InfluxDB token rotation via documented runbook.
security.security_risks: PI server certificate expiry not monitored automatically; add Prometheus blackbox prober checking PI Web API health including certificate expiry with 30-day warning alert.

data.entities: historian_tags (id, pi_server_id, pi_tag_path, canonical_name, measurement_name, field_key, tag_metadata_json, last_synced_at), scada_readings (measurement: canonical_measurement_name, tag: pi_server, field: value FLOAT, timestamp NANOSECONDS stored in InfluxDB), migration_batches (id, pi_server_id, start_time, end_time, status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed/failed, tags_migrated INT, rows_migrated BIGINT, started_at, completed_at), pi_server_connections (id, server_name, base_url, auth_cert_secret_ref, rate_limit_per_min, active), data_quality_metrics (id, migration_batch_id, pi_record_count, influx_record_count, match_pct, checked_at).
data.storage_design: InfluxDB bucket 'scada-historian' with 5-year retention policy, shard group duration 7 days. Tag set: pi_server, site_code. Field set: value (float64). PostgreSQL: migration_batches and historian_tags tables for tracking state.
data.migration_notes: Backfill strategy: 30-day rolling window chunks to limit PI Web API memory footprint per request. After each chunk completes, data_quality_check runs and marks batch completed only when match_pct >= 99.5%.
data.data_risks: PI tag timestamp precision varies (some PI servers store at millisecond, some at second resolution); normalize all timestamps to nanosecond precision in tag_transformer to prevent false duplicate detection in InfluxDB.

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot in GCP europe-west1 region (utility data residency requirement). Separate namespaces: pipeline (pi_connector, tag_transformer, influxdb_writer), jobs (CronJobs), data (InfluxDB, PostgreSQL via Cloud SQL). Private GKE cluster with VPC Service Controls.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR - pytest, mypy, ruff, docker build. On merge to main - docker push Artifact Registry, helm upgrade to staging, integration test against sandboxed PI data replay environment using pi-replay container, promote to production.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus: Kafka consumer lag per topic and consumer group, InfluxDB write rate and error rate, PI Web API request duration and error rate, migration_batches completion rate. Grafana: migration progress dashboard showing % of total tag-hours migrated, backfill window completion timeline, data quality match percentages. Alertmanager: Kafka consumer lag > 10K messages fires warning, influxdb_writer error rate > 1% fires critical.
devops.devops_risks: PI connector to PI server crosses network boundary between GCP VPC and utility on-premise network; requires Cloud VPN or Dedicated Interconnect; test latency impact on PI Web API streaming.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a SCADA historian migration pipeline from OSIsoft PI to InfluxDB on GKE Autopilot. Key architectural decisions: Kafka for decoupling PI connector from InfluxDB writer with consumer lag monitoring, PI Web API (not AF SDK) per constraint, rate-limited aiohttp client to protect live SCADA operations, InfluxDB upsert semantics for deduplication. Note: this plan contains intentional flaws for training purposes - the frontend_stack contract specifies React 18 which has no place in a data pipeline project, the data_model references a 'users' entity irrelevant to SCADA data migration, and the api_design describes user-facing CRUD endpoints misaligned with a batch pipeline architecture. These represent contract-plan inconsistency and domain confusion issues that an auditor should flag.
target_output.title: SCADA Historian Migration Pipeline: OSIsoft PI to InfluxDB on GKE
target_output.executive_summary: A containerized data pipeline on GKE Autopilot migrating SCADA time-series data from legacy OSIsoft PI historian servers to a cloud-native InfluxDB deployment over an 18-month schedule. Three Kafka-decoupled GKE workloads handle PI Web API extraction, tag schema normalization, and InfluxDB write with deduplication. Kubernetes CronJobs drive backfill window scheduling and data quality validation. The pipeline uses rate-limited PI Web API calls to protect live SCADA operations and validates migration completeness via record count comparison before marking batches complete.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven pipeline on GKE Autopilot with three Kafka-connected stages: pi_connector streams tag values from PI Web API into scada-raw topic, tag_transformer normalizes PI tag paths to canonical measurement names and publishes to scada-normalized, influxdb_writer consumes normalized records and writes to InfluxDB using upsert semantics. PostgreSQL on Cloud SQL tracks migration_batches state and historian_tags metadata. A React 18 dashboard (note: inconsistent with data_pipeline project class having no frontend capability) is included for monitoring migration progress. GitHub Actions CI with PI replay sandbox integration tests drives deployment to GKE via Helm. Cloud VPN connects GKE VPC to on-premises PI servers.
target_output.technology_stack: Pipeline: Python 3.12, Apache Kafka (Confluent Cloud), aiokafka consumer/producer, aiohttp PI Web API client. Data: InfluxDB OSS for time-series, Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 for migration state. UI: React 18 with TypeScript and Grafana (note: React UI is not applicable for this data_pipeline project class). Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot, GCP europe-west1, Cloud VPN, Artifact Registry. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with pytest, mypy, ruff, pi-replay integration tests. Observability: Prometheus, Grafana.
target_output.functional_feature_map: PI Connector: rate-limited aiohttp polling of PI Web API tag values in configurable time-window chunks, publishing to Kafka scada-raw topic with PI server metadata. Tag Transformer: canonical name mapping from PI tag path to InfluxDB measurement and field key using historian_tags YAML mapping, timestamp normalization to nanosecond precision. InfluxDB Writer: Kafka consumer writing to InfluxDB bucket with upsert on duplicate timestamps. Backfill Scheduler CronJob: manages migration_batches windows per PI server. Data Quality Check CronJob: compares PI Web API record counts against InfluxDB record counts for completed windows, marks batch complete when match_pct >= 99.5%. User Management (note: not applicable for this pipeline - no users): admin CRUD for user accounts and permissions.
target_output.system_components: pi_connector GKE Deployment (1 replica per PI server): aiohttp PI Web API client with configurable rate limit and window chunking. tag_transformer GKE Deployment (2 replicas): stateless Kafka consumer for tag normalization. influxdb_writer GKE Deployment (2 replicas): Kafka consumer writing to InfluxDB. backfill_scheduler CronJob: daily 02:00 UTC window assignment. data_quality_check CronJob: post-batch record count comparison. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL: migration_batches, historian_tags, pi_server_connections. InfluxDB OSS: scada-historian bucket 5-year retention. Apache Kafka (Confluent Cloud): scada-raw and scada-normalized topics. Cloud VPN: GKE VPC to on-premises PI server network.
target_output.workflows: Backfill Migration Batch: backfill_scheduler reads migration_batches for pending windows, sends Kafka control message to pi_connector; pi_connector fetches PI tag values in 1-hour sub-chunks via PI Web API (100 req/min rate limit), publishes to scada-raw; tag_transformer consumes scada-raw, maps PI tag path to canonical name, normalizes timestamp to nanosecond, publishes to scada-normalized; influxdb_writer consumes scada-normalized, writes to InfluxDB with upsert; on completion, updates migration_batches status to completed. Data Quality Validation: data_quality_check CronJob queries PI Web API for record count in completed window, queries InfluxDB COUNT for same window and tags; if match_pct < 99.5% marks batch as needs_rerun and alerts Prometheus.
target_output.data_model: historian_tags (id UUID PK, pi_server_id UUID FK, pi_tag_path TEXT NOT NULL, canonical_name TEXT NOT NULL, measurement_name TEXT NOT NULL, field_key TEXT DEFAULT 'value', tag_metadata_json JSONB, last_synced_at TIMESTAMPTZ). users (id UUID PK, username TEXT, email TEXT, role TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ) -- note: this entity is not applicable to a SCADA historian migration pipeline and represents a domain modeling error. scada_readings stored in InfluxDB: measurement=canonical_measurement_name, tags={pi_server, site_code}, fields={value FLOAT64}, timestamp NANOSECONDS. migration_batches (id UUID PK, pi_server_id UUID FK, start_time TIMESTAMPTZ, end_time TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM('pending','in_progress','completed','failed','needs_rerun'), tags_migrated INT, rows_migrated BIGINT, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). data_quality_metrics (id UUID PK, migration_batch_id UUID FK, pi_record_count BIGINT, influx_record_count BIGINT, match_pct NUMERIC(5,2), checked_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: Internal REST API at /api/v1/ -- note: this service is a data pipeline and the following endpoints describe user-facing CRUD which is architecturally inconsistent with this project class. GET /api/v1/users: list all registered users with pagination. POST /api/v1/users: create new user account. PUT /api/v1/users/{id}: update user profile. DELETE /api/v1/users/{id}: delete user. POST /api/v1/orders: create new order record. GET /api/v1/orders: list orders. The domain-appropriate API should instead be: POST /api/v1/batches/trigger (trigger backfill for a PI server and time window), GET /api/v1/batches/{id} (batch status), GET /api/v1/quality/{batch_id} (data quality match results), GET /api/v1/tags/{pi_server_id} (historian tag inventory).
target_output.security_and_compliance: PI Web API connections use certificate-based auth with service account certificate in GKE Secret. InfluxDB write token scoped to scada-historian bucket, stored in GKE Secret, rotated quarterly. GKE Workload Identity for GCP resource access instead of static service account key files. TLS 1.3 for all inter-service communication. GKE private cluster with no public API server endpoint. GDPR compliance required for all user data (note: GDPR is not applicable to this internal SCADA historian pipeline containing no personal data; this represents an incorrect compliance mapping). VPC Service Controls restrict Cloud SQL and InfluxDB to GKE VPC.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot in GCP europe-west1. Cloud VPN HA (99.99% SLA) connecting GKE VPC to on-premises PI server network. Artifact Registry for container images. GitHub Actions CI: on PR run pytest with 80% coverage, mypy, ruff; build docker; integration test pi_connector against pi-replay sandbox container. On merge to main: push to Artifact Registry, helm upgrade to staging, run integration tests, promote to production. Rollback: helm rollback to prior chart revision. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL: automated daily backups with 7-day PITR retention. InfluxDB: nightly snapshot to GCS bucket with 90-day retention.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scraping all three pipeline service pods: kafka_consumer_lag_messages (per topic and consumer group, alert if > 10K for influxdb_writer), pi_webapi_request_duration_seconds (histogram by pi_server_id), influxdb_write_rate and influxdb_write_errors_total, migration_batch_completion_rate. Grafana dashboards: total tag-hours migrated vs target (migration burn-down chart), backfill window completion timeline per PI server, data quality match percentage per batch, Kafka consumer lag per consumer group. Alertmanager: influxdb_writer Kafka lag > 10K fires warning; data quality match < 99.5% fires high; pi_connector error rate > 2% fires high.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot: ~$80-120/month for pipeline pods at this tag volume. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL db-standard-2: ~$100/month. Confluent Cloud Kafka basic cluster: ~$60/month. InfluxDB OSS self-managed in GKE (persistent volume): ~$50/month storage at 5M readings/day for 5 years. Cloud VPN: ~$36/month. Cloud Storage for InfluxDB backups: ~$10/month. Total ~$340-380/month. Scaling: additional pi_connector replicas per PI server, influxdb_writer replicas scaled on Kafka consumer lag.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): single PI server connector with configurable tag list, 30-day backfill window, Kafka pipeline with scada-raw and scada-normalized topics, InfluxDB writer with upsert, PostgreSQL migration_batches tracking, data_quality_check CronJob, GitHub Actions CI with pi-replay integration tests, Grafana migration burn-down dashboard. Phase 2 (6 weeks): additional PI server connectors (configurable per-server rate limits), automated tag mapping discovery from PI tag metadata, data quality alerting for match < 99.5%. Phase 3 (8 weeks): real-time streaming via PI Web API event frames subscription replacing batch polling for current data, automated PI decommission validation report, historical data archival policy enforcement.
target_output.development_guidelines: PI Web API client must use aiohttp with configurable rate limiter (token bucket per PI server) to prevent SCADA impact. All Kafka producers use exactly-once semantics with transaction IDs per pi_connector instance. Tag mapping YAML files version-controlled in repository with CI validation step. pytest fixtures provide pi-replay container for integration tests without live PI server access. mypy strict mode; no Any types in PI Web API client or InfluxDB writer modules.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Cloud VPN latency to on-premise PI servers impacts batch throughput; benchmark PI Web API streaming latency over VPN before committing to backfill window size targets. Risk: PI server rate limit misconfiguration causes live SCADA query degradation; start at 50 req/min and increase incrementally with monitoring. Risk: InfluxDB OSS in GKE lacks managed backup automation unlike InfluxDB Cloud; mitigate with nightly snapshot CronJob to GCS. Tradeoff: Confluent Cloud Kafka over self-managed Kafka for operational simplicity at cost of ~$60/month.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose PI Web API over AF SDK per constraint (cross-platform Python compatibility). Selected InfluxDB upsert semantics over explicit deduplication query to handle overlapping backfill windows; InfluxDB natively overwrites same timestamp/tag combination in same measurement. Confirmed 99.5% match threshold for data quality check balancing migration completeness against acceptable rounding differences in PI value storage precision.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed: frontend_stack contract specifies React for a no-frontend data pipeline, data_model includes irrelevant users entity, api_design describes user CRUD instead of pipeline control endpoints, security section incorrectly references GDPR for internal SCADA data.

profile.domain: Energy Transmission - FERC Interconnection Queue Management

project_goal.value: Provide independent power producers a self-service portal to submit, track, and manage interconnection study requests with transmission operators, backed by a structured API consumed by utility planning systems
project_goal.rationale: Replaces email-based interconnection request submission with structured portal supporting FERC Order 2023 queue reform compliance

target_users.value: Independent power producers (project developers) submitting study requests, and transmission operators managing study queues and uploading completed reports
target_users.rationale: Two distinct user roles with separate portal workflows and API access patterns

project_class.rationale: Developer self-service portal with structured backend API consumed by utility systems

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, public_api, integrations, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Self-service developer UI, utility planning system API, Auth0 auth, S3 for study reports, admin queue management

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-tenant with two distinct user roles, webhook delivery to utility systems, file upload for study reports, milestone tracking with FERC deadline enforcement

risk_level.rationale: Confidential project developer information and interconnection study data; financial and regulatory risk from deadline mismanagement

data_sensitivity.rationale: Project developer trade secrets and interconnection study cost allocations are confidential under FERC rules

external_exposure.rationale: Portal accessible over internet but requires Auth0 authentication; no public unauthenticated access to study data

access_model.value: Auth0 with organization-based multi-tenancy: project_developer and transmission_operator roles; admin role for queue management
access_model.rationale: Organization isolation ensures developers cannot access other developers' study data

feature_scope.value: Interconnection request submission with POI details, queue position assignment, study report upload by transmission operators, milestone deadline tracking, webhook notifications to developer project management systems, admin queue management dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Complete FERC Order 2023 interconnection queue reform workflow

mvp_scope.value: Developer request submission, queue assignment, study report upload and download, milestone tracking, Auth0 multi-tenant auth
mvp_scope.rationale: Core queue reform compliance workflow for initial FERC Order 2023 compliance deadline

security_baseline.value: Auth0 organization isolation for multi-tenancy, TLS 1.3, S3 pre-signed URLs for study report access, Fastify rate limiting, row-level access control in PostgreSQL for study data isolation
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential data requires multi-tenant isolation and signed URL access to prevent unauthorized study document access

frontend_stack.rationale: SSR for SEO on public-facing pages; team Next.js expertise

backend_stack.rationale: Fastify performance and schema validation; consistent TypeScript end-to-end with Next.js frontend

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on AWS RDS, Redis 7 for session cache and webhook queue
data_platform.rationale: RDS PostgreSQL for ACID compliance on study queue state; Redis for webhook retry queue

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with auto-scaling; us-east-1 for proximity to FERC regulatory environment

privacy_retention_policy.value: Interconnection study records retained 7 years per FERC record-keeping requirements; study reports (PDFs) retained in S3 for 7 years with Glacier transition after 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: FERC Order 2023 mandates record retention for interconnection queue proceedings

future_scope.value: Integration with FERC eTariff filing system for automatic queue position publication, batch study coordination tools for cluster interconnection under FERC Order 2023
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap for full FERC Order 2023 cluster study compliance

constraints.value: Must support utility planning system webhook endpoints with at-least-once delivery; study report PDFs may be up to 500 MB; FERC milestone deadlines are calendar-strict and must trigger notifications even during system maintenance windows
constraints.rationale: Reliability requirements driven by FERC regulatory deadline compliance

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with traces, custom metrics for queue depth and milestone compliance rate, PagerDuty for milestone deadline alerts
observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate APM standard; milestone deadline alerting critical for FERC compliance

execution_preference.value: Agile sprints with 2-week iterations, FERC compliance milestone gates
execution_preference.rationale: Regulatory deadline drives release gates

compliance_context.value: FERC Order 2023 interconnection queue reform, FERC Order 845 transparency requirements
compliance_context.rationale: Primary regulatory framework governing interconnection study queue management

project.goal: Self-service portal for interconnection study request management with structured API for utility system integration
project.target_users: Project developers and transmission operators
project.timeline: MVP 12 weeks, full feature set 20 weeks

frontend.styling: Tailwind CSS with shadcn/ui component library
frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for UI state

security.auth: Auth0 with organization-based multi-tenancy and RBAC
security.files: S3 pre-signed URLs with 15-minute expiry for study report access

data.cache: Redis 7 (ElastiCache) for session cache and webhook retry queue

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with ESLint, TypeScript check, Jest, Playwright E2E

constraints.webhooks: At-least-once delivery with exponential backoff retry
constraints.milestones: FERC deadline notifications must fire even during maintenance

reasoner_reviews.product: Round 4 is addressing the final convergence issues. The webhook delivery reliability (ISS-003) and the large file upload UX for study reports (ISS-004) are the remaining blockers. Webhook at-least-once delivery is business-critical because utility planning systems treat missed notifications as missing study queue events under FERC Order 2023. The 500 MB study report upload must use S3 multipart upload directly from browser via pre-signed URL to avoid routing large files through the Fastify API tier.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: ISS-003 fix: Redis Bull queue with exponential backoff and dead-letter queue for failed webhook deliveries, with manual retry UI in admin panel. ISS-004 fix: client-side S3 multipart upload with pre-signed part URLs generated by Fastify endpoint; ECS Fargate does not handle 500 MB file upload buffering reliably. Both fixes are targeted changes to the webhook delivery module and file upload flow without redesigning the core interconnection request data model which has been stable since round 2.
reasoner_reviews.security: S3 pre-signed multipart upload part URLs must be scoped to the specific study report S3 key prefix for the organization. Completed multipart upload must be confirmed by the Fastify API before study_reports record is marked available. Webhook endpoint validation using HMAC-SHA256 signature header prevents spoofed delivery confirmations.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: FERC milestone deadline notifications must use a separate ECS scheduled task independent of the main API tier to guarantee firing during maintenance windows. This was addressed in round 3 but the admin manual retry for failed webhooks (ISS-003 partial fix) remains pending.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 App Router with route groups: (developer) for project developer portal, (operator) for transmission operator queue management, (admin) for admin queue dashboard. Auth0 session via next-auth v5. Server components for study queue list pages; client components for file upload progress and milestone tracking.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Developer Portal: /requests/new (multi-step POI submission wizard), /requests (queue position and milestone status list), /requests/{id} (request detail with study report download). Operator Portal: /queue (pending requests with assignment controls), /queue/{id}/upload-report (S3 multipart upload with progress indicator), /milestones (FERC deadline calendar). Admin Panel: /admin/queue (queue position management), /admin/webhooks (failed delivery retry UI), /admin/organizations (org and user management).
frontend.state_management: React Query for all server state with optimistic updates on milestone status changes. Zustand for file upload state (part progress, completion). No Redux.
frontend.frontend_risks: S3 multipart upload requires client-side part size management (minimum 5 MB per part per AWS spec); implement in useMultipartUpload hook with progress state per part.

backend.service_design: Fastify application with plugins: auth0-jwt-verify (middleware validating Auth0 JWT and extracting organization_id and role), rls-context (sets PostgreSQL session variable app.current_org_id for row-level security), rate-limiter (per-org per-endpoint), webhook-emitter (Redis Bull producer). Route modules: /requests, /queue, /reports, /webhooks-admin, /milestones.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.0 at /api/v1/. Zod schema validation on all request bodies. Cursor pagination on list endpoints. Webhook delivery: POST /api/v1/webhooks/endpoints (register URL and secret), and async delivery via Bull queue.
backend.business_modules: Interconnection Request Module: validates POI coordinates against allowed_interconnection_zones lookup, computes queue_position as MAX(queue_position)+1 within study_queues for the operator organization. Study Report Module: generates S3 pre-signed multipart upload part URLs via AWS SDK, records upload_parts state in Redis during upload, confirms completed multipart assembly on finalize. Milestone Engine: reads FERC Order 2023 deadline rules (configurable in milestone_templates table) and computes absolute deadlines per interconnection_request.submitted_at.
backend.backend_risks: Bull queue Redis connection loss causes webhook delivery halt without alerting; add Bull queue health check to Datadog custom metric and alert on zero delivery throughput for > 10 minutes.

security.auth_design: Auth0 organizations for multi-tenant isolation. JWT claims include organization_id, role (project_developer/transmission_operator/admin). Fastify JWT middleware extracts org and role on every request and sets PostgreSQL SET LOCAL app.current_org_id for RLS. Admin role additionally validated against hardcoded allowed admin org list.
security.authorization_model: PostgreSQL RLS policies: interconnection_requests SELECT/UPDATE WHERE organization_id = current_setting('app.current_org_id'); study_reports SELECT WHERE linked request organization_id matches. Transmission operators see all requests they are assigned to via study_queues.operator_org_id. Webhook endpoints owned by their registering organization.
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager for Auth0 client secret, S3 HMAC signing key, PostgreSQL credentials. ECS task role assumes IAM role for S3 pre-signed URL generation and Secrets Manager access.
security.security_risks: S3 multipart upload abort not called for abandoned uploads; implement 24-hour S3 lifecycle rule to abort incomplete multipart uploads on study-reports bucket.

data.entities: project_developers (id, organization_id, name, contact_email, auth0_org_id). transmission_operators (id, organization_id, name, planning_region). interconnection_requests (id, developer_org_id, operator_org_id, project_name, poi_latitude, poi_longitude, capacity_mw, study_type ENUM feasibility/system_impact/facilities, queue_position, status, submitted_at, ferc_filing_ref). study_queues (id, operator_org_id, queue_name, max_concurrent_studies). study_reports (id, request_id, report_type, s3_key, file_size_bytes, uploaded_by_id, available_at). milestone_deadlines (id, request_id, milestone_type, due_date, completed_at, notification_sent_at). webhook_endpoints (id, organization_id, url, hmac_secret_encrypted, events TEXT[], active). webhook_deliveries (id, endpoint_id, event_type, payload_json, status, attempts, next_retry_at, last_error).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ: all tables with row-level security. S3 bucket 'interconnection-study-reports': server-side encryption (SSE-S3), versioning enabled, lifecycle rule aborting incomplete multiparts after 24 hours, Glacier transition at 2 years, deletion at 7 years. ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster mode disabled (single primary): Bull queue for webhook deliveries, session cache.
data.retention_and_deletion: interconnection_requests soft-deleted only (withdrawn_at timestamp). study_reports S3 objects 7-year retention via lifecycle. PostgreSQL data retained 7 years per FERC Order 2023. webhook_deliveries older than 90 days purged by nightly cleanup job.
data.data_risks: Queue position gaps if concurrent requests assign same queue_position; use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on study_queues.last_position row in request submission transaction to serialize position assignment.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate: api service (2 tasks, auto-scale 2-8 on CPU 60%), webhook-worker service (2 tasks, Bull queue workers), milestone-notifier scheduled task (runs every 5 minutes, independent of api service). ALB with HTTPS listener, WAF for OWASP ruleset. RDS Multi-AZ PostgreSQL in private subnet. ElastiCache Redis in private subnet. S3 VPC endpoint for pre-signed URL generation without internet egress.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR - ESLint, TypeScript strict check, Jest unit tests 80% coverage, Playwright E2E against dev environment. On merge to main - docker build, push ECR, ECS rolling deploy for api and webhook-worker, ECS update for milestone-notifier task definition.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM: traces for all Fastify handlers including Bull queue producer latency and RLS policy evaluation time. Custom metrics: interconnection_requests_submitted_total, webhook_delivery_success_rate, webhook_delivery_p99_latency, milestone_overdue_count. PagerDuty integration: milestone_overdue_count > 0 fires critical (FERC compliance risk), webhook delivery success rate < 90% over 15 minutes fires high.
devops.devops_risks: ECS milestone-notifier scheduled task must survive API deployment rolling updates; ensure it runs as a separate task definition with its own deployment lifecycle.

ISS-001.title: Queue position race condition on concurrent submissions
ISS-001.detail: Round 1: concurrent interconnection_request submissions could assign the same queue_position. Fixed in round 2 with SELECT FOR UPDATE on study_queues.last_position.

history.note: SELECT FOR UPDATE serializes position assignment within transaction.

ISS-002.title: RLS policy not enforced for transmission operator cross-org study report access
ISS-002.detail: Round 2: transmission operators could access study_reports for requests not assigned to their operator_org_id. Fixed in round 3 with study_reports RLS policy checking request.operator_org_id.

history.note: RLS policy on study_reports added operator_org_id join condition.

ISS-003.title: Webhook delivery has no dead-letter queue or admin retry mechanism
ISS-003.detail: Round 3: Bull queue failed jobs move to failed state with no dead-letter queue, no admin visibility, and no manual retry path. Utility planning systems receiving no notification of study status updates is a FERC Order 2023 compliance risk.

history.note: Auditor flagged missing DLQ and admin retry UI for webhook failures.

ISS-004.title: Study report upload routes 500 MB files through Fastify API tier
ISS-004.detail: Round 3: study report upload endpoint accepts file body in Fastify, buffering up to 500 MB in ECS task memory before S3 upload, causing memory exhaustion and task crashes under concurrent uploads.

history.note: Load test showed ECS task OOM at 3 concurrent 500 MB uploads.

focus_issues.title: Webhook delivery has no dead-letter queue or admin retry mechanism
focus_issues.detail: Failed webhook deliveries have no DLQ or admin retry path; utility systems miss FERC-relevant status notifications.

focus_issues.title: Study report upload routes 500 MB files through Fastify API tier
focus_issues.detail: Buffering 500 MB study reports in Fastify causes ECS task memory exhaustion under concurrent uploads.

latest_recommendations: Implement Bull dead-letter queue for failed webhook deliveries with admin retry UI in /admin/webhooks panel
Replace Fastify file body buffering with client-side S3 multipart upload: Fastify generates pre-signed part URLs, browser uploads directly to S3, Fastify confirms assembly
