previous_plan.title: Relay Protection Settings Desktop Tool - Round 1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Electron-Python desktop tool for SEL relay coordination on Windows workstations. IPC calls synchronous, audit log unprotected.
previous_plan.system_components: Electron main, Svelte renderer, Python SciPy subprocess (synchronous IPC), SQLite plain table audit log.
previous_plan.data_model: relay_devices, settings_files, protection_zones, coordination_curves, settings_packages, audit_log (no triggers).

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for relay protection settings desktop tool. Three issues from round 1: ISS-001 (sync IPC), ISS-002 (installer size), ISS-003 (audit immutability). Plan quality is 'flawed' - I'll address all three substantially but introduce the characteristic flaw: the 'scalability' primary theme is incongruent with a local single-workstation app. I'll include language about 'auto-scaling the Python worker pool' that doesn't apply here, plus the SQLCipher key rotation gap on AD password change will remain unaddressed.

fix_report.action_taken: Redesigned IPC as async job queue: renderer dispatches {jobId: uuid4, command, payload}, receives job:progress (20% increments) and job:result events via ipcRenderer.on without blocking Svelte UI thread.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Coordination study execution no longer blocks Svelte renderer; progress bar updates keep engineer informed during multi-second N-1 fault batches

fix_report.action_taken: Specified python-build-standalone packaging strategy with per-component size budget: Electron 28 ~82MB, python-build-standalone 3.12 ~45MB, SciPy + NumPy pre-built wheels ~52MB, SQLCipher module ~1MB, Svelte bundle ~3MB = ~183MB. CI size assertion gate added (fail if >200MB).
fix_report.expected_outcome: NSIS installer confirmed under 200MB with CI enforcement on every build

fix_report.action_taken: Added BEFORE UPDATE trigger raising SQLITE_ERROR 'AUDIT_TAMPER_DENIED' and BEFORE DELETE trigger raising 'AUDIT_DELETE_DENIED' on audit_events table. Monthly encrypted USB export procedure for CIP-007 evidence archive documented.
fix_report.expected_outcome: audit_events records cannot be modified or deleted; tamper-evident log suitable for CIP-007 audit evidence submission

target_output.title: Relay Protection Settings Desktop Tool - Revised Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: An Electron 28 / Svelte 4 / Python 3.12 SciPy desktop application for transmission protection engineers to manage SEL relay settings files, run N-1 time-overcurrent coordination studies, and produce approved settings export packages for substation field deployment. Runs fully offline on air-gapped Windows workstations with SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite and Windows AD role-based access. Round 2 resolves async IPC blocking (ISS-001), installer size analysis (ISS-002), and CIP-007 audit log immutability (ISS-003).
target_output.architecture_overview: Electron 28 main process manages Windows AD authentication via node-activedirectory2, SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite project database, and an async IPC job dispatcher backed by a Python subprocess pool of 2 workers. Svelte renderer communicates to main via contextBridge: study commands dispatched as async jobs {jobId: uuid4, command, payload}, renderer receives job:progress and job:result events without blocking the UI thread. Python workers process SEL .rdb parsing, IEEE C37.112 TCC calculations using SciPy optimize, N-1 coordination selectivity checking (CTI >= 0.3s), and SEL .acselerator export building over stdio JSON-RPC.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 28, Svelte 4, TypeScript, Konva.js for TCC log-log canvas. Backend: Python 3.12 (python-build-standalone), SciPy 1.13, NumPy 1.26, custom SEL .rdb parser. Database: SQLite with SQLCipher 4.5 via better-sqlite3-sqlcipher. Auth: node-activedirectory2 for Windows AD LDAP. Packaging: Electron Builder NSIS. Observability: Sentry offline queue mode.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Relay Device Library: SEL .rdb settings import, canonical JSON parsing, version history tracking. TCC Coordination Canvas: Konva.js log-log overlay of primary and backup relay curves with fault current marker, drag time-dial handles for live CTI feedback. N-1 Study Runner: async SciPy fault study with per-scenario CTI violation table. Settings Diff Tool: SHA-256 comparison of current parameters against deployed_baseline_hash. Export Package Builder: compile approved SEL .acselerator zip with Approver AD countersign and change manifest.
target_output.system_components: Electron Main Process: AD auth, SQLCipher DB lifecycle, async IPC job queue (FIFO, UUID keyed), Python worker pool (2 processes, auto-restart on crash - NOTE: 'auto-scaling worker pool' referenced in cost section is inapplicable; pool is fixed at 2 for workstation memory constraints). Svelte Renderer SPA: project browser, relay editor, TCC canvas, study runner, export builder, audit viewer. Python Worker Pool: JSON-RPC stdio, commands parse_sel_file/run_tcc_study/export_package/diff_settings. SQLCipher Database: encrypted project store with append-only audit_events. NSIS Installer: ~183MB bundling all runtime components.
target_output.workflows: Coordination Study Workflow: Engineer opens project, selects primary and backup relays, inputs zone fault current. Renderer dispatches {jobId: uuid4, command: 'run_tcc_study', relayIds: [...], faultCurrentA: 12500}. Main enqueues to available Python worker. Worker evaluates IEEE C37.112 standard inverse equation t=0.14*TDS/(M^0.02-1) at fault current for each relay, checks CTI >= 0.3s between primary and backup operating times, streams 20% progress increments, returns violation set. Renderer receives job:result, populates violation table, highlights offending curves red on Konva canvas. Export and Approval Workflow: CIPProtectionEditor initiates export, system computes diff against deployed_baseline_hash. CIPProtectionApprover reviews changed parameters, re-enters AD credentials to countersign. Approval recorded in audit_events with approver_ad_hash. SEL .acselerator zip generated with change manifest for field deployment.
target_output.data_model: relay_devices (id UUID PK, model TEXT, manufacturer TEXT, substation_id TEXT, firmware_version_id FK, imported_at TIMESTAMP). settings_files (id UUID PK, relay_id FK, format ENUM sel/ge/abb, raw_encrypted BLOB, parsed_json TEXT, version INT, imported_by TEXT, imported_at TIMESTAMP). protection_zones (id UUID PK, project_id FK, zone_name TEXT, fault_current_amps REAL, zone_type ENUM primary/backup/pilot). coordination_curves (id UUID PK, relay_id FK, curve_type ENUM toc/ioc/distance, pickup_amps REAL, time_dial REAL, curve_family TEXT). settings_packages (id UUID PK, project_id FK, status ENUM draft/approved/exported, approved_by TEXT, approver_ad_hash TEXT, exported_at TIMESTAMP). firmware_versions (id UUID PK, relay_model TEXT, version_string TEXT). audit_events (id UUID PK, user_samaccountname TEXT NOT NULL, action_type TEXT, relay_id TEXT, old_value_hash TEXT, new_value_hash TEXT, timestamp INTEGER) with BEFORE UPDATE trigger 'AUDIT_TAMPER_DENIED' and BEFORE DELETE trigger 'AUDIT_DELETE_DENIED'.
target_output.api_design: Internal Electron contextBridge IPC only (no network endpoints). ipcMain.handle('job:dispatch', {jobId, command, payload}) -> {jobId, status: 'queued'}. ipcRenderer.on('job:progress', {jobId, percent}) for canvas progress bar. ipcRenderer.on('job:result', {jobId, success, data}) for study results. Python JSON-RPC: {cmd:'parse_sel_file', path:'...'} -> {relays:[...]}; {cmd:'run_tcc_study', curves:[...], faultCurrentA:N} -> {violations:[...], ctis:[...]}; {cmd:'diff_settings', baselineHash:'...', current:{...}} -> {changes:[...]}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Windows AD LDAP bind with four role groups: CIPProtectionReadOnly, CIPProtectionEditor, CIPProtectionApprover, CIPProtectionAdmin. SQLCipher AES-256 CBC key derived via PBKDF2-SHA256 (100k iterations) from AD service account credential stored in Windows Credential Manager. Export packages encrypted AES-256 GCM. Audit log immutability enforced by BEFORE UPDATE and BEFORE DELETE triggers on audit_events. CIP-007 evidence: monthly CSV export to encrypted USB, 3-year local retention. No network egress: Sentry beforeSend returns null in production builds. UNRESOLVED: SQLCipher key rotation on AD service account password change not addressed - databases become unreadable if service account rotates until manual key re-encryption is performed.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: NSIS installer (~183MB) distributed via internal SharePoint. Component budget: Electron 28 ~82MB, python-build-standalone 3.12 ~45MB, SciPy + NumPy wheels ~52MB, SQLCipher module ~1MB, Svelte bundle ~3MB. GitHub Actions CI: ESLint + Svelte-check, mypy, Vitest, pytest (0.01s tolerance on TCC operating times vs. IEEE C37.112 reference), electron-builder NSIS, installer size assertion (fail >200MB), SharePoint upload. Rollback: previous installer retained 90 days; no auto-update. Migrations run at startup via applied_migrations table.
target_output.observability: Sentry offline queue at %APPDATA%/relay-settings-tool/sentry-queue.json; no cloud flush in production. Structured log at %APPDATA%/relay-settings-tool/app.log (7-day daily rotation). Logged: AD auth events, IPC job dispatch/completion/timeout, Python worker start/crash/respawn, SQLCipher open/close, export package creation.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud infrastructure cost. Fixed workstation deployment. Performance targets: SEL .rdb parse <2s for 500-parameter file; N-1 study with 20 scenarios <15s async non-blocking. Python worker pool fixed at 2 processes - NOTE: the 'auto-scaling worker pool' language referenced elsewhere in this plan is architecturally incorrect for a single-workstation application; pool size is static and does not scale. Installer capped at 200MB enforced in CI.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 months): Windows AD auth with four roles, SEL .rdb import/export, IEEE C37.112 TCC coordination study (standard/very inverse/extremely inverse), protection zone definition, async IPC job queue with progress callbacks, Konva.js TCC canvas, settings diff, CIP-007 append-only audit log with DB trigger enforcement, NSIS installer <200MB, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 (months 7-12): GE Multilin .txt and ABB REL650 format support, distance relay impedance coordination, PDF report with annotated TCC diagrams, multi-project batch study runner, SQLCipher key rotation on AD password change. Phase 3 (months 13-18): Optional internal LAN project sharing, SCADA asset register integration, automated NERC PRC compliance report generation.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode for Electron and Svelte. mypy strict for Python engine. All IPC message schemas defined as shared TypeScript interfaces. Vitest for Svelte components; pytest with 0.01s abs tolerance for TCC operating times. ESLint rule blocking fetch/XMLHttpRequest in renderer. SQLCipher native module version pinned; electron-rebuild automated in afterPack hook. ADR log in /docs/adr.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SQLCipher key rotation not implemented - AD service account password rotation makes databases unreadable. Mitigation deferred to phase 2. Risk: python-build-standalone SciPy wheels quarantined by Windows Defender on restricted workstations - mitigate by pre-whitelisting installer hash via GPO. Risk: Electron ABI version pin creates upgrade friction - document ABI in release notes. Tradeoff: 2-worker Python pool vs. single worker - two workers allow concurrent user sessions; accepted at ~600MB memory footprint on 8GB workstations. FLAW: Plan uses 'auto-scaling worker pool' language in system_components which is architecturally inapplicable for a single-workstation desktop application - worker count is static at 2.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Async IPC job queue adopted to eliminate UI blocking. BEFORE UPDATE/DELETE triggers chosen for audit immutability over application-layer enforcement. python-build-standalone adopted for SciPy version guarantee. SQLCipher key rotation deferred to phase 2.

metadata.notes: Round 2 relay protection settings desktop tool. All three round-1 issues resolved but plan retains inapplicable auto-scaling language and leaves SQLCipher key rotation gap unaddressed.

profile.domain: Power Systems Research — Optimal Power Flow Computation

project_goal.value: Implement GPU-accelerated optimal power flow (OPF) solvers using novel interior-point methods and benchmark them against commercial solvers (CPLEX, Gurobi) for real-time economic dispatch applications on large-scale transmission networks.
project_goal.rationale: Commercial OPF solvers take 2-5 minutes for 10,000-bus networks; GPU parallelism may achieve sub-10-second solve times enabling real-time dispatch application.

target_users.value: Power systems research engineers and computational scientists at the laboratory evaluating novel OPF algorithms for real-time economic dispatch.
target_users.rationale: Internal R&D research team; no external users in the prototype phase.

project_class.rationale: R&D prototype benchmarking GPU-accelerated OPF methods — not a production deployment.

capabilities.rationale: Solver implementation, benchmark dataset management, and performance analytics; no frontend UI required.

complexity_level.rationale: GPU-accelerated interior-point method implementation with CUDA kernels, sparse matrix operations, and benchmark harness across multiple network scales.

risk_level.rationale: Internal research prototype with no external exposure, no user data, and no production impact.

data_sensitivity.rationale: Network topology data and solver results are internal research data; no personal data or financial data.

external_exposure.rationale: Runs on Vertex AI Workbench compute instances within the research team GCP project; no external endpoints.

access_model.value: GCP IAM project-level access for research team members. Vertex AI Workbench instances provisioned per researcher. No application-level authentication.
access_model.rationale: GCP IAM provides sufficient access control for an internal research prototype.

feature_scope.value: GPU-accelerated interior-point OPF solver with CuPy sparse matrix operations; standard IEEE test case loader (IEEE 118, 300, 2383, 9241 bus); custom network topology loader from PSS/E RAW and MATPOWER .mat formats; configurable solver hyperparameters; automated benchmark harness comparing GPU solver vs CPLEX and Gurobi; structured result logging with LMP outputs, generation dispatch, iteration counts, solve times, and optimality gap.
feature_scope.rationale: Core functionality for the R&D benchmarking objective.

mvp_scope.value: GPU-accelerated primal-dual interior-point OPF on IEEE 118 and 300 bus test cases with comparison against CPLEX baseline, logging solve time and optimality gap.
mvp_scope.rationale: Smallest scope that validates the GPU acceleration hypothesis.

security_baseline.value: GCP IAM for compute access. No secrets in code — GCP Workload Identity for service account access. No public endpoints.
security_baseline.rationale: Minimal security baseline appropriate for an internal research prototype with no external exposure.

frontend_stack.value: None — results accessed via Jupyter notebooks and CLI scripts
frontend_stack.rationale: Research team uses Jupyter notebooks on Vertex AI Workbench for interactive result visualization.

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with CuPy and CUDA 12.3 for GPU-accelerated linear algebra
backend_stack.rationale: CuPy provides NumPy-compatible GPU array API enabling algorithm prototyping with minimal CUDA kernel authoring for standard linear algebra operations.

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL for benchmark result persistence; Google Cloud Storage for network model files and result exports.
data_platform.rationale: Structured benchmark results require relational storage for comparative queries; GCS for large network files.

hosting_target.value: Vertex AI Workbench with A100 GPU instances (n1-standard-8 + NVIDIA A100 40GB) on GCP
hosting_target.rationale: A100 CUDA compute capability 8.0 required for tensor core acceleration of sparse matrix factorization.

privacy_retention_policy.value: Benchmark results retained indefinitely in Cloud SQL for research reproducibility. Network model files archived in GCS Standard. No data purge schedule.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Research data retention is indefinite for reproducibility; no regulatory requirements.

future_scope.value: Multi-GPU distributed OPF solvers for 100,000-bus continental-scale networks, integration with real-time PMU streaming data for online dispatch applications, open-source publication of solver codebase.
future_scope.rationale: Long-term research roadmap toward real-time continental-scale OPF.

constraints.value: Solver must achieve sub-10-second wall clock for IEEE 9241-bus network on A100 GPU. Solver convergence tolerance must match or exceed commercial solver KKT residual threshold (1e-6). All benchmark results must be reproducible given the same random seed and hyperparameters.
constraints.rationale: Performance target derived from real-time dispatch application requirements; reproducibility required for publication.

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for GPU utilization, memory bandwidth, and solve throughput metrics. Structured JSON solve telemetry persisted to PostgreSQL via the benchmark harness.
observability_baseline.rationale: GPU utilization monitoring needed to identify memory bandwidth bottlenecks in sparse matrix operations.

execution_preference.value: Iterative research cycles: implement algorithm variant, run benchmark suite, analyze results, refine. GitHub for version control of solver code and benchmark configurations.
execution_preference.rationale: Research workflow with flexible iteration cadence, not sprint-based delivery.

project.goal: GPU-accelerated OPF solver benchmarking for real-time economic dispatch
project.target_users: Power systems research engineers

backend.framework: CuPy with CUDA 12.3, SciPy for CPU baseline
backend.solver_comparison: CPLEX 22.1 and Gurobi 11 via Python APIs

data.storage: GCS Standard for network model files and result exports

open_questions.parallelism: Whether to use CuPy sparse direct factorization or implement custom CUDA kernels for interior-point linear systems

reasoner_reviews.product: Research scope is well-defined around the GPU acceleration hypothesis. IEEE 118 to 9241 bus test case progression is the correct benchmark ladder. The CPLEX and Gurobi comparisons are essential for publication credibility. The open question about CuPy sparse direct vs custom CUDA kernels is the most important architectural decision to resolve early — it determines implementation complexity and achievable solve time.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The interior-point OPF solve bottleneck is the linear system solve at each Newton step, involving sparse indefinite matrix factorization. CuPy cuSPARSE and cuSOLVER bindings can handle this for networks up to 5,000 buses, but the 9,241-bus target may require cuDSS (CUDA Direct Sparse Solver) for the modified KKT system. The benchmark harness design is critical — solver comparison must control for network preprocessing time and exclude it from reported solve times.
reasoner_reviews.security: Internal research prototype with no external exposure — GCP IAM project-level access is sufficient. CPLEX and Gurobi license tokens must be loaded from GCP Secret Manager at runtime, never committed to the repository.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The sub-10-second constraint on the 9,241-bus network is aggressive. CuPy cuSOLVER performance needs profiling against cuDSS for the sparse KKT system scale. Budget approximately ,000/month for A100 Vertex AI instances running benchmark suites 8 hours per day.

backend.service_design: Python package opf_gpu with modules: network_loader (PSS/E RAW and MATPOWER .mat parsers producing standard admittance matrix and generator cost curve structures), interior_point_solver (primal-dual interior-point method with CuPy GPU linear algebra), benchmark_harness (controlled comparison runner executing GPU solver, CPLEX, and Gurobi on identical problem instances), result_logger (structured telemetry writer to PostgreSQL solver_runs table).
backend.business_modules: InteriorPointSolver: assembles sparse KKT system at each Newton iteration, solves via CuPy cuSOLVER or cuDSS, updates primal-dual variables, checks KKT residual convergence. BenchmarkHarness: loads network topology, warms GPU cache with a no-solve dry run, records wall clock time excluding preprocessing, repeats N trials and reports median and stddev. ComparativeSolver: thin wrappers around cplex.Model and gurobipy.Model accepting the same standardized OPF problem instance.
backend.scaling_notes: For networks above 5,000 buses, CuPy cuSOLVER may be memory-bandwidth limited — cuDSS with reordering (AMD/METIS nested dissection) reduces fill-in and improves factorization throughput on A100 HBM2e memory.
backend.backend_risks: CuPy cuSOLVER sparse factorization may not achieve sub-10-second target on 9,241-bus network. CPLEX and Gurobi Python API licenses on Vertex AI Workbench require network-accessible license servers within the GCP project.

security.auth_design: GCP IAM project-level access for all research team members. No application authentication.
security.secrets_management: CPLEX and Gurobi license server addresses and tokens loaded from GCP Secret Manager via google-cloud-secret-manager Python client at runtime. Never committed to repository.

data.entities: network_topologies (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, bus_count INT, branch_count INT, generator_count INT, source_format ENUM ieee_cdf/psse_raw/matpower, gcs_file_uri, loaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ), generator_cost_curves (id UUID PK, topology_id FK, generator_id INT, cost_model ENUM quadratic/piecewise, a_coeff DECIMAL, b_coeff DECIMAL, c_coeff DECIMAL, p_min_mw DECIMAL, p_max_mw DECIMAL), bus_constraints (id UUID PK, topology_id FK, bus_id INT, v_min_pu DECIMAL, v_max_pu DECIMAL, p_load_mw DECIMAL, q_load_mvar DECIMAL), solver_runs (id UUID PK, topology_id FK, solver_name ENUM gpu_ipm/cplex/gurobi, hyperparams_json JSONB, solve_time_ms DECIMAL, iterations INT, objective_value DECIMAL, optimality_gap_pct DECIMAL, kkt_residual DECIMAL, converged BOOLEAN, random_seed INT, run_at TIMESTAMPTZ), benchmark_results (id UUID PK, topology_id FK, benchmark_suite VARCHAR, summary_json JSONB, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL for structured benchmark results. GCS Standard bucket for raw network model files (IEEE CDF, PSS/E RAW, MATPOWER .mat) and exported result CSVs for publication.
data.analytics_events: solver_runs table serves as the primary analytics event stream; queried via Jupyter notebooks for convergence analysis, solve time distribution, and LMP accuracy comparison between GPU and commercial solver outputs.
data.data_risks: PSS/E RAW parser edge cases for non-standard network encoding may cause silent data corruption in admittance matrix construction — mitigated by cross-validating admittance matrix eigenvalue spectrum against reference implementation.

devops.deployment_topology: Vertex AI Workbench managed notebooks provisioned with A100 GPU, CUDA 12.3 driver, and Python 3.12 environment. Prometheus node exporter and NVIDIA DCGM exporter installed as startup scripts for GPU utilization monitoring.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions for code quality only (pytest unit tests on CPU using small IEEE 14-bus fixture, mypy type checking, ruff linting). No automated GPU CI — GPU runs triggered manually by researchers on Workbench instances.
devops.observability_stack: NVIDIA DCGM exporter for GPU utilization, memory bandwidth, and SM occupancy metrics scraped by Prometheus. Grafana dashboard: solve_time_ms by solver and network size, GPU memory utilization during sparse factorization, convergence iteration count distribution.
devops.cost_controls: Vertex AI Workbench A100 instances at .67/hour. Instances stopped when not in active benchmark runs via a scheduled Cloud Function. Estimated ,200-,200/month for intensive benchmarking phases.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a GPU-accelerated OPF solver research prototype on GCP Vertex AI with A100 hardware. Key decisions: CuPy cuSOLVER for the sparse KKT linear system at each interior-point Newton iteration, with cuDSS as fallback for the 9,241-bus scale where cuSOLVER may be memory-bandwidth limited. Benchmark harness must exclude preprocessing time from reported solve durations for fair comparison with CPLEX and Gurobi. PostgreSQL on Cloud SQL for structured benchmark result persistence enabling cross-solver comparative queries.
target_output.title: GPU-Accelerated Optimal Power Flow Solver Research Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: Python research prototype implementing a primal-dual interior-point OPF solver accelerated via CuPy GPU sparse matrix operations on NVIDIA A100 hardware, targeting sub-10-second solution of the 9,241-bus PEGASE network for real-time economic dispatch benchmarking. The prototype includes IEEE and PSS/E network loaders, a controlled benchmark harness comparing GPU solver performance against CPLEX and Gurobi baselines, and structured result persistence to Cloud SQL for convergence and LMP accuracy analysis.
target_output.architecture_overview: Python package opf_gpu executed on Vertex AI Workbench A100 GPU instances within a GCP research project. The interior-point solver assembles a sparse KKT system at each Newton iteration using CuPy cupyx.scipy.sparse module and solves it via CuPy cuSOLVER sparse direct factorization (cuDSS for networks above 5,000 buses where fill-in reduction via nested dissection reordering improves A100 HBM2e memory bandwidth utilization). Network topologies are loaded from IEEE CDF, PSS/E RAW, and MATPOWER .mat formats into a canonical bus-branch model with admittance matrix and generator cost curves. The benchmark harness warms the GPU with a pre-solve dry run, measures median wall clock time across N trials excluding preprocessing, and logs structured telemetry to PostgreSQL on Cloud SQL.
target_output.technology_stack: Solver: Python 3.12, CuPy 13.x with CUDA 12.3 (cuSPARSE, cuSOLVER, cuDSS for sparse KKT system), SciPy sparse for CPU reference baseline. Commercial baselines: CPLEX 22.1 via docplex Python API, Gurobi 11 via gurobipy. Network loaders: pandapower for IEEE CDF and MATPOWER .mat ingestion; custom PSS/E RAW parser. Data: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL, google-cloud-storage Python client, google-cloud-secret-manager for CPLEX/Gurobi license tokens. Monitoring: NVIDIA DCGM exporter, Prometheus, Grafana Cloud. CI: GitHub Actions with pytest and ruff. Runtime: Vertex AI Workbench n1-standard-8 + A100 40GB.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Interior-Point Solver module: primal-dual path-following interior-point method with Mehrotra predictor-corrector enhancement; CuPy sparse KKT system assembly; cuSOLVER/cuDSS linear system solve per Newton iteration; convergence check on KKT residual (primal infeasibility, dual infeasibility, complementarity) against configurable tolerance (default 1e-6). Network Loader module: PSS/E RAW parser for bus, branch, generator, and load records into canonical NetworkModel dataclass; MATPOWER .mat loader via pandapower; admittance matrix assembly as CuPy sparse CSR matrix. Benchmark Harness module: controlled N-trial runner with GPU cache warmup, wall-clock timing excluding I/O and preprocessing, result logging to solver_runs table. Commercial Baseline module: CPLEX and Gurobi wrappers accepting identical NetworkModel input for direct solve time and objective comparison.
target_output.system_components: opf_gpu Python package: NetworkLoader, AdmittanceMatrixBuilder, KKTSystemAssembler, InteriorPointSolver, BenchmarkHarness, CPLEXBaseline, GurobiBaseline, ResultLogger. Vertex AI Workbench A100 instance providing CUDA 12.3 compute environment. CuPy cuDSS sparse direct solver for KKT linear system for 5,000-plus bus networks. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 storing structured benchmark results. GCS bucket storing raw network model files. Prometheus node exporter and NVIDIA DCGM exporter for GPU performance telemetry. Grafana Cloud dashboard for solve throughput and GPU utilization visualization. Jupyter notebooks on Workbench for interactive analysis and publication figure generation.
target_output.workflows: Benchmark Execution Workflow: Researcher opens Jupyter notebook on Vertex AI Workbench, configures benchmark parameters (network topology, solver list, N trials, random seed, convergence tolerance), calls BenchmarkHarness.run(); harness loads network from GCS, builds admittance matrix, warms GPU with a no-solve dummy forward pass, executes GPU interior-point solver for N trials recording solve_time_ms and KKT residual per trial, executes CPLEX and Gurobi on identical problem instances, logs all results to solver_runs table. Result Analysis Workflow: Researcher queries solver_runs table from Jupyter via psycopg2, computes solve time distributions and optimality gap comparisons across solvers and network sizes, generates comparative performance figures for paper draft, exports result summary to GCS as CSV.
target_output.data_model: network_topologies (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, bus_count INT, branch_count INT, generator_count INT, source_format ENUM ieee_cdf/psse_raw/matpower, gcs_file_uri VARCHAR, loaded_at TIMESTAMPTZ). generator_cost_curves (id UUID PK, topology_id UUID FK, generator_bus_id INT, cost_model ENUM quadratic/piecewise_linear, a_coeff DECIMAL, b_coeff DECIMAL, c_coeff DECIMAL, p_min_mw DECIMAL, p_max_mw DECIMAL). bus_constraints (id UUID PK, topology_id UUID FK, bus_id INT, v_min_pu DECIMAL DEFAULT 0.95, v_max_pu DECIMAL DEFAULT 1.05, p_load_mw DECIMAL, q_load_mvar DECIMAL). solver_runs (id UUID PK, topology_id UUID FK, solver_name ENUM gpu_ipm/cplex/gurobi, hyperparams_json JSONB, solve_time_ms DECIMAL, preprocessing_time_ms DECIMAL, iterations INT, objective_value_usd DECIMAL, optimality_gap_pct DECIMAL, kkt_primal_residual DECIMAL, kkt_dual_residual DECIMAL, converged BOOLEAN, random_seed INT, cuda_device VARCHAR NULLABLE, run_at TIMESTAMPTZ). convergence_metrics (id UUID PK, solver_run_id UUID FK, iteration INT, kkt_residual DECIMAL, step_size DECIMAL, barrier_parameter DECIMAL). benchmark_results (id UUID PK, topology_id UUID FK, benchmark_suite VARCHAR, solver_summary_json JSONB, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: Python module API (no HTTP endpoints). NetworkLoader.load_psse_raw(gcs_uri: str) -> NetworkModel. NetworkLoader.load_matpower(gcs_uri: str) -> NetworkModel. InteriorPointSolver.solve(network: NetworkModel, config: SolverConfig) -> SolverResult where SolverResult contains dispatch_mw: dict, lmp_per_bus: dict, solve_time_ms: float, iterations: int, kkt_residual: float, converged: bool. BenchmarkHarness.run(network: NetworkModel, solvers: list[str], n_trials: int, seed: int) -> BenchmarkReport. ResultLogger.persist(run: SolverResult, topology_id: UUID, session: Session) -> None.
target_output.security_and_compliance: GCP IAM controls access to Vertex AI Workbench instances — only research team GCP project members with aiplatform.notebookRuntimes.create and cloudsql.instances.connect permissions. CPLEX and Gurobi license server addresses and academic license tokens stored in GCP Secret Manager, loaded at runtime via google-cloud-secret-manager; never committed to repository. Cloud SQL connections via Cloud SQL Auth Proxy using Workload Identity — no password credentials in notebooks or code. GCS bucket private with IAM-enforced access. No public network endpoints exposed.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Vertex AI Workbench instances provisioned via Terraform with A100 GPU, CUDA 12.3 driver, Python 3.12 environment, and startup script installing opf_gpu package from GitHub, NVIDIA DCGM exporter, and Prometheus node exporter. GitHub Actions CI runs pytest tests/unit against IEEE 14-bus CPU fixture and ruff check src/opf_gpu on every PR — no GPU required for CI. Cloud Function triggered on schedule to stop idle Workbench instances (idle = no Jupyter kernel activity for 2 hours) to control A100 compute costs.
target_output.observability: NVIDIA DCGM exporter scrapes GPU SM utilization, memory bandwidth utilization, and PCIe transfer rate from A100 during solve runs. Prometheus scrapes DCGM and node exporter metrics every 15 seconds. Grafana Cloud dashboard panels: solve_time_ms histogram by solver and network bus count, GPU memory utilization during sparse factorization phase, KKT residual convergence curves per solver run, A100 SM occupancy during KKT assembly vs linear solve phases. PostgreSQL solver_runs table directly queried in Jupyter notebooks for research analysis. Alert: Grafana alert if A100 GPU memory utilization exceeds 95%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vertex AI Workbench n1-standard-8 + A100 40GB at .67/hour. Estimated 600-800 active compute hours/month during intensive benchmarking phases = ,200-,900/month. Cloud SQL db-n1-standard-2 at /month. GCS Standard storage negligible at typical network file volumes. Idle stop Cloud Function reduces costs during non-working hours by approximately 40%. Post-MVP multi-GPU scaling: A3 instance (8x A100 80GB) at .77/hour for continental-scale network experiments.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — Core Solver MVP (Weeks 1-4): Implement NetworkLoader for IEEE CDF and MATPOWER .mat formats, AdmittanceMatrixBuilder to CuPy CSR sparse matrix, primal-dual interior-point solver with CuPy cuSOLVER for IEEE 118 and 300 bus networks, basic result logging to Cloud SQL. Validate convergence on IEEE 118 test case against published OPF solutions. Phase 2 — Benchmarking and Commercial Baselines (Weeks 5-8): Implement BenchmarkHarness with GPU warmup, controlled timing, and N-trial statistics. Add CPLEX and Gurobi baseline wrappers. Benchmark on IEEE 2383 and 9241 bus networks. Evaluate cuDSS vs cuSOLVER for 5,000-plus bus KKT factorization. Phase 3 — Analysis and Profiling (Weeks 9-12): PSS/E RAW loader. Convergence telemetry per Newton iteration. NVIDIA Nsight Systems profiling of KKT assembly and solve phases. Grafana dashboard for GPU performance monitoring. Publication result figure generation in Jupyter.
target_output.development_guidelines: All solver correctness tests must verify KKT residual convergence on IEEE 14, 30, and 118 bus test cases against published OPF benchmark solutions from Matpower. Random seed must be set on CuPy random state before each solver run to ensure reproducibility. BenchmarkHarness must record preprocessing_time_ms separately from solve_time_ms. CPLEX and Gurobi license tokens must be loaded exclusively from Secret Manager. Jupyter notebooks in results/ branch must be cleared of kernel output before commit.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CuPy cuSOLVER sparse direct factorization does not achieve sub-10-second solve on 9,241-bus PEGASE network due to memory bandwidth saturation on A100 HBM2e. Mitigation: evaluate NVIDIA cuDSS with AMD or METIS nested dissection reordering to reduce KKT system fill-in. Risk: Primal-dual interior-point divergence on ill-conditioned bus-branch networks with generation-demand imbalance. Mitigation: implement predictor-corrector Mehrotra enhancement and add network feasibility pre-check validating total generation capacity exceeds total load. Tradeoff: CuPy Python API over custom CUDA C++ kernels — 10-20x faster development at the cost of approximately 30% performance loss on custom sparse operations; acceptable for prototype phase.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: CuPy cuSOLVER selected as primary KKT linear system solver for Phase 1 and 2; cuDSS evaluated in Phase 2 for 5,000-plus bus networks. Benchmark timing protocol: wall clock excluding preprocessing, GPU cache warmup via dummy forward pass before first timed trial, median of N trials reported. CPLEX and Gurobi license tokens via Secret Manager confirmed. Cloud SQL as structured result store confirmed over flat JSON files — enables cross-solver comparative SQL queries.

metadata.notes: GPU-accelerated OPF solver prototype for power systems R&D — round 1 strong plan with interior-point method, CuPy/cuDSS, and controlled benchmark harness design.

project.goal: Python SDK for ICCP TASE.2 protocol parsing and object model

backend.framework: Pure Python core with optional asyncio bindings

security.input_validation: Defensive parsing with structured exceptions on malformed TASE.2 frames

data.serialization: JSON export for data_objects and bilateral_tables

constraints.dependencies: zero mandatory runtime deps in core

reasoner_reviews.product: The SDK scope is well-bounded: TASE.2 receive path first, then send path. Bilateral table management is the critical integration surface that determines SDK adoption by utility teams.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Zero-dependency core is correct for enterprise Python environments. The asyncio layer must be fully decoupled from the synchronous parser to avoid forcing event loop requirements on batch consumers. Round 1 mixed sync and async methods without clear separation.
reasoner_reviews.security: Defensive parsing against malformed TASE.2 frames is the primary security concern for this library running in OT integration environments. All struct.unpack calls must be wrapped to prevent memory errors on truncated messages.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Python 3.10 compatibility restricts use of match statements. Zero-dependency constraint means no pydantic or construct in the core parser module.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Previous plan lacked clear module structure separating binary codec from typed object model. API surface example was incomplete - developers need both sync parser and async streaming usage patterns.

backend.service_design: Module hierarchy: iccp.codec (binary framing, ASN.1 BER helpers, no asyncio imports), iccp.model (typed dataclasses with __slots__: DataStateQualified, DataRealQ, BilateralTable, TransferSet), iccp.parser (synchronous public API composing codec and model), iccp.async_client (asyncio transport importing asyncio only inside function bodies), iccp.server_fixture (loopback test server for CI)
backend.api_patterns: Public exports from iccp.__init__: IccpParser, AsyncIccpClient, BilateralTable, DataObject, TransferSet, IccpFrameError, IccpConformanceError. IccpParser.parse_frame(bytes)->Tase2Message; build_frame(Tase2Message)->bytes; AsyncIccpClient.subscribe_transfer_set(ts_id) async generator yielding DataObject.
backend.failure_handling: Malformed frame raises IccpFrameError with byte offset and expected frame type; truncated frame detected before struct.unpack; AsyncIccpClient reconnect uses exponential backoff with configurable max timeout.

data.entities: DataObject (tag_id: str, object_class: Literal['state','analog','discrete','time_series'], value: float|int|bool, quality_flags: int, timestamp: datetime|None). BilateralTable (local_vcc_id, remote_vcc_id, agreed_data_sets: list[DataSetRef]). TransferSet (transfer_set_id, data_set_ref, transfer_interval_ms, integrity_check). Tase2Message (message_type, sequence_number, payload_bytes). All use __slots__ dataclasses with to_dict().
data.schema_notes: All typed objects use Python dataclasses with __slots__ for memory efficiency in high-volume streaming contexts; no ORM involved
data.migration_notes: Version 2 will introduce Protocol Buffer serialization option for consumers needing reduced JSON overhead in high-throughput pipelines

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy --strict, pytest with 90% coverage gate, conformance tests against binary reference TASE.2 fixture files, PyPI trusted publisher via OIDC on tag push. Matrix: Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12.
devops.environments: dev (local pytest), ci (GitHub Actions matrix), release (PyPI trusted publisher via GitHub OIDC)
devops.devops_risks: PyPI trusted publisher misconfiguration could publish untested versions; mitigation: CI matrix pass required before publish workflow triggers; tag signing enforced.
